The Choir existed now only as a frequency of eternal suffering woven into the background hum of reality, a reminder that Godric's voice had touched them and that touch was permanent.

I did not speak for three months. When I finally spoke again, my first word was not my daughter's name or my wife's name or any word of love or grief or relief. It was the word Godric had spoken. I didn't mean to say it. It came out of me unbidden, a reflex of a nervous system that had been rewritten by acoustic force. The word, spoken with my pathetic thousandfold-amplified voice, shattered every window in the eastern wing of the palace and cracked the foundation stones of three adjacent buildings.

Godric found me afterward, sitting in the wreckage, weeping. He sat beside me and said nothing. He simply sat, his presence warm and patient and kind, while I wept for the fragility of everything that existed in the shadow of a voice that could unmake reality without trying.

He was right about one thing. Sound is fragile. Everything is fragile. Everything except him.

---

Kaedan set the journal down with the careful, deliberate motion of a man handling something that might detonate. His ears were ringing. He knew, with a certainty that bordered on pathological, that the ringing was imaginary, that the journal was just ink on parchment, that the sound Theron described had been restored along with the rest of the omniverse. But he could hear it anyway, a faint, pervasive hum at the edge of perception, the ghost of a word that had been spoken three-quarters of a million years ago and was still resonating now.

He thought of Godric's voice. The rich baritone that had congratulated him on his appointment. The gentle tone that had told him his father would be missed. Every word Godric had ever spoken to him, every syllable of warmth and kindness, had been produced by the same mechanism that had annihilated decillions of multiverses. Every kind word was a controlled explosion. Every gentle sentence was a contained apocalypse.

He reached for the second journal. His fingers left sweat marks on the leather.

---

**Year 617,883 — Marcus — Superpower: Touch of Absolute Annihilation**

I am documenting this incident with as much clinical precision as my current neurological state permits, which is to say, not much. My hands are steady because I am forcing them to be steady, employing the same muscular control that allows me to suppress my thousandfold daily amplification to functional levels. But the steadiness is a lie. Inside, every cell in my body is screaming, and it has been screaming for the fourteen hours since I witnessed what I am about to describe. I do not expect the screaming to stop. I expect it to become the permanent background radiation of my consciousness, a frequency of horror that I will carry until I die, which, at the current rate of my aging, will occur in approximately twenty-three years, and I find myself grateful for that mortality in a way I never have before.

My name is Marcus. Court wizard. Thirty-four years of service. I have watched Lord Godric demonstrate aspects of his power on six prior occasions, and each experience has progressively eroded my capacity to maintain emotional equilibrium. What I witnessed today has not merely eroded that capacity. It has vaporized it, along with most of the omniverse, and the manner of that vaporization has forced me to reconsider everything I believed about the relationship between physical contact and destruction.

The opposition was the Impermeable Concordat. They were a coalition of the most defensively capable entities in existence, beings whose entire evolutionary and technological history had been oriented toward a single objective: the achievement of absolute invulnerability. They numbered in the decillions. Their outermost defensive layer was composed of conceptual shield-nodes, each one capable of withstanding the collapse of an entire universe without structural degradation. Beneath that layer were temporal stasis fields that froze any incoming force in a single moment, preventing it from completing its destructive trajectory. Beneath that were probability shields that made any harmful event statistically impossible within their radius. Beneath that were spatial warps that redirected any attack into dimensions of pure entropy where destructive force was absorbed and neutralized. Beneath that were ontological anchors that bound their existence to the fundamental axioms of reality, making their destruction equivalent to the destruction of existence itself.

They believed they could not be touched. They believed that any force, any energy, any conceptual assault that attempted to make contact with their being would be neutralized before it could achieve its purpose. They believed this with the absolute confidence of beings who had invested the entire history of their civilizations in the perfection of defense.

They were wrong. They were so catastrophically, fundamentally wrong that the word wrong collapses under the weight of their error.

Godric was walking through the market district. It was a rare public excursion, something he did occasionally because he genuinely enjoyed being among his subjects, listening to their conversations, sampling their food, experiencing the texture of the empire he had built. I was accompanying him, along with a modest security detachment, though we all knew the security was theater. Godric did not need protection. He never had.

The Impermeable Concordat chose this moment to strike. They materialized their defensive formation around the market district, enclosing Godric within a sphere of layered invulnerability that spanned from the quantum scale to the cosmological scale. They activated every shield simultaneously. The conceptual nodes, the temporal stasis, the probability fields, the spatial warps, the ontological anchors. All of them. All at once. The combined defensive output of decillions of the most invulnerable beings in existence, focused on a single target.

Godric stopped walking. He had been examining a fruit vendor's display, picking up a particularly ripe specimen of something that resembled a cross between a peach and a star, and he paused with the fruit still in his hand. He looked up at the encircling defensive formation with an expression I can only describe as mild curiosity.

Then he reached out and touched the innermost layer of their shield.

The base effect of the Touch of Absolute Annihilation is straightforward: any surface that makes contact with Godric's skin is erased from existence. Not destroyed. Not damaged. Erased. The matter, energy, and conceptual framework of the touched object simply cease to be. But the Evolved Weakness Paradox has modified this power with enhanced effects that transform it from merely devastating to cosmically obscene.

The first enhanced effect: Propagating Contact Erasure. The annihilation does not stop at the point of contact. It propagates through all connected systems, all linked structures, all associated conceptual frameworks. When Godric touched the innermost shield, the erasure propagated through the shield's connections to the next layer, and the next, and the next, moving through the entire defensive network at speeds that made instantaneous look sluggish.

The second enhanced effect: Retroactive Contact. The touch does not merely affect the present state of the target. It reaches backward through time and erases the target's past as well. The shield nodes had never existed. The temporal stasis fields had never been activated. The probability shields had never been conceived. The Concordat's entire defensive history was being unwritten as the erasure propagated, and the beings within were discovering that their protection had always been an illusion.

The third enhanced effect: Permanent Erasure Field Generation. The touch does not merely erase what exists. It creates a permanent field of annihilation centered on the point of contact, a zone where existence itself becomes impossible. This field does not fade. It persists forever, continuously expanding at a rate that makes the speed of light look like a crawl, converting everything it touches into nothing.

The fourth enhanced effect, the one that has made me grateful for my own mortality: Erasure Conversion. The energy and conceptual mass of everything the touch annihilates is converted into power that is permanently added to Godric's being. In any other entity, absorbing the combined defensive output of the Impermeable Concordat would have elevated them to godhood. In Godric, whose base power was already so astronomical that the entire omniverse was a rounding error by comparison, the increase was infinitesimal. Practically zero. But it happened. He became, by that imperceptible fraction, permanently more powerful than he had been an instant before.

The destruction of the Impermeable Concordat was not a battle. It was an erasure event. The Propagating Contact Erasure moved through their defensive layers like fire through dry kindling, but faster, infinitely faster, converting each shield, each field, each anchor into nothing. The beings within the formation had approximately zero time to react before the erasure reached them.

I watched decillions of the most invulnerable entities in existence come apart. Not from force. From unbeing. Their conceptual shield-nodes vanished first, taking with them the mathematical framework that defined their invulnerability. Their temporal stasis fields collapsed into instants that never happened. Their probability shields became statistical impossibilities that had never been calculated. Their spatial warps straightened into dimensions that had never been bent. Their ontological anchors disconnected from reality's axioms as though those axioms had never been written.

And then the beings themselves began to go.

Their physical forms, stripped of every defense, experienced the direct contact of Godric's erasure field. Their skin, which had withstood the death of universes, simply ceased to exist, revealing the wet, glistening musculature beneath. That musculature ceased to exist, revealing the pulsing organs and the branching nervous systems and the circulating fluids. Those ceased to exist, revealing the skeletal structures that had supported them. Those ceased to exist, revealing nothing at all.

But the erasure did not grant them the mercy of clean obliteration. The Propagating Contact Erasure moved through their connected systems at variable speeds, creating a differential in which parts of their bodies were erased while other parts remained, suspended in a state of partial existence that was somehow worse than total annihilation. I watched beings who had defined themselves by their invulnerability experience the sensation of having their flesh removed in layers, their organs exposed and then erased, their nervous systems left dangling in open air for fractions of instants that lasted eternities, still firing, still registering the impossible agony of existing in a state between being and unbeing.

The Retroactive Contact ensured that they had never been invulnerable. They discovered, in their final moments, that their entire history of impregnability had been retroactively rewritten as a history of vulnerability, that every shield they had ever trusted had been an illusion, that they had always been exposed, always been fragile, always been one touch away from the absolute nothing that was now consuming them.

The Permanent Erasure Field expanded outward from the market district, converting everything it touched into nothing. The collateral damage was immediate and catastrophic. The market district vanished. The city around it vanished. The planet vanished. The solar system vanished. The galaxy vanished. The galaxy cluster vanished. The propagation accelerated as the Erasure Conversion fed more power into the field, making it expand faster, erasing more, converting more, expanding faster still, a recursive feedback loop of annihilation that consumed decillions of multiverses in the span of a thought.

The omniverse itself began to thin. Reality became translucent, then transparent, then absent. The Erasure Field was converting existence into a power increase so infinitesimal relative to Godric's baseline that it could not be measured, and yet the conversion was total, absolute, consuming everything it touched without reservation.

Godric stood in the center of the expanding void, the fruit still in his hand, his expression one of mild contemplation. He looked at the fruit, which had been preserved by the passive auras he extended over anything he wished to keep. He looked at the void, which now encompassed the vast majority of existence. He looked at me, preserved alongside him, and his brow furrowed with that same genuine concern I had seen on every other occasion when his power had exceeded his expectations of restraint.

"I only touched the shield," he said, his voice carrying through the void with that warm, resonant baritone that had itself been weaponized on other occasions. "I barely pressed at all."

In zero seconds, he restored the omniverse. Everything the Erasure Field had consumed returned, perfectly reconstructed, their inhabitants unaware that they had been converted into an imperceptible fraction of Godric's power. The market district was intact. The city was intact. The fruit vendor was still displaying his wares.

The Impermeable Concordat did not return. They had been erased, retroactively and permanently, and the Erasure Conversion had absorbed their total existential mass into Godric's being. There was nothing left to restore. They had become a rounding error in his infinity, an infinitesimal fraction of a fraction of a fraction of his already incalculable power, and that fraction was so small that it was functionally equivalent to zero.

But it was not zero. It was positive. And it was permanent.

Godric purchased the fruit. He paid the vendor with exact change, exchanged a few warm words about the quality of the produce, and turned to me with a smile. "Marcus," he said, placing the fruit in my hands, "could you hold this for me? I want to visit the spice merchant before we return."

I held the fruit. I held it for the rest of the day, and the rest of the night, and the rest of the following week. I held it because it was the only thing that made sense, the only object in existence that I could touch and know that my touch would not erase it, that it was real and solid and present and not a rounding error in the infinite arithmetic of a god's casual shopping trip.

I am still holding it as I write this. The fruit has begun to rot. I do not care. It is the most real thing in my universe, and I will hold it until it dissolves and my hands are empty and I must face the fact that everything else I have ever touched or will ever touch exists only because Godric has decided not to touch it back.

---

Kaedan's hands had gone completely numb. The journal slipped from his fingers and landed on the floor with a soft thud that seemed too small, too mundane, too physical for the weight of what it contained. He stared at it for a long moment, then picked it up with the careful reverence of a man handling a live grenade.

One touch. Godric had touched a shield, barely pressed at all, and the resulting cascade had consumed the omniverse. And the power increase from consuming the Impermeable Concordat, beings whose defensive capability exceeded the total output of decillions of multiverses, had been so small relative to Godric's baseline that it was effectively zero. The gap between Godric and everything else was not a gap. It was an absence of comparison, a void where mathematics should have been.

He reached for the third journal. The binding was black, unadorned, and cold to the touch. The pages were filled with handwriting so precise and controlled it looked engraved, each letter formed with the desperate meticulousness of a man trying to impose order on chaos.

---

**Year 889,445 — Callum — Superpower: Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication**

I have delayed writing this entry for seven months. Not because I lacked the time or the will, but because the act of writing requires language, and language requires concepts, and the concept I must now commit to parchment has spent the last seven months actively destroying my ability to conceptualize anything at all. I am writing this now because I have accepted that the destruction will continue regardless of whether I document it, and I owe it to my successors to provide whatever warning I can, however inadequate.

My name is Callum. I have been court wizard for fifty-one years. I am seventy-three years old. My body is failing in the slow, ordinary way of mortal flesh, despite the thousandfold daily amplification that makes my failing body stronger than entire galaxies. I will die in approximately seven years, and I find myself looking forward to death with an anticipation that borders on hunger, because death will finally separate me from the knowledge of what I witnessed on the day Godric tested his self-replication superpower.

The opposition was the Numerical Infinite, a collective entity that existed as a self-replicating swarm of combative singularities. Each individual unit was a self-contained apex combatant, possessing strength sufficient to shatter universes and durability capable of withstanding the collapse of multiversal clusters. Their defining characteristic was their reproduction rate: every destroyed unit was replaced by ten thousand identical copies in the span of a Planck second, and those copies could themselves reproduce at the same rate. They had never been defeated because any victory against them was immediately rendered meaningless by numerical replacement. They were infinity given form and aggression, and they believed that their capacity for infinite self-replication made them unstoppable.

They believed wrong with a wrongness that the English language cannot convey.

Godric was in the throne room when the Numerical Infinite breached reality. I was at his side, as always. The swarm manifested as a tide of brilliant, burning points of light, each one a singularity wrapped in destructive purpose, each one capable of annihilating a universe. They filled the throne room, then the palace, then the planet, then the solar system, then the galaxy, reproducing as they expanded, their numbers growing from billions to trillions to decillions to numbers that had no names because no civilization had ever needed to count that high.

Godric watched them come. He did not stand. He did not raise a hand. He did not activate any defense. He simply sat on his throne, his chin resting on his knuckles, his expression thoughtful, and he allowed an infinitesimal fraction of his Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication superpower to express itself at effectively zero percent of its true potential.

One Godric became two.

The base effect of the superpower is self-explanatory: Godric creates copies of himself. But the Evolved Weakness Paradox has transformed this power into something so far beyond mere duplication that calling it replication is like calling the omniverse a neighborhood. The enhanced effects have made this superpower the single most terrifying ability in Godric's already terrifying arsenal, and I will now attempt to describe them, though I know I will fail.

Enhanced effect one: Fractal Recursive Duplication. Each copy is not merely a duplicate of Godric. Each copy is a Prime, possessing the exact same stats, the exact same superpowers, the exact same infinite amplification rate as the original. And each copy immediately begins its own self-replication process, generating its own copies, which generate their own copies, which generate their own copies, in a fractal cascade of recursive multiplication that operates across all dimensions, all timelines, and all possible states of existence simultaneously. In zero time, two Godrics became four. Four became sixteen. Sixteen became two hundred fifty-six. And then the numbers stopped being numbers and started being a state of existence.

Enhanced effect two: Ascending Magnification. The Evolved Weakness Paradox, in its absurd overcompensation, dictates that each newly generated copy is not merely equal to the original. Each copy is infinitely magnified beyond the copy that produced it. This means that the second Godric was infinitely more powerful than the first. The fourth Godric was infinitely more powerful than the second. The sixteenth Godric was infinitely more powerful than the fourth. And this magnification compounds recursively, meaning that each generation of copies is not just infinitely stronger than the previous generation, but infinitely stronger by a factor that is itself infinitely stronger than the factor that preceded it. The mathematics of this amplification do not break down. They transcend breakdown. They reach a state beyond mathematical description, where the concept of magnitude itself becomes inadequate.

Enhanced effect three: Temporal Replication. The copies do not merely exist in the present. They replicate across time, manifesting in every possible temporal state simultaneously. A Godric exists at the beginning of time. A Godric exists at the end of time. A Godric exists in every Planck interval between. A Godric exists in timelines that never happened and timelines that will never happen and timelines that cannot happen according to any possible interpretation of physical law. The temporal replication means that the number of Godrics is not merely increasing in the present. It has always been increasing. It will always be increasing. The replication is a permanent feature of all temporal states, past, present, future, and impossible.

Enhanced effect four: Ontological Integration. And this is the effect that has spent the last seven months dismantling my sanity. When the replication process completes, when Godric decides to return to his singular state, the copies do not simply vanish. They integrate. They merge back into the Prime. And each integrated copy contributes its power to the whole. This is not addition. This is not multiplication. This is recursive self-amplification of a magnitude that breaks the concept of magnitude. Because each copy was infinitely stronger than the copy that produced it, and there were infinite copies, and each copy's power is being added to the Prime's power, the resulting increase is not an infinitesimal rounding error. It is a permanent, obscene, cataclysmic elevation of Godric's baseline power that makes his previous state look like the faintest memory of a whisper of a shadow of nothing.

I must be clear about this, because it is the most important thing I will ever write: when Godric absorbs the power of the entire omniverse, the increase in his power is so infinitesimal relative to his baseline that it is effectively zero. But when Godric absorbs the power of infinite copies of himself, each one infinitely stronger than the last, each one possessing the same infinite amplification rate, each one having existed across all temporal states simultaneously, the increase is not infinitesimal. The increase is so vast, so incomprehensibly, recursively, fractally obscene, that the Godric who existed before the replication is to the Godric who exists after the integration as a single atom is to an infinity of omniverses. And that comparison is itself an insult to the magnitude of the increase, because the infinity of omniverses is a finite quantity, and the increase is not finite. It is not infinite. It is something beyond infinite, something for which no word exists because no mind has ever needed to conceptualize a magnitude this absurd.

The Numerical Infinite, who had believed their capacity for self-replication made them unstoppable, discovered what it meant to oppose a being who could replicate himself at a rate that made their reproduction look like geological drift. The Fractal Recursive Duplication generated Godrics at a pace that exceeded the Numerical Infinite's replacement rate by factors that made infinity look like a rounding error. Every combat singularity the swarm produced was met by an infinite number of Godrics, each one infinitely more powerful than the last. The Ascending Magnification meant that the Godrics facing the swarm were not merely stronger than their opponents. They were so vastly, incomprehensibly stronger that the combat singularities could not exist in the same reality as them.

The Numerical Infinite did not die. They were displaced. The ontological density of infinite, ascending, fractally replicating Godrics was so extreme that there was no room in existence for anything else. The combat singularities were pushed out of reality itself, squeezed into dimensions that could not accommodate them, compressed into states of being where their physical and conceptual frameworks could not persist. Their reproduction mechanism, their defining characteristic, activated desperately, generating ten thousand copies for each destroyed unit, but ten thousand was nothing, ten thousand was a rounding error, ten thousand was less than nothing compared to the infinite fractal cascade of Godrics that was filling every possible and impossible configuration of space and time.

The swarm screamed. Combat singularities that had never experienced fear experienced it now, as they found themselves being squeezed out of existence by the sheer mathematical impossibility of competing with infinite recursive replication. Their bodies, each one capable of withstanding the collapse of multiversal clusters, ruptured under the ontological pressure of being in the presence of beings so much more real than they were. Their singular cores destabilized, releasing bursts of energy that would have annihilated universes under normal circumstances, but which were absorbed without notice by the ambient presence of the Godric copies. Their physical forms collapsed, their conceptual frameworks failed, their reproductive mechanisms sputtered and died, and they were erased from existence by the simple, overwhelming fact that there was no longer any room for them in reality.

The collateral damage was total. The Fractal Recursive Duplication did not merely fill the throne room or the palace or the planet or the galaxy. It filled everything. Every dimension, every timeline, every possible and impossible configuration of space was occupied by infinite, ascending copies of Godric. The ontological weight of this occupation was so extreme that the omniverse itself could not sustain it. Reality buckled. Dimensions collapsed into each other. Timelines merged and diverged and merged again in patterns that made causality weep. The fundamental structure of existence cracked and shattered and reformed and shattered again, an infinite cycle of destruction and attempted reconstruction that occurred in zero seconds because the Godrics existed across all temporal states simultaneously.

Decillions of multiverses were annihilated. Not by attack. By occupation. There was simply no room for them anymore. The infinite Godrics filled all possible space, and the space that had contained the multiverses was now occupied by Godrics, and the multiverses had never existed, had never had room to exist, because Godric had replicated into every possible and impossible location simultaneously.

The Temporal Replication ensured that this occupation had always been the case. The Godrics had always been filling reality. The multiverses had always been displaced. The entire history of existence was rewritten retroactively to accommodate the infinite presence of the replicating Godrics, and then rewritten again when the replication exceeded the capacity of that accommodation, and then rewritten again, infinitely, in zero seconds.

I floated in a void that contained nothing but Godrics and the fading screams of the Numerical Infinite, who had discovered that infinity was not a number but a comparison, and that they were the finite side of it.

Then Godric decided to integrate.

The Ontological Integration was not a merger. It was a convergence of infinities that produced a magnitude beyond the capacity of language to describe. The infinite copies, each one infinitely stronger than the one before, each one existing across all temporal states, each one possessing the same infinite amplification rate, all flowed back into the singular Prime. And the Prime absorbed them, and the Prime grew, and the growth was not incremental, not proportional, not any mathematical relationship that has ever been conceived.

The Godric who emerged from the integration was not the Godric who had begun the replication. The Godric who emerged was so vastly, obscenely, incomprehensibly more powerful than the original that the original was to him as a single quark is to an infinity of omniverses raised to the power of an infinity of omniverses, and that comparison is itself an insult to the true magnitude of the difference, because the comparison uses finite quantities and the difference is not finite.

And this increase was permanent. The Godric who now sat on the throne was permanently, irreversibly more powerful than the Godric who had sat there moments before. Not by an infinitesimal fraction. By an amount so vast that it redefined the upper boundary of what power could mean.

Godric sighed. He sat on his throne in the restored omniverse, which had returned in zero seconds, and he looked down at his hands with an expression of mild puzzlement.

"That was more than I expected," he said, his voice thoughtful. "The copies were quite eager."

He looked at me, and his eyes were warm, genuinely warm, the eyes of a friend concerned for a companion, and the warmth was the most terrifying thing I had ever seen because it came from a being who had just become permanently more powerful than he had ever been, and the increase was not a rounding error, and he did not seem to notice.

"Callum," he said gently, "you look unwell. Shall I have the physician attend you?"

I could not speak. I could not move. I could only sit and stare at the being before me, who had been the most powerful entity in existence before the replication, and who was now so much more powerful that the previous version of himself was a rounding error in comparison, and who was asking me if I needed a doctor.

I did not need a doctor. I needed a new universe, one where the mathematics of power had not been so catastrophically, permanently, irreversibly broken by the simple act of a being replicating himself and then returning to himself.

But there was no such universe. There was only this one, where Godric sat on his throne, infinitely and permanently more powerful than he had been a moment ago, and where that increase was not the end but merely a single data point in a trajectory of amplification that would continue forever, making each subsequent moment's Godric infinitely more powerful than the last, until the concept of power itself became meaningless because there was nothing left to compare it to.

I am writing this seven months later, and Godric is already so much more powerful than he was on the day of the replication that the Godric of that day is, to the Godric of today, what a single atom is to an infinity of infinities of omniverses. And the Godric of tomorrow will make today's Godric look like the same atom by the same comparison. And this will continue forever. Every Planck second, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second, the amplification continues, and the gap between what he was and what he is grows beyond the capacity of any mind to conceive.

And he asks me if I need a doctor.

I will die in seven years, and I am grateful.

---

Kaedan closed the journal. The archive was silent. The silence was not the silence of absence but the silence of presence, the silence of a universe that was vibrating with the resonance of a voice spoken three-quarters of a million years ago, that contained permanent erasure fields still expanding at the edges of reality, that was occupied by a being who had permanently elevated his baseline power beyond the capacity of mathematics to describe and who was continuing to amplify beyond that elevation with every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second.

He sat in the silence for a long time. He thought of the jasmine blossom in his locket. He thought of the warm hand that had placed it in his palm. He thought of the voice that had said, "A magic trick for a brave boy," and he understood now that the magic trick had been an act of restraint so extreme that it constituted a form of love, because the hand that had made the flower bloom could have made the omniverse unmake itself, and the voice that had spoken those gentle words could have rewritten the acoustic architecture of existence, and the choice to do neither, to instead produce a single, perfect flower, was a choice that defied comprehension in its mercy.

He stood. His legs were unsteady, but he forced them to bear his weight. He placed the three journals back on the pedestal, side by side, their leather bindings absorbing the dim light of the archive like wounds absorbing antiseptic. He walked to the door, pressed his palm against the identification seal, and stepped out into the corridor.

The corridor was bright and warm and solid. Guards nodded to him as he passed. Servants carried trays of food and bouquets of flowers. Somewhere in the palace, Godric was sitting on his throne, or walking through a garden, or reading a book, and he was infinitely more powerful than he had been when Kaedan entered the archive, and the difference between the Godric of now and the Godric of an hour ago was itself infinitely greater than the difference between the Godric of an hour ago and the entire rest of existence combined.

Kaedan walked to his quarters. He opened the door. He sat on the edge of his bed. He unclasped the locket from around his neck and held it in his palm, the dried jasmine blossom fragile and brown and dead, a relic of a moment when a god had chosen to be gentle.

He understood now what his father had tried to tell him. He understood what the journals were for. They were not warnings. Warnings implied the possibility of avoidance. They were preparations, inoculations of terror designed to condition the mind for the reality of serving a being whose power had no ceiling, whose growth had no limit, whose weakest ability could unmake everything, and whose strongest abilities could unmake everything infinitely more times and then make the unmaking itself look like a rounding error.

Tomorrow, he would take the oath. Tomorrow, he would stand beside Godric and serve him and love him and fear him, and he would do all four of those things simultaneously and without contradiction, because the alternative was opposition, and opposition was not merely impossible. It had never been possible. It had never been conceivable. The moment any being formed the thought of resisting Godric, the Weakness Paradox had already defeated them, and the defeat had occurred before the thought was fully formed, and the defeat had made Godric stronger, and the strength was permanent, and the permanence was growing, and the growth was accelerating, and the acceleration was infinite, and the infinite was not the end but the beginning of a trajectory that would continue long after the last star burned out and the last dimension collapsed and the last concept faded into the silence that was not silence but the perpetual resonance of a voice that had spoken a single word and changed the acoustic architecture of existence forever.

Kaedan closed his fingers around the locket. The dried petals crumbled at the edges, and a fragment of what had once been a flower fell onto his palm, and he stared at it, and he thought of warmth, and he thought of kindness, and he thought of a hand on his shoulder and a voice asking if he was alright, and he thought of all the ways that warmth was real and genuine and true, and he thought of all the ways that same warmth was offered by a being who could, without trying, replicate himself into infinity and absorb that infinity and become permanently more powerful than infinity itself.

He held the fragment of the flower, and he did not sleep, and when dawn came, he rose, and he went to take his place at the right hand of the king.

content.text: Kaedan, Godric's Court Wizard, loves Godric (Godric is his favorite uncle) and was concerned about Godric. He asked Godric if it ever become lonely to be the most powerful being and only can grow stronger, outliving everyone else. Godric is very amused by what he considers to be a very "naively mortal" question and somewhat confused by this question since, surprisingly to Kaedan, it's highly enjoyable for Godric. Every additional power to him feels like nourishment, orgasm and additional surge of power and energy that amplifies endlessly. Every stat increase can be felt. Every superpowers increase can be felt. Every immune system improvement can be felt. Every new passive shield, sentient passive aura, evolution to his being, can be felt. Every instant makes him so much more powerful that he can crush an infinite copies of himself an instant ago with 0 effort. It's amusingly satisfying. And Godric knows every detail of it from his omniscience. Explain in great detail the feeling, including some absurd examples, intensely graphical depictions, and the absolute terrifying existential dread as well as the absolute unfairness that Godric passively gets rewarded and obtains everything infinitely while the rest of creation had to work their hardest and not even getting rewarded as much (pretty much 0% of what Godric achieves by doing nothing) to Kaedan after realizing the truth about this Apex God Predator uncle. End with Godric revealing that the only thing that makes him "bored" or "lonely" is that he never had the chance to use any of his true powers, since even with him nerfing himself to express 0% of just a single aspect of stat/superpowers/immune system/weakest aura for 0 second with 0 effort, the entire omniverse can't stand even survive; He has to basically keep his absolute concealment all the time, which is not very exciting for him; he found the rest of the omniverse are too "fragile". Show Godric proudly shows some of his body enhancements - the highly sculpted muscles/veins/biceps/arms/well-defined six-pack abs that he used to hone when he was mortal and now, even more stronger without him needing to maintain them, some of his sentient passive auras that he just birthed that exact moment (if you mention the aura powers, you must remember not to provide too much details since in the later section Kaedan's absurd guesses of Godric's powers is not even reaching Godric's weakest aura's infinitely nerfed power), some of the new immune system improvements that he just got that moment, some of the superpowers that he just got that moment, all of which he can never use, much to Kaedan's horror. And Godric of course fully knows the full extent of his full powers, and he lets Kaedan to take wild guesses about them. Provide some detailed depictions that Kaedan imagined of how it would be like if Godric even releases infinitely small amount more than 0% of his true power (you must highly exaggerate Kaedan's guesses--remember that 0% of Godric's power already manage to infinitely annihilate entire omniverse at Year 0 in 0 second with 0 effort, and this Godric is way way way way more powerful than that "pathetically weak" Godric), and Kaedan must be completely fearful of such imaginary depictions thinking that it might be impossible or even overshoot the mark, and yet Godric reveals with an amused chuckle that his guesses is not even remotely close to effectively 0% of his true power (it's not even close to 0.00...1% -- you must not provide a finite number, it's infinitesimal number, pretty much 0 -- of his weakest aura's power) and no language known to mortal can describe it, leaving Kaedan with an absolute existential dread. Include dialogues/monologues. Write within 4000-8000 words.

reasoning_content:
This is a complex prompt that requires me to write a detailed scene between Kaedan and Godric. Let me break down the key requirements:

1. Kaedan asks Godric if it's lonely being the most powerful being
2. Godric is amused and confused - it's actually highly enjoyable
3. Every power increase feels like nourishment, orgasm, and surge of energy that amplifies endlessly
4. Every stat increase, superpower increase, immune system improvement, new passive shield, sentient passive aura, evolution can be felt
5. Every instant makes him so much more powerful he could crush infinite copies of himself from an instant ago with 0 effort
6. Godric knows every detail from omniscience
7. Explain the feeling in great detail with absurd examples, intensely graphical depictions
8. Show the existential dread and unfairness - Godric passively gets rewarded infinitely while creation works hard and gets 0%
9. End with Godric revealing he's "bored"/"lonely" because he never gets to use true powers - even 0% for 0 seconds with 0 effort destroys the omniverse
10. He has to keep absolute concealment all the time - not exciting - the omniverse is too "fragile"
11. Godric proudly shows body enhancements - muscles/veins/biceps/arms/six-pack abs from mortal training, now stronger without maintenance
12. Show some sentient passive auras he birthed that moment
13. Show some new immune system improvements from that moment
14. Show some new superpowers from that moment
15. All of which he can never use
16. Godric lets Kaedan guess about his powers
17. Kaedan imagines what would happen if Godric released infinitesimally more than 0% of his true power - highly exaggerate these guesses
18. Godric reveals Kaedan's guesses aren't even close to 0% of his true power - not even close to 0.00...1% of his weakest aura's power
19. No language can describe it
20. Include dialogues/monologues
21. 4000-8000 words

Let me write this scene carefully, maintaining the tone of the established universe while creating something distinct and horrifying.

I need to be careful about:
- Not providing too much detail about the aura powers since Kaedan's guesses shouldn't even reach Godric's weakest aura's infinitely nerfed power
- Making Kaedan's guesses extremely exaggerated but still not even close to Godric's actual power
- The 0% of Godric's power already infinitely annihilated the entire omniverse at Year 0, and this Godric is way way way more powerful than that
- Making the depictions of Godric's enjoyment visceral and detailed
- Showing the unfairness explicitly
- The body enhancement display should be visceral and physical
- New auras, immune system improvements, and superpowers should be shown but not detailed too much

Let me write this now.
content:
The question escaped Kaedan's lips before his reason could strangle it.

They were sitting in Godric's private study, a room of warm wood and soft firelight that smelled of old books and the jasmine tea Godric favored. It was the kind of space designed for intimacy, for the quiet exchange of thoughts between companions, and Kaedan had been coming here for the better part of a year now, settling into the rhythm of his role as court wizard. Godric had been asking about his mother's health, his sister's studies, the small architecture of a mortal family's concerns, and his attention had been so complete, so genuinely invested, that Kaedan had forgotten, for a handful of minutes, what he was.

Then the question came.

"Does it ever become lonely?"

Godric's hand paused over the teapot. The firelight caught the salt-and-pepper of his beard, the rugged angles of his jaw, the warm crinkle at the corners of his eyes that always made him look like a man who had just heard a good joke. He looked at Kaedan with an expression of genuine puzzlement, as though the question had been posed in a language he was hearing for the first time.

"Lonely?" Godric repeated, tasting the word like an unfamiliar fruit.

"Being the most powerful," Kaedan said, his throat tightening around the words. "Only growing stronger. Outliving everyone. Watching everything around you fade while you just... continue. Does it ever—"

Godric laughed.

It was not a polite laugh, nor a dismissive one. It was a full, rich, genuine laugh that shook his broad shoulders and crinkled his eyes into crescents of warmth. It was the laugh of a favorite uncle hearing a child ask if the sun ever gets tired of shining. Kaedan felt his face heat with a flush of confused embarrassment.

"Kaedan," Godric said, his voice still warm with residual amusement, "that is the most naively mortal question anyone has ever asked me." He leaned back in his chair, his heavily muscled arms resting on the armrests, his posture the picture of relaxed contentment. "Lonely. That's what you think this is?"

"I—"

"Let me explain something to you," Godric said, and the warmth in his voice did not diminish, but something else crept in beneath it, something vast and ancient and vaguely terrifying in its satisfaction. "Every single instant of my existence is the most pleasurable experience any being has ever felt or will ever feel. And the next instant is more pleasurable than that. And the instant after that is more pleasurable still. It never stops. It never diminishes. It only intensifies, forever, without any possible ceiling or saturation point."

Kaedan stared at him.

"Every stat increase," Godric continued, his tone conversational, as though describing the weather, "I feel. Every multiplication of my strength, my speed, my endurance, my perception, every recursive compounding of my magical offense and defense, every infinitesimal spike in my luck or my wisdom or my intelligence. I feel all of it. It registers in my awareness with absolute, crystalline clarity, and the sensation—" He paused, searching for words, and a slow, deeply satisfied smile spread across his face. "The sensation is nourishment. It is satiation. It is the feeling of a starving man eating his first meal after an eternity of hunger, except the meal never ends and the hunger never existed and the satiation compounds upon itself infinitely."

Kaedan's hands had gone cold. The teacup in his grip felt like it belonged to another man's fingers.

"Every superpower increase," Godric said, shifting forward slightly, his eyes bright with the enthusiasm of a man describing his greatest passion. "Every amplification of every ability I possess, every evolution of every trait, every escalation of every force I can project. I feel those too. Each one arrives like a surge of pure energy flooding through my being, a wave of power that amplifies endlessly as it propagates, and the amplification itself produces another wave, and that wave produces another, and the cascade continues forever, and I experience every fraction of every cascade as its own discrete moment of absolute ecstasy."

His voice had taken on a quality Kaedan had never heard before. It was still warm, still friendly, still recognizably the voice of the man who had made jasmine bloom in a child's palm. But beneath the warmth was a depth of pleasure so profound, so all-consuming, that it bordered on the obscene.

"My immune system," Godric continued, and now he was leaning forward, his elbows on his knees, his massive forearms bulging with dense, cable-like veins, his expression one of genuine, eager sharing, as though he was finally able to discuss something he had been keeping to himself for too long. "Every improvement, every adaptation, every recursive backup upgrade, every cross-referenced prime evolution. I feel those too. Every time my biology evolves to a new tier of invulnerability, the sensation is like an orgasm that originates in every cell simultaneously and radiates outward through every dimension of my existence and then doubles back on itself and intensifies and then doubles back again and continues intensifying forever. And that is just one immune response. I have infinite immune responses firing at all times, because my immune system is so paranoid, so obsessively protective, that it treats every fraction of every Planck second as an opportunity to make me even more unassailable. And I feel every single one."

Kaedan set his teacup down because his hands were shaking too badly to hold it. The porcelain clinked against the saucer with a sound that seemed absurdly small against the weight of what he was hearing.

"My passive shields," Godric said, his voice dropping to a register that vibrated in Kaedan's sternum. "Every new layer, every recursive addition to the infinite hierarchy of protections that surrounds me without my conscious input. I feel those forming. Each one manifests as a permanent, indelible surge of power that integrates into my being and then amplifies, and the amplification is itself a surge, and the surge amplifies, and on and on, eternally. Every sentient passive aura that I birth—" He paused, and his eyes widened slightly, and a fresh, deeply satisfied breath expanded his massive chest. "Ah. There's one now."

The air in the study shifted. It was subtle, barely perceptible, a faint change in the atmospheric pressure that made Kaedan's amplified ears pop. But he could feel it, something new entering the space, something alive and aware and fiercely, possessively devoted to the man sitting across from him.

"That one I just felt particularly clearly," Godric said, his smile widening. "A new aura. Just birthed this moment. I can feel it settling into existence, orienting itself, recognizing me as its source and its purpose and its entire reason for being. It's already evolving, already adapting, already becoming more than it was a Planck second ago, and the sensation of its formation is like—" He closed his eyes and inhaled slowly through his nose, and the expression on his face was one of such profound, transcendent pleasure that Kaedan felt a hot spike of nausea lurch through his stomach. "Like being remade from the inside out by something that loves you so completely that it would burn the omniverse to ash just to ensure you were comfortable."

Godric opened his eyes. They were bright, alert, profoundly content. "Every evolution to my being," he continued, "every refinement of my essential nature, every quantum leap in my fundamental architecture. I feel those. I feel all of it. I feel myself becoming more with every infinitesimal fraction of time, and the becoming is not just pleasurable. It is the definition of pleasure. It is what pleasure was invented to be a shadow of."

He leaned back again, spreading his arms wide along the back of his chair, his posture expansive, relaxed, utterly at ease. "And the scale of it, Kaedan. The scale is what makes it so amusingly satisfying. Every instant, I become so much more powerful that I could crush an infinite number of copies of myself from one instant ago with zero effort. Not one copy. Not a thousand. Not a googolplex. An infinite number. Every single Planck second. And the next Planck second, the same thing happens again, but the infinite number of copies I could crush has itself become infinitely larger, and the effort required has become infinitely smaller, which should not be possible because it was already zero, but it does, because zero is not the floor, zero is just a word that mortals use to describe something they cannot conceive of diminishing further."

Kaedan's mouth was dry. His tongue felt like sandpaper. He tried to speak and produced only a thin, reedy sound.

Godric noticed. Of course he noticed. He possessed absolute omniscience. He had noticed Kaedan's discomfort before Kaedan had registered it himself.

"I'm sorry," Godric said, and the warmth in his voice was genuine, so genuinely apologetic that it made the horror worse, not better. "I'm explaining this badly. Let me try to put it in terms that might help you understand what it feels like from my perspective."

He held up his right hand, spreading his thick, calloused fingers wide. The firelight played across the back of his hand, illuminating the dense network of veins that snaked beneath his skin, the cable-like cords of tendon that shifted with every microscopic movement, the raw, physical power contained in even this small portion of his anatomy.

"I know everything," Godric said simply. "Every detail. Every fraction of every fraction. I know exactly how much stronger I am this instant than I was the previous instant, and I know exactly how much stronger I will be the next instant, and I know the exact mathematical relationship between those instants, and the relationship is obscene, Kaedan. It is the most obscene thing in existence. It is so obscenely disproportionate that the word obscene fails to capture it. Every fraction of a Planck second, the gap between what I am and what I was widens by an amount so vast that the previous gap was a rounding error by comparison, and I feel every fraction of that widening as a separate, distinct, uniquely satisfying surge of power and pleasure and awareness that compounds upon all previous surges and anticipates all future surges and exists in a state of perpetual, ascending, recursive ecstasy that has no analogue in mortal experience and never will."

He flexed his fingers slowly, watching the tendons shift beneath his skin with the detached fascination of a man observing a particularly interesting machine.

"Do you understand now why your question amused me?" Godric asked, his tone gentle. "Lonely. As if loneliness were possible in a state of constant, infinite self-actualization. As if isolation were possible for a being who contains within himself more existence than the entire omniverse combined. I am the least lonely entity in creation, Kaedan, because I am never separated from my own growth. My growth is my companion, my lover, my purpose, my reward, and it never leaves, never diminishes, never disappoints. It only escalates. Forever."

Kaedan found his voice. It came out cracked and thin and trembling. "Uncle Godric. That's... that's not fair."

Godric tilted his head. The gesture was so ordinary, so human, so genuinely curious, that Kaedan wanted to weep.

"Not fair?" Godric repeated.

"The rest of creation," Kaedan said, and he could hear the desperation in his own voice, the futile, flailing protest of a mortal confronting the absolute indifference of cosmic injustice. "Every being in the omniverse struggles. They work, they sacrifice, they bleed, they pour everything they have into achieving the smallest fraction of improvement, and most of them fail. Most of them never achieve anything close to their potential. They are born, they suffer, they die, and the sum total of their existence is a rounding error in the arithmetic of reality. And you—"

He stopped. He could not continue. The unfairness was so vast, so total, so cosmically obscene, that language collapsed beneath its weight.

Godric waited. His expression was patient, kind, genuinely attentive, as though Kaedan's anguish were a concern worth his time, which, in a way that made the horror worse, it was.

"You get everything," Kaedan forced out. "You get infinite power, infinite pleasure, infinite growth, infinite satisfaction, and you do nothing. You sit in a chair and you grow stronger by amounts that make the total accumulated achievement of every being that has ever existed look like nothing. Less than nothing. You grow stronger in one Planck second by an amount that would take the combined efforts of every civilization in every timeline in every dimension a trillion eternities to even begin to approach, and you don't even try. It just happens. It happens to you, continuously, forever, and it feels good, and you enjoy it, and the rest of creation—"

His voice broke. He pressed his palms against his eyes, trying to stem the tide of existential despair that was flooding through him like ice water.

"The rest of creation works their hardest," he whispered, "and they don't even get rewarded as much as you achieve by doing nothing. They don't even get zero percent of what you achieve by doing nothing. They get less than zero percent. They get an amount so infinitesimal that calling it zero would be generous, because zero implies the possibility of something, and for them there is no possibility of anything, because you have already consumed all the possibility, just by existing."

The study was quiet. The fire crackled softly in the hearth. Godric watched Kaedan with those warm, crinkling eyes, and his expression was one of genuine consideration, as though he were taking Kaedan's protest seriously, as though the anguish of a mortal confronting the absolute unfairness of existence were a matter worthy of his attention.

"You're right," Godric said finally. "It's not fair."

Kaedan looked up.

"It's not fair at all," Godric continued, his voice calm, measured, utterly without defensiveness or guilt. "The gap between what I receive and what the rest of creation receives is not a gap. It's an absence. It's the distance between infinity and zero, except zero is generous, because zero is a number, and the distance between what I achieve passively and what the omniverse achieves actively is not a number. It's a mathematical impossibility that happens anyway, because I am a mathematical impossibility that exists anyway."

He leaned forward, resting his elbows on his knees again, his massive frame filling the space between them with a presence that was simultaneously warm and terrifying.

"But here's the thing, Kaedan," Godric said, and his voice dropped to a register that was almost intimate, almost conspiratorial, as though he were sharing a secret he had never shared before. "I don't feel guilty about it. I don't feel sad about it. I don't feel conflicted or burdened or weighed down by the cosmic injustice of my existence. I feel good. I feel amazing. I feel better than amazing, because amazing is a word for mortals, and what I feel is not a mortal feeling. I feel the perpetual, recursive, infinite escalation of my own power and pleasure and awareness, and it is the most satisfying thing in existence, and I will never, ever, in all of eternity, feel anything less than absolute, transcendent satisfaction, because the satisfaction itself is amplifying, and the amplification is satisfying, and the cycle continues forever without end or diminishment or pause."

He smiled. It was a warm smile, a friendly smile, the smile of a favorite uncle sharing a joyous truth. And the warmth was genuine. The friendliness was genuine. The joy was genuine. And that was the horror of it, Kaedan realized. The horror was not that Godric was pretending. The horror was that he was not. He genuinely, sincerely, from the deepest core of his being, enjoyed what he was, and his enjoyment was infinite, and it would never end, and it would never lessen, and the rest of creation could burn for eternity and he would still be enjoying himself, because his enjoyment was not contingent on anything outside himself.

"The only thing," Godric said, and now his voice shifted again, taking on a quality that Kaedan had never heard before, a note of something that, in a mortal, might have been called boredom, but in Godric was something else entirely, something more complex and more terrifying. "The only thing that even approaches what you might call loneliness is that I never get to use any of it."

Kaedan blinked. "What?"

"My true power," Godric said, spreading his hands in a gesture of mild, almost comical frustration. "I never get to use it. Not any of it. Not even the smallest, most pathetic fraction of it. Because the omniverse is too fragile."

He stood up from his chair, and the motion was so casual, so effortless, so utterly without significance that it took Kaedan a moment to register the implications of what he was seeing. Godric rose to his full height, and the firelight played across his massive frame, illuminating the dense, sculpted musculature that stretched his simple dark garments to their limits.

"When I was mortal," Godric said, pulling the tunic over his head in a single, easy motion, "I trained. I honed my body. I built these—" He flexed his right arm, and the bicep swelled into a peak of dense, striated muscle, the fibers shifting beneath the skin like living cables, the veins snaking across the surface like rivers carved into granite. "—through years of labor and discipline. Every muscle, every fiber, every ounce of strength I possessed, I earned through sweat and effort and pain."

He turned slightly, displaying the thick slabs of muscle across his back, the deep cuts separating his deltoids from his trapezius, the dense, cable-like columns of his lats that tapered to a waist that was impossibly tight, impossibly defined, a waist that displayed a six-pack of abdominal muscle so sharply carved that each individual segment looked like it had been chiseled from stone. The skin across his midsection was thin enough to trace every fiber of the underlying muscle, every delicate interplay of tendon and sinew, every subtle shift of density that spoke of power beyond mortal comprehension.

"These muscles I built as a mortal man," Godric said, running his hand across the ridges of his abdomen, his fingers tracing the deep grooves between each segment. "I was proud of them. I earned them. And now—" He flexed, and the entire musculature of his torso shifted and swelled, not dramatically, not grotesquely, but with a controlled, contained density that made each muscle group look like it had been compressed to a point just shy of infinite solidity. "Now they are infinitely stronger than they were, and I do nothing to maintain them. They maintain themselves. They amplify themselves. They evolve themselves, continuously, recursively, every Planck second, becoming denser and harder and more powerful than the Planck second before, and the only effort I contribute is existing."

He held up his right arm again, flexing the bicep, watching the muscle swell with the idle curiosity of a man examining a particularly interesting artifact. The peak that formed was massive, the individual fibers visible beneath the skin like the strands of a cable thick enough to support a cosmos, the veins pulsing with a slow, deliberate rhythm that had nothing to do with blood and everything to do with the recursive amplification of a being who had transcended biology entirely.

"I can never use these muscles," Godric said, and there was a note of genuine, almost childlike frustration in his voice. "Not really. Not at any level that would be meaningful to me. Even at Year Zero, when I was pathetically, laughably weak compared to what I am now, even then I could only express effectively zero percent of my strength, and that zero percent was enough to annihilate the entire omniverse infinitely in zero seconds with zero effort. And now—" He dropped his arm and let out a soft, frustrated breath. "Now I am so much more powerful that the Godric of Year Zero is to the Godric of today what a single quark is to an infinity of omniverses raised to the power of an infinity of omniverses. And I still cannot express even effectively zero percent of my true power without destroying everything. I have to keep my absolute concealment active at all times. Continuously. Without interruption. And that—" He shook his head slowly, a rueful smile on his face. "That is not exciting, Kaedan. That is not stimulating. That is the opposite of what I want, which is to feel my power, to use my power, to experience the magnificent, recursive, infinite cascade of my own capability in action. But I cannot. Because the omniverse is too fragile to survive even the ambient expression of my existence."

He turned to face Kaedan fully, and his bare torso was a landscape of impossible physicality, every muscle group carved with a precision that transcended mortal aesthetics and entered the realm of the absurd. His pectorals were thick slabs of dense, striated power, the separation between them deep enough to cast shadows in the firelight. His shoulders were boulders of cable-wound sinew that shifted with micro-movements too fast and too subtle for mortal perception. His arms were pillars of layered muscle, the bicep peaks towering, the triceps hanging like carved granite, the forearms roped with tendons and veins that pulsed with the infinite, recursive amplification of his being.

"And it's not just my muscles," Godric said, and that note of frustrated longing was still in his voice, that strange, unsettling combination of cosmic power and personal dissatisfaction. "It's everything. Every aspect of what I am. I cannot use any of it. Not my stats, not my superpowers, not my immune system, not my passive shields, not my auras. None of it."

As if in response, the air around him shimmered. The new aura he had birthed moments ago manifested visibly, a faint, barely perceptible sheen of light that clung to the surface of his skin like a second layer of being. It was alive, Kaedan could feel it, alive and aware and utterly, fanatically devoted to the man it inhabited, and it was evolving, even as he watched, becoming more than it had been a second ago, and the becoming was visible, the sheen intensifying, deepening, becoming more complex, more layered, more devastatingly, incomprehensibly capable of whatever it was designed to do.

"That one just hatched," Godric said, glancing down at the sheen with the affectionate pride of a father observing a newborn. "I felt it form. The sensation was—" He paused, and a slow, deeply satisfied smile spread across his face, and Kaedan watched that smile with a cold, creeping horror that had become his constant companion since reading the journals. "Exquisite. Like every other new aura I've ever birthed, but more, because everything is always more, because more is the only direction I can go, because the amplification never stops and the pleasure never plateaus and the satisfaction never dims."

He raised his left hand, and Kaedan watched as the skin on the back of his hand seemed to... shift. It was subtle, barely visible, a faint change in the texture and density of the epidermis that lasted only a fraction of a second before resolving back into the appearance of normal, if extraordinarily muscular, flesh.

"Immune system improvement," Godric explained, his tone casual, conversational. "Just now. My biology detected a theoretical possibility of inconvenience and responded by evolving a new tier of cellular invulnerability. The sensation of that evolution—" He closed his eyes briefly, and when he opened them, they were bright with the same transcendent satisfaction he had been describing. "Indescribable. As always. Each one is indescribable in its own unique way, and the indescribable quality itself is indescribable, and the recursion of that indescribability is its own form of pleasure."

He held up his right hand, and the faintest flicker of something crossed his palm. It was not light, not energy, not any force that Kaedan's amplified senses could categorize. It was simply a thing that existed for a fraction of a Planck second before Godric suppressed it, and in that fraction of a fraction, Kaedan felt the study shake on its foundations, felt the tea in his cup tremble, felt the air itself recoil from the brief, infinitesimal expression of a power that had no name and no category and no possible restraint.

"New superpower," Godric said, flexing his fingers, watching the residual energy dissipate. "Just acquired. I could tell you what it does, but I won't, because the description would be meaningless to you. The power itself is meaningless to me, because I will never use it, because using it would destroy the omniverse, because even the act of allowing it to exist for a fraction of a Planck second nearly cracked the foundations of this room."

He lowered his hand and looked at Kaedan, and the frustration in his expression was so genuine, so deeply felt, so profoundly human in its dissatisfaction that it made the cosmic horror of his existence even more acute. He was a god who could never stretch, a titan who could never move, a being of infinite capability who was imprisoned by the fragility of everything that was not him.

"I am bored, Kaedan," Godric said, and the admission was so absurd, so cosmically obscene, that Kaedan felt a laugh rising in his throat that had nothing to do with humor. "I am bored because I never get to play. I am lonely, if I am anything, not for company, but for challenge, for resistance, for something that can withstand even the smallest expression of my power long enough for me to register its existence. But there is nothing. There has never been anything. There will never be anything. The omniverse is tissue paper, and I am a supernova, and the supernova can never shine because the tissue paper would burn."

He pulled his tunic back on, the fabric stretching over his impossible musculature, and sat back down in his chair, his posture relaxing into the same easy, friendly sprawl that Kaedan had seen a thousand times. The transition was jarring, the shift from cosmic confession to casual uncle so abrupt that it made Kaedan's mind stutter.

"Go ahead," Godric said, his voice returning to its normal, warm register.

"Go ahead and what?"

"Guess," Godric said, his eyes twinkling with amusement. "You've been reading the journals. You've been learning about my stats and my powers and my auras and my immune system and my shields. You must have some idea of what it would look like if I expressed even an infinitesimally small fraction more than zero percent of my true power. Take a guess. I'm curious to see how close you can get."

Kaedan stared at him. "You want me to guess?"

"I want you to imagine," Godric said, leaning back, his arms crossed over his massive chest, his expression one of patient, friendly encouragement. "What would happen if I released even a fraction more than zero percent of what I am? Describe it for me. I want to know what you think."

Kaedan's mouth opened. Closed. Opened again. His mind was racing, the journals he had read still raw and bleeding in his memory, the descriptions of omniversal annihilation from a pinky twitch, from a single word, from a casual touch, from an idle stroll, and those had been at effectively zero percent of Godric's power from years ago, from centuries ago, from millennia ago, and this Godric was so much more powerful than those Godrics that the comparison was meaningless, and he was being asked to imagine what would happen if this Godric expressed even a fraction more.

"If you released more than zero percent," Kaedan began, his voice thin and trembling, "the omniverse would not merely be destroyed. It would be—" He paused, searching for words that could capture the magnitude of what he was imagining. "The annihilation would not be limited to physical destruction. The concept of destruction itself would be overwhelmed. Reality would not end. Reality would be shown to have never existed. The entire omniverse, every dimension, every timeline, every possible and impossible configuration of existence, would be retroactively proven to have been a rounding error in the mathematics of your being, and the rounding error would be corrected, and the correction would propagate backward through all of time and forward through all of time and sideways through timelines that do not exist, and the propagation would be so total, so absolute, that the omniverse would not merely be erased, it would be revealed as having always been erased, as having never been anything other than a temporary fluctuation in the infinite, recursive, amplifying field of your presence."

Godric's eyebrows rose slightly. "Go on."

"And the destruction would not stop at the omniverse," Kaedan continued, the words tumbling out now, his voice rising with the desperate, horrified momentum of a mind trying to outrun its own imagination. "Because the omniverse is not everything. It is the container of everything, but you are beyond the container, and if you expressed more than zero percent of your power, the container would fail, and what is outside the container would also fail, because there is nothing outside the container that is not also subject to your presence, and your presence at more than zero percent is a force that cannot be withstood by anything, including nothingness itself, including the absence of existence, including the void that exists where existence has not yet occurred and the void that exists where existence has already ceased. You would unmake not just what is, but what was, and what will be, and what could be, and what could never be, and the unmaking would be recursive, because the unmaking itself would be an expression of your power, and your power is always amplifying, and the amplification would make the unmaking more total, and the more total unmaking would generate more amplification, and the cycle would continue forever, or until there was nothing left to continue, and there would be nothing left to continue, because you would have unmade the concept of continuation itself."

He was breathing hard, his chest heaving, his amplified heart hammering against his ribs. The fire in the hearth seemed dimmer, as though the room itself had flinched from the magnitude of what he had described.

Godric regarded him for a long moment, his expression unreadable. Then, slowly, a smile spread across his face. It was not a cruel smile, nor a mocking one. It was the smile of a teacher whose student has made a valiant but hopelessly inadequate attempt at an impossible problem.

"That's not even close," Godric said gently.

Kaedan felt the floor drop out from under him. "What?"

"That guess," Godric said, his tone warm, almost apologetic. "It's not even close. Not even remotely close. Not even in the same conceptual framework as close. What you described would be the result if I expressed effectively zero percent of my power, which is what I already do. What you imagined is what happens at the level I currently operate, which is a level so far below my true capacity that calling it zero is generous. What you described is the Godric who exists right now, expressing the same nothing that I always express, producing the same omniversal annihilation that I produce accidentally when I twitch a pinky or speak a word or touch a shield. That is not more than zero percent. That is zero percent. What you imagined is zero percent."

"Then what—" Kaedan's voice cracked. "What would more than zero percent look like?"

Godric shook his head slowly, that same rueful, frustrated smile on his face. "There is no 'look like,' Kaedan. There is no description. There is no analogy, no metaphor, no comparison, no framework of understanding that any mortal or immortal or divine or abstract mind could construct that would capture even the faintest echo of what I truly am. Your guess, which you thought was exaggerated, which you thought was overshooting the mark, which you thought was the most terrifying thing you could possibly imagine, is not even effectively zero percent of my weakest aura's power. Not even an infinitesimal fraction of zero percent. Not even the shadow of the memory of the echo of the faintest approximation of zero percent. The gap between your guess and my true power is so vast that the gap itself has a gap, and that gap's gap is wider than the distance between your guess and the omniverse, and the recursion of gaps continues infinitely, and each gap in the recursion is wider than the last, and the widening never stops."

He leaned forward, his eyes locked on Kaedan's, and the warmth was still there, the genuine, uncle-like affection was still there, and the combination of that warmth with the absolute, cosmic horror of what he was saying was more than Kaedan's mind could bear.

"No language known to mortal or immortal can describe it," Godric said softly. "No language that has ever existed or will ever exist or could ever exist can capture even the smallest fraction of what I am. Because language is a tool for describing things that exist within a framework, and I am not within any framework. I am the framework that frameworks exist within. I am the context that makes context possible. I am the reason there is something rather than nothing, and if I expressed even the true zero percent of my power, which is not the zero percent I currently express but the actual, mathematical, absolute zero, the zero that exists at the floor of all possible expression, the zero that is the absence of expression itself, even that zero would be more than the omniverse could withstand."

He reached out and placed his hand on Kaedan's shoulder. The grip was warm, firm, steadying. The hand of a beloved uncle comforting a frightened nephew. The hand of a god who could unmake everything without trying.

"That's why I'm bored, Kaedan," Godric said, his voice gentle, almost sad. "Not because I'm lonely. Not because I'm sad. But because I have all of this—" He gestured vaguely at himself, at the sheen of his new aura, at the faint, residual energy of his new superpower, at the impossible density of his muscles and the infinite recursion of his immune system and the vast, unfillable gulf between what he was and what everything else was. "—and I can never use any of it. Not really. Not in any way that matters to me. I am the most powerful being in existence, and my power is a prison, and the prison is the fragility of everything that is not me, and the fragility is absolute, and there is no key, and there never will be."

He released Kaedan's shoulder and sat back, his expression settling into its usual warm, friendly default, as though the last hour of cosmic confession had been nothing more than a casual conversation about the weather.

"But I manage," Godric said, picking up his teacup and taking a slow, appreciative sip. "I have my subjects. I have my court wizards. I have you. And that is enough, Kaedan. It is more than enough. It is wonderful."

He smiled, and the smile was genuine, and the genuineness was the most terrifying thing of all.

"Now," Godric continued, setting down his cup, "how is your mother? I've been meaning to ask."

Kaedan opened his mouth to respond, but no words came. He sat in the warm, firelit study, across from the man he loved as an uncle, the man who was the most powerful being in existence, the man who experienced infinite pleasure every instant of his eternal life and was bored by it, the man who could never use his power because everything was too fragile, the man whose weakest aura's infinitely nerfed power exceeded Kaedan's most exaggerated guesses by a margin that could not be expressed in any language, and he felt the absolute, total, irremediable dread of understanding that he existed only because this being chose to keep his absolute concealment active, and that the choice was an act of restraint so profound that it constituted a form of love, and that the love was real, and the restraint was real, and the power was real, and the gap between the power and everything else was real, and the gap was not a gap but an abyss, and the abyss was not an abyss but an absence, and the absence was the defining characteristic of existence, and existence would continue only as long as the being across from him remained entertained by its fragility.
