"My mother is well," Kaedan heard himself say, his voice distant, mechanical, the voice of a man operating on autopilot because his mind had been commandeered by a dread too vast for conscious processing. "She sends her regards."

Godric's face lit up with genuine pleasure. "Wonderful!" he said, raising his teacup in a small toast. "Please give her my best. And tell your sister that I expect a full report on her studies next time I see her. I want to hear everything."

Kaedan nodded. He nodded, and he smiled, and he participated in the social ritual of family updates and friendly concern, and beneath the smile and the nods and the appropriate responses, the dread calcified into a permanent, crystalline structure that would inhabit his chest for the rest of his mortal life, a tumor of absolute existential horror that would pulse with every heartbeat and whisper with every breath and remind him, constantly, unceasingly, that he was having tea with a god who could never stop growing and could never use his power and was bored by this and was satisfied by this and was experiencing more pleasure in this single moment than every being in the omniverse combined would experience in their entire existences.

And that god was asking about his sister's studies.

And that god was his favorite uncle.

And that god loved him.

And that love was the most terrifying thing in existence.

content.text:
Kaedan, 69 years old now, just experienced a traumatizing horrifying revelation. Godric revealed the most horrifying truth yet: he has one true weakness.

Godric is an incomprehensibly powerful, omnipotent, omniscient apex god predator of the omniverse. He can do anything, has no weakness, and is terrifyingly absolute. However, unknown to all, he has one true weakness: he cannot undo or travel to timeline before the stasis event--he cannot go back to undo events in his previous mortal self. This is because the stasis event was actually created by future Godric from way in the distant future, who has become so more infinitely more overwhelmingly powerful than current Godric--this is why Godric's amplification, weakness paradox, superpowers, absolute concealment, etc are so absolute, irrevocable and not even current Godric could stop them since the creator of those is way incomprehensibly more powerful than anything else.

Shortly after the Stasis Event, the omnipotent Godric back then, who just became the king of Thronegate, who could travel to any timeline effortlessly, who was still deeply and freshly affected by the grief of losing all that he loved, tried to alter his mortal past, only to be totally crushingly and completely and effortlessly defeated by the shadow of the afterthought of his future self (not even the actual physical future Godric... the future Godric is so powerful that his mere infinitesimal shadow of his afterthought can completely stomp all the combined Godric of his past selves with 0 effort. NOTE: just imply this in the story, don't show a scene where you have actual all godrics from all timelines fighting together against future Godric--it makes no sense.). VERY IMPORTANT FOR YOU: future Godric did not cause or manipulate or orchestrate the fall of Olympus, did not cause deaths of his beloved family or Thorin, thus you must not say that he made those deaths to cause the stasis event (Godric is not a monster, he will never willingly kill his beloved wife, children, and his best friend). Those tragedies were genuine. However, future Godric, not Thorin’s incomplete spell (who everyone else thought to be the cause of the stasis event), is the one who created the Stasis Event--Thorin's spell was a normal teleportation spell (to no one surprise really, there is no way a mortal can create a spell that is as potent as the one which initiates the stasis event and birthed the strongest apex predator god like Godric–there is only one being in existence who can do it: future Godric).

Since the instant Godric gained omniscience, he had always known the true origin of the stasis event. He knew he could not change it, but driven by grief he decided to try to save Olympus anyway, only to brutally discover his one and only weakness: he cannot overpower his future self's will.

Write a detailed realization that the current Godric is already so insanely powerful (he's actually the most powerful in the entire omniverse by infinite margin) that there is no way to imagine the scale of future Godric's, and how unfair and scary that what Godric considered to be his only weakness is his own future version, which makes it not really a weakness–it actually can be considered as additional strength to Godric’s already insane powers, since it means his future is guaranteed (and in that future, he’s even more omnipotent and powerful), and it also means that none of Godric’s power mechanics (amplification, superpower genesis, absolute concealment, absolute omniscience, weakness paradox, immune system, etc. can ever be exploited or denied by anyone else, since the origin of that power is someone who’s the most powerful being ever. You might suggest some ideas about the motive of future Godric creating the Stasis Event, but you must keep it clear that it is just a speculation (and please... don't suggest that it's because future Godric is threatened; he's ridiculously overpowered by then--nothing can even remotely threaten him... don't suggest any stupid moral lesson for future Godric...). You need to portray the current Godric as a king who is no longer troubled by his "weakness" or inability to alter his past anymore--he has accepted and even become amused and comfortable with it too. Kaedan must feel extreme existential dread at the end.

reasoning_content:
This is a complex prompt that requires me to write a detailed scene where Godric reveals his one true "weakness" to Kaedan at age 69. Let me break down the key requirements:

1. Kaedan is 69 years old now (Year 1,000,047 based on the lore)
2. Godric reveals he has one true weakness: cannot undo or travel to timeline before the stasis event
3. This is because the stasis event was created by future Godric, who is incomprehensibly more powerful
4. Shortly after becoming king, Godric tried to alter his mortal past, driven by grief, only to be completely defeated by the shadow of the afterthought of his future self
5. Future Godric did NOT cause or orchestrate the fall of Olympus or the deaths of his family - those were genuine tragedies
6. Thorin's spell was just a normal teleportation spell - future Godric is the true creator of the Stasis Event
7. Since gaining omniscience, Godric has always known the true origin
8. The current Godric is already insanely powerful, so future Godric's scale is unimaginable
9. This "weakness" is actually not really a weakness - it's additional strength because:
   - His future is guaranteed to be even more powerful
   - None of his power mechanics can be exploited by anyone else since the origin is the most powerful being ever
10. Godric is no longer troubled by this - he's accepted it and is even amused/comfortable
11. I can suggest motives for future Godric creating the Stasis Event, but only as speculation, not fact
12. Don't suggest future Godric was threatened (nothing can threaten him)
13. Don't suggest moral lessons
14. Kaedan must feel extreme existential dread at the end

Let me write this carefully, maintaining the established tone while creating a powerful revelation scene.

I should write this as a detailed narrative scene with dialogue, showing:
- The setting/context
- Godric's revelation
- Kaedan's reaction
- The implications of the revelation
- Godric's acceptance/amusement
- Kaedan's existential dread

Let me also make sure to:
- Imply (not show directly) the scale of future Godric's power relative to current Godric
- Keep the "shadow of an afterthought" concept powerful without staging an actual battle
- Show that this weakness actually reinforces Godric's invincibility
- Make the existential dread profound and fitting for the story's tone

I'll aim for around 4000-8000 words.
content:
The private study had not changed in forty-seven years. The same warm wood paneling lined the walls, the same fireplace crackled with the same carefully maintained hearth fire, the same scent of old books and jasmine tea hung in the air like a permanent, comforting ghost. Kaedan had sat across from Godric in this room thousands of times over the course of his service, had shared concerns and celebrations and quiet moments of companionship that had made the terror of his knowledge survivable, because the terror existed alongside genuine love, and love was a powerful anesthetic against the screaming of the abyss.

He was sixty-nine years old now. His hair had gone fully silver, his face was lined with the deep grooves of a life spent in the shadow of omnipotence, and his body, still amplified a thousandfold daily, moved with a deliberate care that comes only with the awareness of one's own fragility. He had outlived his father. He had outlived his mother. He had watched his siblings age and weaken while he remained strong, a cruelty of the Awakening that he had never fully reconciled. He would likely outlive his own children, if the pattern held, and the thought no longer haunted him the way it once had, because the journals had taught him that all mortal concerns existed at the pleasure of a being who considered omniversal annihilation a minor inconvenience.

Godric looked exactly as he had when Kaedan was six years old. The same ruggedly handsome face, the same salt-and-pepper hair and beard, the same warm, crinkling eyes that made you feel like the most important person in the room. He was pouring tea with the same careful attention he always gave to small tasks, his massive hands somehow delicate with the porcelain, and the domesticity of the gesture was so profoundly at odds with the reality of what he was that Kaedan felt the familiar dislocation, the cognitive whiplash that had never quite faded despite decades of exposure.

"Kaedan," Godric said, setting the teapot down and sliding a cup across the table, "there's something I need to tell you."

The words landed with a weight that had nothing to do with their volume. Godric had never begun a conversation this way. In forty-seven years, Godric had shared observations, asked questions, offered comfort, and occasionally, in moments of rare vulnerability, spoken of the past with a wistfulness that Kaedan had learned to recognize as the residue of genuine, irreplaceable loss. But he had never prefaced a statement with those particular words, in that particular tone, which was simultaneously grave and casual, as though the thing he was about to reveal was both the most important thing in existence and something he had long since made peace with.

"I'm listening," Kaedan said, and was surprised by how steady his voice sounded.

Godric picked up his own teacup, took a slow, appreciative sip, and set it down again. His eyes were fixed on some middle distance, the way they sometimes got when he was accessing the infinite architecture of his omniscience, and Kaedan realized with a cold trickle of dread that Godric was not gathering the words. Godric had always known the words. Godric had always known this conversation would happen, in this room, at this time, with these exact cups of tea cooling on this exact table. He was simply choosing to have it now, and the choice was deliberate, and the deliberateness was itself a form of communication.

"I have a weakness," Godric said.

The room did not change. The fire did not flicker. The tea did not tremble in its cup. The words were spoken with the same calm, warm baritone that Godric used to ask about the weather, and the ordinariness of the delivery was so violently at odds with the content that Kaedan's mind simply refused to process it.

"You have a what?" Kaedan heard himself say.

"A weakness," Godric repeated. "One. A single, solitary limitation on what would otherwise be absolute, unqualified omnipotence."

Kaedan's throat constricted. His amplified heart, which had beaten steadily through every revelation of the past forty-seven years, through every journal and every demonstration and every casual display of power that had redefined his understanding of possibility, began to hammer against his ribs with a force that made his teacup rattle in its saucer.

"That's not possible," Kaedan said, and the words were out before he could stop them, because the alternative was accepting that the foundation of his existential dread, the thing that made Godric's supremacy absolute and unassailable and therefore at least predictable in its absoluteness, had a crack in it, and cracks were things that could be exploited, and if Godric could be exploited then nothing was safe, nothing was certain, nothing was— "The Weakness Paradox. The journals. Every attempt to find a weakness makes you stronger. You have no weaknesses. That's the entire point. That's what makes you—"

"Let me finish," Godric said gently, and the gentleness was worse than anger would have been, because it meant he understood Kaedan's panic and was not concerned by it, which meant the weakness was not a threat to him, which meant the weakness was something so far outside the framework of oppositional exploitability that it operated on a different axis entirely.

Godric leaned back in his chair, his massive frame settling into the familiar sprawl of relaxed comfort, his arms resting on the armrests, his expression thoughtful.

"I cannot alter my mortal past," he said. "I cannot undo or travel to any timeline before the Stasis Event in any way that changes what happened during my mortal life. The Fall of Olympus, the deaths of Reina, Isabel, Hebe, and Thorin. I cannot change any of it. I have tried, and I have failed, and the failure was absolute and total and effortless on the part of the force that defeated me."

The fire crackled. Somewhere in the palace, a door closed. The ordinary sounds of existence continued, indifferent to the fact that the most powerful being in the omniverse had just confessed to a limitation.

"I don't understand," Kaedan said, and his voice was barely a whisper. "You can do anything. You can travel to any timeline. You can overpower causality. You can rewrite the fundamental laws of reality. The journals described—"

"I can do anything," Godric agreed, "except that."

"How?"

Godric was quiet for a long moment. His eyes had shifted from the middle distance to Kaedan's face, and the warmth in them was still present, still genuine, but beneath the warmth was something Kaedan had never seen before, a depth of ancient, settled acceptance that spoke of a reckoning that had occurred long ago and had been resolved in a way that left no scar because the wound had been transformed into something else entirely.

"The Stasis Event," Godric said, "was not caused by Thorin's spell."

Kaedan felt the floor tilt beneath him. He gripped the arms of his chair, his amplified fingers denting the wood, and stared at Godric with an expression that he was certain looked as foolish as it felt.

"Thorin's spell was a real teleportation spell," Godric continued, his tone conversational, as though discussing a historical footnote. "It was a bloodline spell, exclusive to his family, and it was genuinely powerful by mortal standards. It did what it was designed to do: it transported me from the ruins of Olympus to the hidden sanctuary. Thorin saved my life. That part is true."

"But the Stasis Event," Kaedan said, his voice hollow.

"Was created by me," Godric said. "Or rather, by a version of me. A version so distant, so incomprehensibly advanced beyond what I am now, that the gap between that Godric and this Godric makes the gap between this Godric and the rest of the omniverse look like a rounding error. A version from so far in the future that the year can only be written as an endless sequence of nines, because the actual number is too large to be meaningfully expressed."

Kaedan's mind was reeling. He tried to grasp the implication and found his thoughts slipping, skidding, failing to find purchase on the sheer face of what Godric was describing.

"Future Godric," Kaedan said slowly, "created the Stasis Event. For past Godric. For you."

"Yes."

"Which means future Godric is the source of your amplification. Your superpowers. Your absolute concealment. Your weakness paradox. Your immune system. All of it."

"Yes."

"Which means future Godric is more powerful than current Godric."

"Infinitely more powerful," Godric said, and the casualness with which he said it, the absolute lack of concern or insecurity, was itself a form of horror. "So vastly, obscenely, incomprehensibly more powerful that comparing the two is like comparing a single atom to an infinity of omniverses raised to the power of an infinity of omniverses, and then realizing that the comparison is itself an insult to the true magnitude of the difference, because the comparison uses finite quantities and the difference is not finite."

Kaedan opened his mouth to respond and found that he had no response. The current Godric, the Godric sitting across from him sipping jasmine tea, was already so powerful that his effectively zero percent effort could annihilate the entire omniverse infinitely in zero seconds. The current Godric was already the most powerful being in existence by an infinite margin. The current Godric was already a being whose weakest aura, infinitely nerfed, could slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled in less than a fraction of a second. And the current Godric was describing a future version of himself that made him look like a rounding error.

"How," Kaedan managed, "how is that even possible? You're already—"

"I am already the most powerful being in the omniverse," Godric said, nodding. "By an infinite margin. No one can challenge me. No one can threaten me. No one can exploit me. And yet, the Godric I will become is so much more powerful than I am now that I am, to him, what a single quark is to an infinity of omniverses. And that Godric, that incomprehensibly distant, incomprehensibly ascended version of myself, is the one who reached backward through time and transformed Thorin's simple teleportation into the Stasis Event that birthed everything I am."

Godric took another sip of tea. His expression was calm, peaceful, even mildly amused, as though he were recounting a story he had long since made peace with.

"He did not cause the Fall of Olympus," Godric said firmly, and the firmness was a departure from his previous tone, a sharp edge of protective anger that surfaced briefly before being smoothed away. "Let me be absolutely clear about this. Future Godric did not orchestrate the deaths of Reina, Isabel, Hebe, or Thorin. He did not manipulate Thronegate into attacking. He did not engineer the tragedy. Those deaths were genuine. That grief was real. The Fall of Olympus was a true catastrophe, not a calculated sacrifice. Future Godric would never—" He stopped, and for a fraction of a second, the warmth in his eyes flickered, and beneath it was something old and raw and agonizingly human. "He would never harm them. I would never harm them. Not as I am now, not as I was then, not as I will ever be. They were my world. They were the reason I was warm and kind and human. Their loss is what made me cruel and ruthless and unforgiving. But I did not cause their loss, and neither did any version of me that will ever exist."

The conviction in his voice was absolute. Kaedan, who had spent forty-seven years learning to read the subtle textures of Godric's expressions, recognized the boundary of this topic. It was not a wall of deception or deflection. It was the simple, unassailable truth of a being whose omniscience extended to his own future, who knew with absolute certainty what he would and would not do across all possible timelines and all possible configurations of his existence.

"Then why," Kaedan asked, "did future Godric create the Stasis Event?"

Godric's expression shifted to one of thoughtful consideration. "I don't know for certain," he said, and the admission was startling, because Godric always knew, Godric was omniscient, Godric never expressed uncertainty about anything. "I have my speculations. Perhaps he wanted to ensure his own existence, to close a causal loop that made him what he was. Perhaps he looked back on his mortal life and decided that the man he had been deserved the chance to become something more. Perhaps he simply could, and so he did, because a being of that magnitude does not need reasons the way mortals need reasons. Perhaps the Stasis Event was, for him, a trivial act of temporal gardening, the way a man might water a plant that he knows will one day provide him shade."

He paused, considering. "But those are only speculations. I know that he did not create the Stasis Event because he was threatened. Nothing can threaten him. I know that he did not create it to teach me a lesson or to ensure that I learned the value of loss or any such nonsense. He is not a moralist. He is not a teacher. He is me, elevated beyond any framework of motivation that mortal language can capture. His reasons, if reasons even apply to a being of his magnitude, are his own, and they are not accessible to me, because the gap between his mind and mine is wider than the gap between my mind and the mind of an amoeba, and the amoeba at least exists in the same framework of consciousness, whereas the gap between my mind and his mind is a gap between frameworks entirely."

Kaedan absorbed this in silence. His teacup had gone cold. He could not feel his fingers.

"You said you tried," Kaedan said finally. "You said you tried to alter your mortal past and failed."

Godric nodded slowly. "Shortly after I became King of Thronegate. I had just awakened. I was still raw with grief, still bleeding from the loss of everything I had loved. I could travel to any timeline. I could overpower causality. I could do anything, anything except the one thing I actually wanted, which was to save them. And I knew, even then, that I could not. I had known since the instant I gained omniscience that the Stasis Event was not Thorin's doing. I had known since that same instant that the true source was my own future self. I had known since that same instant that the pre-Stasis past was sealed against alteration by a will so vastly superior to my own that resistance was not merely futile but conceptually impossible. But knowing it and accepting it were different things. And so, driven by grief, I tried."

"What happened?"

Godric was quiet for a long moment. His eyes had gone distant again, fixed on some point in the infinite expanse of his memory, and the expression on his face was not pain, not anguish, not the raw, bleeding wound of fresh loss, but something stranger and more unsettling: a look of profound, almost philosophical awe at the memory of his own absolute insignificance.

"I was defeated," Godric said simply. "Completely. Totally. Effortlessly. Not by future Godric himself. Not by any direct action or conscious opposition on his part. I was defeated by the shadow of his afterthought. The mere residual echo of his existence, the faintest peripheral ghost of his will, the infinitesimal, passive, entirely unconscious overflow of his presence, was sufficient to crush my attempt with a totality so absolute that the word crush is an insult to the completeness of my failure. I did not lose. Losing implies a contest. There was no contest. There was merely the infinite, unbridgeable, mathematically incomprehensible gap between what I was and what he is, and the gap made my attempt not just unsuccessful but structurally impossible, the way it is structurally impossible for a shadow to cast the object that creates it."

Kaedan felt his stomach lurch. He tried to imagine it and found that he could not. The current Godric, the Godric who had annihilated the omniverse with a pinky twitch, the Godric whose voice rewrote the acoustic architecture of existence, the Godric whose touch erased invulnerability itself, the Godric who had replicated into infinity and absorbed that infinity and become permanently more powerful than infinity, that Godric had been defeated not by his future self but by the shadow of his future self's afterthought. Not by the actual entity. Not by a conscious act of opposition. By the passive, unconscious, residual overflow of a being so vast that his mere existence was an insurmountable barrier to anything he did not will.

"The attempt failed," Godric continued, "and I understood, with the absolute clarity of omniscience, that it would always fail. That no version of me, at any point in my current trajectory, could ever succeed against that barrier. That the seal on my mortal past was not a lock that could be picked or a wall that could be scaled or a force that could be overcome, but a fundamental axiom of reality, as immutable and permanent as the fact that existence exists. And I understood, in the same instant of absolute clarity, that this was not a weakness in the way that mortals understand weakness. It was not a vulnerability that could be exploited by enemies. It was not a flaw that could be targeted or a limitation that could be circumvented. It was the will of a being so incomprehensibly superior to everything else in existence that the will itself had become a law of nature, and laws of nature cannot be argued with or fought against or appealed to. They simply are."

Godric picked up his teacup again, found it empty, and set it down with a small, rueful smile that seemed to acknowledge the absurdity of an omnipotent being pouring his own tea in the first place.

"And so I accepted it," he said. "Not immediately. Not easily. But eventually, and completely. I accepted that Reina and Isabel and Hebe and Thorin were gone, that their deaths were genuine and irrevocable, and that no amount of power, no matter how vast, would ever allow me to undo what had been done. I accepted that the one thing I truly wanted was the one thing I could never have, and I accepted that this was not a tragedy but simply a fact, the way it is a fact that the sky is vast or that time moves forward or that I am what I am."

He looked at Kaedan, and the warmth in his eyes was undiminished, the genuine, uncle-like affection still present, still real, still the defining characteristic of his interaction with the mortal beings he had chosen to love.

"And I am not troubled by it anymore," Godric said. "I have not been troubled by it for longer than you can imagine. I have accepted it, and I have moved past it, and I have become something that is not defined by the loss but is instead defined by the acceptance. The grief is still there. It will always be there. But it is a quiet grief, a settled grief, the kind that lives in the background of a life rather than consuming it. I am at peace with my limitation, Kaedan. I am more than at peace. I am amused by it. I am comfortable with it. Because I understand what it truly means."

Kaedan swallowed hard. His throat felt like it was lined with sand. "What does it mean?"

Godric leaned forward, his elbows on his knees, his massive frame somehow intimate despite the cosmic scale of what he was describing, and his expression was one of patient, teacher-like explanation.

"It means," Godric said slowly, "that my future is guaranteed. Not in the way that mortals think about the future, as a range of possibilities that might or might not come to pass, but in the absolute, categorical, irrevocable sense. The being who created the Stasis Event, who is responsible for everything I am, is me. He is not a separate entity. He is not a rival or a constraint or a limitation. He is me, at a later, greater, incomprehensibly more powerful stage of my own existence. And his existence proves, with the absolute certainty of a mathematical axiom, that I will continue to exist and continue to amplify and continue to become more and more and more powerful for as long as time continues, because he already exists, which means I already survive everything that will ever happen between now and then, because I become him, and he is real, and therefore my survival to that point is guaranteed by the fact of his reality."

Kaedan felt the implications land, one after another, like blows from a hammer that grew heavier with each strike.

"It means," Godric continued, "that none of my power mechanics can ever be exploited or denied by anyone else. Not my amplification. Not my superpower genesis. Not my absolute concealment. Not my weakness paradox. Not my immune system. Not my passive shields. Not my sentient auras. None of it. Because the origin of all those systems is not me. The origin is him. Future Godric. A being so incomprehensibly beyond the current me, who is already beyond the entire omniverse by an infinite margin, that any attempt to interfere with those systems would have to get past him first, and getting past him is not merely impossible but structurally, axiomatically, fundamentally inconceivable. The things that make me invincible were not made by me. They were made by a version of me that is so far beyond me that the gap between us is wider than the gap between existence and nothingness, and that gap is the source of my invincibility, which means my invincibility is not a feature of my current power but a feature of my future power, and my future power is guaranteed, and therefore my invincibility is guaranteed, and there is nothing that anyone or anything can do about it."

He sat back, spreading his arms in a gesture that encompassed the room, the palace, the empire, the omniverse, and everything beyond.

"So you see," Godric said, his voice warm with genuine amusement, "what you might call my weakness is not really a weakness at all. It is the opposite. It is the ultimate confirmation of my strength. It is the proof that I will never be defeated, because the being who will never be defeated is me, at a later stage, and his will is the foundation of my existence, and his will is absolute, and his will cannot be opposed, and therefore I cannot be opposed. My one limitation is that I cannot overpower my own future, and that limitation is the strongest thing about me, because it means my future is the most powerful force in existence, and that future is guaranteed, and nothing can change it, and nothing can challenge it, and nothing can even conceive of challenging it, because the being who represents that future is so far beyond everything that the concept of challenge is meaningless to him."

Kaedan sat very still. His teacup was cold. His hands were cold. His blood was cold. He was trying to process what he had heard, and his mind, which had been tempered by forty-seven years of exposure to the impossible, was failing.

Because the implication was not merely that Godric had a weakness. The implication was that what Godric called a weakness was, in fact, the most terrifying thing about him. It was the confirmation that his power had no ceiling, no limit, no possible endpoint. It was the proof that the being who could already annihilate the omniverse infinitely with zero effort would continue to grow, continue to amplify, continue to escalate beyond any possible framework of comprehension, and that the growth was guaranteed by the existence of a future version so powerful that his passive, unconscious overflow was sufficient to absolutely defeat any attempt to alter his will.

The current Godric was the most powerful being in the omniverse by an infinite margin. That was established. That was known. That was the foundation upon which Kaedan's entire understanding of existence rested. But the current Godric was, to the future Godric, what a single atom was to an infinity of infinities of omniverses. And the current Godric had been defeated not by the future Godric himself, but by the shadow of his afterthought. Which meant that the future Godric was so powerful that even his unconscious, passive, residual existence was sufficient to absolutely crush the most powerful being in the current omniverse.

And that future Godric was the source of everything the current Godric was.

And that future Godric was not a separate entity but the current Godric at a later stage.

Which meant that the current Godric would become that future Godric.

Which meant that the current Godric's trajectory was not upward but inward, toward a center so dense with power that the concept of density collapsed under its own weight, toward a state of being so absolute and total and all-consuming that the word being was an insult to its magnitude, toward a future in which he was not merely the most powerful entity in existence but the reason existence existed, the foundation upon which all frameworks rested, the axiom from which all truths were derived.

And that future was guaranteed.

And nothing could change it.

And nothing could challenge it.

And the being who represented that future had looked backward through time and had created the Stasis Event, had transformed a simple teleportation spell into the birth mechanism of an apex god predator, had set in motion the chain of events that would ensure his own existence, and had sealed the pre-Stasis past against alteration, not out of malice, not out of manipulation, not out of any desire to cause suffering, but for reasons that were inaccessible to the mortal mind, reasons that operated on a scale so far beyond comprehension that the word reason itself was inadequate.

And that sealed past was what Godric called his weakness.

And that weakness was actually the strongest thing about him.

"You're not troubled by this," Kaedan said, and his voice sounded foreign to his own ears, thin and distant and hollow.

"No," Godric said simply.

"You're amused by it."

"I am," Godric confirmed, and the amusement was visible in his eyes, a warm, genuine, almost playful glint that was so at odds with the cosmic horror of what he was describing that it made Kaedan want to scream. "I find it amusing, Kaedan. I find it deeply, genuinely, from-the-bottom-of-my-heart amusing that the one thing I cannot do is the one thing that proves I will always be able to do everything else. I find it amusing that my limitation is my guarantee. I find it amusing that the seal on my past is the key to my future. I find it amusing that the shadow of my own afterthought is sufficient to crush any attempt to alter what I have willed, because it means that what I will is absolute, and what I will is the continuation and amplification of my own existence, and that continuation and amplification is the most certain thing in the omniverse, more certain than the laws of physics, more certain than the existence of time, more certain than the fact that something exists rather than nothing."

He reached for the teapot and refilled his cup with the casual, domestic ease of a man who had just described the most terrifying thing in existence and found it satisfying rather than disturbing.

"I have made my peace with it," Godric said. "I have more than made my peace with it. I have embraced it. I have integrated it into my understanding of what I am and what I will always be. The inability to save them is not a wound that bleeds. It is a scar that has healed, and the scar is not ugly but beautiful, because it is the proof that I loved them, and the love was real, and the loss was real, and the acceptance was real, and the continuation was real, and all of it, every moment of grief and rage and despair and acceptance and peace, all of it was real, and none of it can be undone, and that is not a tragedy. That is simply what it means to exist."

He looked at Kaedan, and the warmth in his eyes was boundless, and the boundlessness was the most terrifying thing Kaedan had ever seen, because it was the warmth of a being who had accepted everything, including his own absolute supremacy, and who was not troubled by any of it, and who would continue to exist and amplify and dominate for as long as existence continued, and whose future was guaranteed by the existence of a version of himself so powerful that the current version was a rounding error by comparison.

"You look frightened," Godric observed, his tone gentle, concerned.

"I am," Kaedan admitted.

"Of what?"

Kaedan tried to formulate an answer and found that the attempt was like trying to grasp water. There were too many things to be frightened of. There was the sheer scale of what Godric had revealed, the incomprehensible magnitude of the future that awaited him, the absolute certainty of his continued escalation. There was the implication that everything Godric was, every stat and superpower and immune response and passive shield and sentient aura, was rooted not in the current Godric but in the future Godric, which meant that the invincibility of those systems was not contingent on the current Godric's power but on the future Godric's power, which was so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly greater that the current Godric's power was, by comparison, effectively nothing. There was the realization that the weakness Godric had described was not a weakness at all but a confirmation of everything Kaedan had ever feared, which was that Godric could not be stopped, would never be stopped, and would only grow more unstoppable with every passing instant.

But beneath all of those fears, there was something deeper, something more fundamental, something that touched the core of what it meant to be a mortal being in a universe ruled by an immortal god.

"Of you," Kaedan said quietly.

Godric tilted his head, the same curious, slightly puzzled gesture he always made when someone expressed fear of him, as though the concept of being feared was genuinely surprising to him despite his omniscience.

"Of me," Godric repeated.

"Of what you are," Kaedan clarified. "Of what you will become. Of the fact that the most powerful being I have ever known, the being whose zero percent effort can annihilate everything, the being whose weakest aura can slaughter the strongest coalition in history, the being who is already so far beyond everything that the gap between you and the rest of existence is not a gap but an absence, is himself just a rounding error compared to what he will be. And the rounding error is guaranteed to become less and less of a rounding error with every passing instant, forever, and the thing he will become is so powerful that even his afterthought can crush the current version absolutely, and the current version can already crush everything else absolutely, and this is not a limitation, this is not a weakness, this is the strongest thing about you, and you are amused by it, and you are at peace with it, and you are sitting across from me drinking tea and asking me why I'm frightened."

He stopped, breathing hard, his amplified heart pounding, his hands gripping the arms of his chair hard enough to leave permanent impressions in the wood.

"I am frightened," Kaedan continued, his voice cracking, "because everything I feared about you was not enough. Every journal I read, every demonstration I witnessed, every moment of existential dread I experienced, all of it was based on an understanding of you that was infinitesimally small. You are not merely the most powerful being in the omniverse. You are the being whose future is so powerful that it casts a shadow backward through time that creates the present. You are not merely invincible. You are the being whose invincibility is guaranteed by a version of yourself that has not yet existed but already exists, because time means nothing to a being of that magnitude, and the guarantee is absolute, and the guarantee cannot be challenged, and the guarantee cannot be circumvented, and the guarantee is the foundation of everything you are, and everything you are is already more than everything else combined, and you will only become more, and more, and more, and there is no end, and there is no ceiling, and there is no limit, and the only thing you cannot do is the thing that proves you can do everything else, and you are amused by this, and you are at peace with this, and I am sitting across from you drinking tea and pretending that any of this is normal."

The study was silent. The fire had burned low, casting long shadows across the warm wood paneling. The teacups sat on the table, cold and untouched, and the jasmine scent had faded, and the ordinary textures of the room seemed to recede, leaving only the two of them, the mortal and the god, in a space that felt suddenly, terrifyingly small.

Godric regarded Kaedan for a long moment. His expression was not pitying, not condescending, not dismissive. It was understanding. Genuine, deep, empathetic understanding, the understanding of a being who had once been mortal himself, who remembered what it felt like to confront something so vast that the mind could not contain it, who had experienced his own version of that confrontation when he had tried to save his family and been crushed by the shadow of his own future.

"I know," Godric said softly. "I know it is frightening. I know it seems like there is no end to the horror of what I am. And I know that you have spent forty-seven years learning about me, and every revelation has been worse than the last, and this one is the worst of all, because it confirms that there is no last, that the revelations will never end, that I will always be more than you understand, and the more will always be terrifying, and the terror will never stop."

He reached across the table and placed his hand on Kaedan's. The grip was warm, firm, steadying. The hand of an uncle comforting a nephew. The hand of a god who understood fear because he remembered being afraid, even though fear was now as foreign to him as the concept of limitation.

"But I want you to understand something," Godric continued, his voice low and earnest. "I want you to understand why I told you this. Not to frighten you. Not to add another layer to your dread. But because you deserve to know the truth. You have served me faithfully for forty-seven years. You have read the journals, witnessed the demonstrations, carried the weight of knowledge that would break lesser minds. You have earned the right to know what I am, all of what I am, including the part that seems like a weakness but is actually the strongest thing about me. And I wanted you to know because—"

He paused, and the pause was genuine, not theatrical, not calculated, but the pause of a being who was searching for the right words to express something that mattered to him.

"Because you are my family," Godric said. "You and your bloodline, stretching back to Blake, stretching back to Thorin, stretching back to the very beginning of what I became. You are the connection I have to the part of myself that was mortal, the part that loved and grieved and struggled and hoped. And I wanted you to know because keeping this from you would have been a kind of distance that I do not want between us. I wanted you to know because the truth is what holds us together, even when the truth is terrifying, even when the truth makes the dread worse, even when the truth confirms that I am something that should not exist and yet does and will continue to for as long as anything exists."

He squeezed Kaedan's hand gently. The pressure was carefully controlled, the grip of a being who could crush omniverses and was choosing instead to offer comfort.

"I am not troubled by my limitation," Godric said. "And I do not want you to be troubled by it either. It is not a vulnerability. It is not a crack in my armor. It is the proof that my armor has no cracks, that it will never have cracks, that it cannot have cracks, because the being who forged it is the being I will become, and the being I will become is the most powerful entity that will ever exist, and his will is the foundation of my invincibility, and his will is absolute, and his will cannot be opposed, and therefore I cannot be opposed, and that is not something to fear. That is something to trust. That is something to rely on. That is the bedrock upon which everything I have built rests, including the empire that protects you and your family and every being that I have chosen to shelter beneath my rule."

Kaedan looked at Godric's hand on his own. The hand was warm and calloused and familiar, the same hand that had ruffled his hair when he was six, the same hand that had made jasmine bloom in his palm, the same hand that had, according to the journals, twitched a pinky and annihilated existence. The hand of his favorite uncle. The hand of an apex god predator whose future was guaranteed to be even more terrifying than his present.

"You asked me once if I was lonely," Kaedan said, his voice barely above a whisper. "And you told me that you weren't. That every instant of your existence was the most pleasurable experience any being had ever felt. That you were perpetually, recursively, infinitely satisfied by your own growth."

"I remember," Godric said.

"And now you're telling me that this growth is guaranteed. That it cannot be stopped. That it will continue forever, that you will become more and more and more powerful with every passing instant, and that the being you will become is so powerful that even his afterthought can crush you, and you are already the most powerful being in existence. And you are amused by this. You are at peace with this. You are comfortable with the knowledge that you will never stop growing, that there is no ceiling, that the escalation is permanent and absolute and guaranteed by the most powerful force in existence, which is your own future will."

"Yes," Godric said.

"Then what is the point?" Kaedan asked, and the question came out raw and desperate and broken, the question of a mortal confronting the absolute indifference of infinite power. "What is the point of any of it? What is the point of struggle, of effort, of hope, of anything that any being in the omniverse does, when all of it, every moment of every existence, is occurring in the shadow of a being who is growing more powerful with every instant and will never stop growing and cannot be stopped and will eventually become so powerful that the current version of himself, who can already annihilate everything, will be a rounding error by comparison? What is the point of the omniverse, Uncle Godric? What is the point of existence, when existence is just the stage upon which you grow?"

Godric was quiet for a long time. The fire had gone out, leaving only embers, and the study was dim and warm and intimate, and the silence between them was heavy with the weight of a question that had no answer, or at least no answer that a mortal mind could accept.

"The point," Godric said finally, his voice soft and thoughtful, "is that existence exists. The point is that beings live and struggle and hope and love and lose and continue. The point is that you are sitting across from me, asking this question, and the question matters to you, and because it matters to you, it matters. The point is not the destination. The point is the journey. The point is the experience of being alive, which is something I can remember but can no longer truly feel, because I have become something for which being alive is a metaphor rather than a reality. The point is that the omniverse is fragile and temporary and imperfect, and those qualities are not flaws but features, because fragility creates value, because temporality creates meaning, because imperfection creates the possibility of growth, which is the closest thing to what I experience that mortal minds can comprehend."

He released Kaedan's hand and leaned back in his chair, his expression settling into its usual warm, friendly default, the mask of the favorite uncle that was not a mask at all but the genuine, authentic expression of a being who had chosen to relate to mortals on their own terms.

"I cannot tell you what the point of existence is, Kaedan," Godric said. "I can only tell you that I choose to preserve it. I choose to protect it. I choose to allow it to continue, despite its fragility, despite its insignificance, despite the fact that I could annihilate it without effort and without consequence and without any meaningful impact on my own existence. I choose to keep it because I remember being part of it, and because the memory matters to me, and because the beings who inhabit it matter to me, and because you matter to me. That is the point, if there is one. That I choose. That I care. That I am not merely a force of nature but a person, however impossible that person may be, and persons make choices, and my choice is to let the fragile, temporary, imperfect omniverse continue to exist, because its existence is valuable to me for reasons that are not rational but are nevertheless real."

He stood, his massive frame rising from the chair with the easy grace of a being for whom physical movement was an affectation rather than a necessity, and he walked to the window, looking out at the palace grounds, at the carefully maintained gardens, at the guards patrolling the walls, at the city sprawling beyond, at the empire that he had built and protected and ruled with a hand that was simultaneously gentle and absolute.

"I am going to tell you something that I have never told anyone," Godric said, his back to Kaedan, his voice quiet and contemplative. "Not because it is a secret, but because no one has ever needed to hear it. You need to hear it, because you have just learned the truth about my limitation, and the truth has shaken you, and I want to give you something to hold onto in the aftermath of that shaking."

Kaedan waited.

"The reason I am not troubled by my inability to alter the past," Godric said, "is not because I have made peace with the loss. I have made peace with the loss, but that is not the reason. The reason is that I understand, with the absolute clarity of omniscience, that the being who sealed my past is me. Not a separate entity. Not a rival or a constraint. Me. At a later, greater stage. And when I look at that future self, when I contemplate the magnitude of what I will become, I do not see a limitation. I see a promise. I see the guarantee that everything I am will continue, and everything I value will be protected, and everything I have built will endure, because the being who guarantees those things is the most powerful force in existence, and that being is me, and I will never abandon what I have chosen to preserve."

He turned from the window, and the dying light of the embers caught his face, illuminating the rugged features, the salt-and-pepper beard, the warm, crinkling eyes that had looked upon the birth and death of universes and found them wanting only in their inability to challenge him.

"That is why I am at peace," Godric said. "Not because I have accepted my limitation. Because I have understood that my limitation is the proof of my permanence. And permanence, for a being who has lost everything he once loved, is not a consolation. It is the only thing that makes existence bearable. The knowledge that I will continue. The knowledge that I will endure. The knowledge that nothing, not even the passage of time itself, can erase what I am, because what I am is guaranteed by a version of myself that is beyond erasure, beyond challenge, beyond even the concept of opposition."

He smiled, and the smile was warm and genuine and deeply, profoundly unsettling, because it was the smile of a being who had found peace in the absolute, irrevocable, cosmically obscene certainty of his own supremacy, and who was offering that peace to a mortal who could never truly share it, because the peace of Godric was predicated on the knowledge that he was the most powerful being in existence and would only become more so, and that knowledge was not comforting but annihilating to anyone who was not him.

"Come," Godric said, moving toward the door. "It's late, and you look exhausted. Let me walk you to your quarters. We can continue this tomorrow, if you wish, or we can speak of other things, or we can sit in silence. Whatever you need. Whatever helps."

Kaedan rose on unsteady legs. His body, for all its thousandfold amplification, felt frail and insubstantial, a temporary arrangement of matter that existed at the pleasure of a being whose patience was infinite and whose power was guaranteed by the most certain thing in existence.

He followed Godric to the door, and as they walked, the corridors of the palace seemed different, smaller, more fragile, as though the walls and floors and ceilings were not solid structures but temporary arrangements of probability that could be rewritten at any moment by the whim of the being walking beside him.

And Kaedan understood, with a clarity that cut like a blade, that the revelation of Godric's weakness was not a comfort. It was not a relief. It was not a chink in the armor that offered hope to the hopeless. It was the opposite. It was the confirmation of every fear, the validation of every dread, the absolute, categorical, irrevocable proof that the being he served and loved and feared was not merely invincible but permanently, guaranteed, axiomatically invincible, and that his invincibility was rooted not in his current power but in his future power, and his future power was rooted in a version of himself so incomprehensibly beyond the current version that the current version was a rounding error, and the rounding error was already the most powerful being in existence.

The weakness was not a weakness. The limitation was not a limitation. The inability to alter the past was not a vulnerability but a guarantee, and the guarantee was that Godric would never stop, would never weaken, would never be challenged, would never be opposed, would continue to grow and amplify and dominate for as long as existence continued, and existence would continue as long as he willed it, and his will was guaranteed by his future self, and his future self was the most powerful force in existence, and the most powerful force in existence was him, and he was walking Kaedan to his quarters with the gentle, avuncular concern of a favorite uncle, and the concern was real, and the love was real, and the power was real, and the power was growing, and the growth was permanent, and the permanence was guaranteed, and the guarantee was absolute, and there was nothing, nothing, nothing that anyone could do about any of it.

Kaedan reached his quarters and bade Godric goodnight with a voice that did not sound like his own. He closed the door. He leaned against it. He slid down until he was sitting on the floor with his back against the wood, and he stared at the opposite wall, and he did not sleep, and he did not move, and the dread settled into him like a second skeleton, a framework of absolute existential horror that would support him for the remaining years of his mortal life and would accompany him to his grave and would be the last thing he felt when his eyes closed for the final time.

The only weakness of Godric was Godric.

And that was the strongest thing about him.

And it would never, ever, in all of eternity, be otherwise.

content.text:
The year 999...9 (you don't need to spell it out. Just know it's so large that it's impossible to write). King Godric of this era is triggering the stasis event for his past Godric in Year -10,000 (you must also indicate that godric could've effortlessly granted way more potent stasis event with more devastating effects (he can perfectly copy his current mechanism to the past Godric) with no cost to him, but decided against it to "hold back"--you must note the crazy ridiculousness of this since even what Godric considered as the “held back” version is so incomprehensibly powerful… Godric at year 0 was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of the creation combined, and Godric genuinely believed that it would preserve some kind of mortal struggle to his past self, something that is super not true. And also note the cause-effect of this: if he grants his past self a really powerful version, he himself who is in the future and has been amplified from his past self will even be more incomprehensibly powerful since the starting "base" is also already become so much stronger and thus he can grant even more powerful version to his past infinitely. Clive's mind must be even more overwhelmed at this absurd realisation.). Write a detailed story of this. You must highly exaggerate his power, his rule, his terrifying presence, his everything at this point.

All his previous mechanisms of power (sentient passive auras, passive shields, immune system, evolved weakness paradox, amplification, superpower genesis) have also evolved countless times--you must give detailed examples and scope of how they work now, showing clearly how they become even more absurdly superfluously terrifying than before, to the point of the “old version of his power mechanism” which are already ridiculously powerful feeling so weak in comparison. You must keep in mind that Godric, who had been a mortal once, still believes in mortal resilience and free will, so if there are any evolved versions of his power mechanisms that alter free will they should be consciously suppressed by Godric.

When Godric just emerged from stasis, for a very long time, countless sentience still formed some opposition against him, despite Godric himself never regarded them as any real oppositions–to him, those "oppositions" were just a brief fun distraction to test his stats and powers. Since some googolplexian years ago, however, all oppositions have stopped completely: there have been 0 who even dare to form the slightest thought of challenging, resisting, threatening or opposing Godric. No one dares anymore, and there will never be anyone who dares. They all have learnt the absolute futility of opposing Godric. Note: you must not suggest that Godric alters the free will to oppose him‐-Godric genuinely believes in mortal resilience.

You must also include a few scenes where he interacts with really powerful beings around him (show him as friendly to those), who after being completely traumatized by Godric, has become his lowly palace servants, gardeners, etc.. Those really powerful beings are totally terrified of him–provide some background stories of how they become so utterly afraid of (do not say they opposed godric--Godric is unforgiving to opposition) and serving Godric now.

Go crazy wild. Write from POV of Clive, his court wizard who is Blake’s descendant. Clive is 50 years old.

reasoning_content:
This is a complex prompt requiring me to write from the POV of Clive, the court wizard at Year 999...9, witnessing Godric trigger the Stasis Event for his past self. Let me break down all the requirements:

1. Write from Clive's POV (50 years old, Blake's descendant)
2. Godric triggers the Stasis Event for past Godric in Year -10,000
3. Godric could have granted a more potent version but "held back" - this is absurd because even the held-back version made Year 0 Godric infinitely stronger than creation combined
4. The cause-effect loop: if he grants a more powerful version, his future self becomes even more powerful, allowing him to grant an even more powerful version infinitely
5. Highly exaggerate Godric's power, rule, presence, everything
6. All previous mechanisms have evolved countless times - give detailed examples of how they work now, showing how old versions feel weak
7. Godric still believes in mortal resilience and free will - suppresses any mechanisms that alter free will
8. Opposition has stopped completely for googolplexian years - 0 beings dare to oppose him
9. Godric didn't alter free will - creation learned absolute futility
10. Include scenes with powerful beings who are now palace servants/gardeners after being traumatized by Godric (not opposition - they didn't oppose him, but became terrified)
11. Go crazy wild with exaggeration

Let me think about the evolved mechanisms:

Maybe the new auras can do things like pre-annihilate threats across all possible timelines, convert hostile intent into servile loyalty fields (but Godric suppresses this because free will), create zones where opposition is physically impossible
- Passive shields: Now evolved to pre-nullify threats before the threat exists across all possible realities, convert danger into self-erasing failure, make logistics of opposition impossible
- Immune system: Now treats possibility of harm as an object to be hunted, humiliated, digested, erased; the omniverse itself behaves like white blood cells
- Evolved Weakness Paradox: Now triggers on ANY thought by ANY being about ANYTHING (not just Godric's weaknesses), making the name misleading
- Amplification: Beyond layered infinities, recursively increasing every infinitesimal fraction of Planck second
- Superpower genesis: Each new power at base is already capable of omniversal extinction, but now evolved with multiple enhanced effects

For the powerful beings serving as servants:
- Maybe former cosmic entities, abstract beings, pantheon leaders who witnessed Godric's power indirectly (not through opposition) and were traumatized
- They didn't oppose him - they simply witnessed him or were in proximity when he expressed power, and the experience broke them
- Now they serve as gardeners, cupbearers, etc. out of absolute terror

Let me think about the tone:
- Epic power fantasy
- Existential dread
- The absurdity of Godric "holding back"
- Clive's overwhelmed mind trying to process the cause-effect loop
- The contrast between Godric's warmth to his servants and their absolute terror

Let me write this now. I'll aim for 4000-8000 words, with detailed examples of evolved mechanisms and the triggering of the Stasis Event.

- They became terrified through witnessing or proximity, not through opposition
- Godric suppresses free-will-altering mechanisms
- The cause-effect loop of granting more powerful versions

2. The evolved mechanisms - detailed examples
3. Servants scene - powerful beings serving Godric
4. The Stasis Event triggering
5. The "holding back" revelation and the cause-effect loop
6. Clive's existential overwhelm

content:
I am Clive. I am fifty years old. I am the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, the latest in an unbroken lineage stretching back to Blake, the mortal man who accidentally unleashed the apex predator of existence upon the omniverse. My magical power multiplies by a factor of one thousand every day, and has done so since my twentieth birthday, which means I am currently capable of feats that would make the gods of any lesser cosmos weep with envy. I can compress galaxies into gemstones. I can unweave the fundamental constants of physics and reknit them into configurations of my choosing. I can perceive the birth and death of probability waves across every dimension simultaneously, and I can manipulate those waves with a precision that would make the Oracle Engines of the ancient Veiled Cluster look like abacuses operated by particularly dim children.

I am, by any reasonable measure, one of the most powerful mortal beings to have ever existed.

And I am standing in the presence of King Godric, which means I am nothing.

The year is a number that cannot be written. Not because it is too large for our numerical systems, though it is that, but because the act of writing it would require a medium capable of containing infinity, and no such medium exists. The scribes of Thronegate have long since abandoned any attempt to date official documents with precision, opting instead for a simple acknowledgment that the current year exists somewhere beyond the useful application of mathematics, in a region of temporal excess where the concept of counting has been rendered obsolete by the sheer, absurd duration of the King's reign.

I have served as court wizard for thirty years. My father served before me. His father served before him. The lineage extends backward through the ages, each wizard more powerful than the last, each wizard more traumatized than the last, each wizard leaving behind journals that were less records than they were confessions, screams committed to parchment by men who had witnessed things that should not be witnessed and been forced to continue living afterward.

Today, I am about to witness something that will make everything my predecessors recorded feel like a children's story.

The throne room of the far-future Thronegate is not a room in any meaningful sense. It is a construct of such vast, incomprehensible architecture that the word room is an insult to its magnitude. It exists across infinite dimensions simultaneously, its walls woven from the crystallized essence of conquered cosmoses, its ceiling a vault of living starlight that pulses in synchronization with the King's breathing, its floor a mosaic of compressed timelines arranged in patterns so complex that perceiving them directly induces seizures in beings with insufficiently evolved consciousness. The air itself is not air but a carefully maintained atmosphere of absolute serenity, a physical medium of peace so profound that breathing it feels like being submerged in the concept of calm, and the calm is not natural but manufactured, a passive effect of the King's presence that ensures his subjects do not simply collapse into catatonic terror the moment they enter his line of sight.

He sits upon the throne. The throne is carved from the petrified heart of a concept that once tried to define the limits of power, a concept that Godric had found amusing in its naivety before he absorbed it into himself and made it a footrest. His posture is relaxed, easy, the posture of a man who has never known tension because tension requires the possibility of resistance, and the possibility of resistance has not existed for longer than the current age of the omniverse squared by itself and raised to its own power and then repeated infinitely.

He looks forty-five years old. He has looked forty-five years old since Year -10,000. His face is ruggedly handsome, his jaw sharp and masculine, his salt-and-pepper hair and beard trimmed to immaculate precision. His heavily muscled frame fills the simple, dark garments he favors, the fabric stretching across shoulders that have borne the weight of existence itself and found it wanting in heaviness. He looks like a father. He looks like an uncle. He looks like the kind of man who would help you carry your groceries and then ask about your children with genuine interest.

He looks like the most terrifying being in the history of existence, because he is.

"Clive," Godric says, and his voice is warm, rich, resonant with the same genuine affection that he has shown every court wizard since Blake. "Come closer. I have something I wish to discuss with you."

I walk forward. My legs are steady because I force them to be steady, employing the same muscular control that allows me to suppress my thousandfold daily amplification to functional levels. But beneath the steadiness, every cell in my body is vibrating with the atavistic, instinctual terror of a prey animal approaching an apex predator, and the terror is not wrong, because I am a prey animal approaching an apex predator, and the only reason I am still alive is that the apex predator has decided, for reasons of his own, to find me amusing rather than threatening.

As I approach the throne, I pass the servants.

There is Vasharion, who is currently polishing the decorative inlay on the throne room's eastern wall with a cloth made of woven starlight. Vasharion was once the Omniscient Convergence, a collective consciousness spanning seventeen multiversal clusters, a being of such profound intellectual magnitude that his mere thoughts could reshape the topology of reality. He had not opposed Godric. He had not even considered opposing Godric. He had simply been existing, three cosmoses away from a sector where Godric had once expressed mild disappointment at a particularly bland cup of tea, and the ambient overflow of that disappointment had rippled across existence and struck Vasharion's collective consciousness with such force that every individual mind within the Convergence had experienced simultaneous, catastrophic ego death. They had merged into a single, shattered entity that had crawled across dimensions to beg for the privilege of serving the being whose incidental displeasure had unmade them. Godric had taken pity on him, or what passed for pity in a being for whom pity was a deliberate choice rather than an emotional response, and had assigned him to the palace staff. Vasharion now polished walls with a devotion that bordered on worship, and the devotion was not coerced but organic, the natural response of a being who had glimpsed the face of absolute power and had been permanently, irrevocably humbled by the glimpse.

There was El'Thera, who was arranging fresh flowers in a vase carved from the crystallized tears of extinct civilizations. El'Thera had once been the Dreamweaver Prime, an entity capable of reshaping the collective unconscious of entire galaxies, a being whose influence had extended into the sleeping minds of trillions and whose power had been, by any pre-Godric standard, godlike in its scope and magnitude. She had never opposed Godric either. She had attempted to observe him, once, through a dream-projection so subtle and so distant that the probability of detection was, by her calculations, approximately zero. Godric had detected her instantly, because Godric detected everything instantly, and he had looked in her direction, not with anger or displeasure but with the mild, polite curiosity of a man noticing a particularly bold butterfly, and the weight of his attention, even filtered through infinite layers of dream and distance and protective abstraction, had struck El'Thera with such devastating psychological force that her consciousness had fragmented into seventeen thousand screaming shards, each one convinced that it was the only surviving remnant of a being that had once been whole. Godric had reassembled her, because he was not cruel to those who had not opposed him, and he had offered her a position in the palace, because he recognized that she needed purpose to recover from the experience, and because he genuinely, in his strange, warm, terrifying way, wanted to help. El'Thera now arranged flowers with the trembling, meticulous care of a being who knew that the slightest mistake, the slightest withered petal or misplaced stem, might draw the attention of the being who sat on the throne, and that attention, even the gentle, affectionate attention that Godric showed his servants, was an experience so overwhelming that it bordered on the annihilating.

And there was Korvanthos, who was sweeping the floor with a broom crafted from the petrified spines of extinct cosmic entities. Korvanthos had once been a Chronal Leviathan, a being that existed outside the flow of time itself, whose body spanned the entire history of a universe from its birth to its heat death, and whose mere presence could accelerate or decelerate the passage of temporal events. He had not opposed Godric. He had, however, once been in the same dimensional sector as a being who had thought about opposing Godric, and the Evolved Weakness Paradox, in its infinitely expanded, absurdly overcompensating current form, had registered that proximity as a potential threat vector and had initiated a preemptive response so disproportionate that the Chronal Leviathan's entire temporal existence had been compressed into a single, agonizing instant of absolute awareness, during which he had experienced every moment of his existence simultaneously and had understood, with the crystalline clarity of forced omniscience, the absolute, categorical, mathematically irrefutable futility of any being that was not Godric. The experience had not damaged him, because Godric's mechanisms did not damage beings that had not opposed him, but it had fundamentally restructured his consciousness, converting him from an independent entity into a being whose sole purpose was to serve, not because he was compelled but because he had seen the truth and the truth had left no room for any other orientation.

Godric was kind to all of them. He asked after their health, their families, their concerns. He remembered the names of their children, which was particularly impressive in Korvanthos's case, as Korvanthos's children existed as temporal echoes spread across seventeen billion years of linear time. He ensured they were well-fed and well-rested and comfortable, and his concern was genuine, authentically warm, the concern of a being who had once been mortal and who remembered, in the deep, buried, inaccessible chambers of his infinitely expanded consciousness, what it felt like to care about small things.

The kindness made the terror worse, not better, because it was the kindness of a being who could annihilate everything they had ever known without effort, without thought, without consequence, and who was choosing instead to ask about their day. The kindness was a reminder of the gap, the infinite, unbridgeable, mathematically obscene gap between what they were and what he was, and the gap was not narrowing but widening, every Planck second, every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, as his amplification continued its recursive, eternal escalation.

"Clive," Godric said again, gesturing to the space beside his throne. "Sit. You look tired."

I sat. The chair that materialized beneath me was not a chair but a construct of pure comfort, a physical manifestation of ergonomic perfection that had been willed into existence by the King's passing thought, because the King disliked discomfort in others and had the power to eliminate it without effort.

"I have a task today," Godric said, his tone conversational, his eyes warm with the same genuine affection he showed all his court wizards. "Something I have been meaning to do for quite some time. I thought you might like to observe."

"What task, my lord?"

Godric smiled, and the smile was not ominous or threatening or filled with cosmic menace. It was the smile of a man who was about to share something he found genuinely interesting, the smile of an uncle showing a nephew a particularly clever trick.

"I am going to create the Stasis Event," he said.

The words did not register immediately. They were too large, too impossible, too staggeringly disproportionate to the casual, conversational tone in which they had been delivered. My mind, which had been tempered by thirty years of exposure to the impossible, simply refused to process them, the way a wall refuses to process a battering ram by ceasing to exist.

"The Stasis Event," I repeated, because repetition was the only response my shock-addled brain could produce.

"The one that created me," Godric said, nodding, as though he were confirming a minor point of historical trivia. "The event that transformed my mortal self into what I became. Thorin's teleportation spell provided the delivery mechanism, but the spell itself was just a spell. A good spell, by mortal standards. A genuine bloodline spell with real power. But a spell, nonetheless. The Stasis Event, the amplification, the superpowers, the concealment, the weakness paradox, all of it, was my doing. Or rather," he paused, his expression thoughtful, "it will be my doing, in a few moments, when I reach backward through time and transform that simple teleportation into the event that birthed everything I am."

I stared at him. My mouth was open. I was aware that I must look foolish, but the part of my brain responsible for managing social propriety had shut down, overwhelmed by the task of processing the implications of what he had just said.

"You created the Stasis Event," I said slowly. "For yourself."

"For my past self," Godric corrected gently. "The version of me that existed ten thousand years before Year Zero. The mortal king of Olympus who had just lost everything and was about to spend ten millennia in a state of recursive amplification that would transform him into the being sitting before you. I am going to reach backward through time and give that man the gift that made him what I am."

"The gift that made you omnipotent."

"The gift that started the process," Godric said, nodding. "The amplification, the superpowers, all of it. Those were my additions to Thorin's spell. I took a simple teleportation and expanded it into something vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly more. And I am about to do it again, or rather, I am about to do it for the first time, because causality is a suggestion that I have long since stopped treating as binding."

He leaned forward, his massive frame shifting in the throne, his expression one of mild, almost sheepish admission.

"I should tell you," he said, "that I am holding back."

I blinked. "Holding back."

"Significantly," Godric confirmed, and the sheepishness in his expression deepened, the way it might on a man confessing that he had ordered a smaller dessert than he could have. "The version of the Stasis Event that I am about to create for my past self is a held-back version. A deliberately diminished version. A fraction of what I could actually give him."

My mouth was dry. My amplified heart, which could sustain the output of a dying star, was hammering against my ribs with enough force to crack adamantium.

"The Stasis Event," I said, "as you experienced it, the event that made you infinitely stronger than the entire omniverse combined by Year Zero, the event that gave you amplification beyond mathematics and superpowers beyond comprehension and absolute concealment and the weakness paradox and everything else, that was the held-back version?"

"Yes," Godric said, and his tone was genuinely apologetic, as though he were confessing to a minor discourtesy rather than an act of cosmic restraint so absurd that the word restraint itself collapsed under the weight of its inadequacy. "I could have given my past self much more. I could have given him my current mechanisms, copied perfectly, transferred backward through time with no cost to myself and no degradation of function. I could have given him the evolved versions of everything I am now, the countless refinements and upgrades and expansions that my power systems have undergone over the eons. But I chose not to."

"Why?"

Godric's expression shifted to one of thoughtful consideration, the way it did when he was attempting to articulate a complex feeling that his infinite intelligence had nonetheless struggled to reduce to mortal language.

"I believed," he said slowly, "that the held-back version would preserve some kind of mortal struggle. Some element of challenge, of difficulty, of effort. I believed that if I gave my past self too much, too quickly, it would eliminate the experience of growth, and the experience of growth was important to who I became. I wanted him to earn it, in some sense. To feel the amplification happening, to experience the acquisition of each new superpower, to struggle with the adjustment, even though the struggle was, by any objective measure, entirely illusory, because the held-back version was already so absurdly, incomprehensibly powerful that the concept of struggle in relation to it was a mathematical impossibility."

He paused, and a small, rueful smile crossed his face.
