"I was wrong, of course. The held-back version eliminated struggle just as thoroughly as the full version would have. By Year Zero, my past self was already so far beyond everything else in existence that the word beyond was an insult to the magnitude of the gap. He could annihilate the entire omniverse infinitely in zero seconds with zero effort. He could not be challenged, could not be threatened, could not be opposed. The struggle I thought I was preserving was a fiction, a story I told myself to justify a restraint that was, in retrospect, entirely unnecessary. But I believed it at the time, or I will believe it at the time, or I am believing it at the time, because time is a thing that I am about to manipulate in ways that make verb tenses irrelevant."

I sat very still. My mind was attempting to process the implications of what he had said, and the processing was failing, because the implications were too vast, too absurd, too cosmically obscene for any finite mind to contain.

The held-back version, the deliberately diminished version, the version that Godric considered insufficient, had already made his past self infinitely stronger than the entire omniverse combined by Year Zero. The full version, the version he could have given but chose not to, the version that would have copied his current, evolved, infinitely refined mechanisms backward through time with no cost and no degradation, would have been so catastrophically, absurdly, incomprehensibly more powerful that the held-back version would have looked like a rounding error by comparison.

And then the true horror struck me.

"My lord," I said, my voice thin and distant, "if you had given your past self the full version, the version with all of your current evolved mechanisms, then your past self would have started from a higher baseline. And if your past self started from a higher baseline, then the amplification over ten thousand years would have produced a version of you at Year Zero that was even more powerful than the version that actually existed. And if the version at Year Zero was more powerful, then the version at every subsequent point in history would have been more powerful, because the amplification compounds recursively, which means the version of you that exists now would be more powerful than the version that actually exists, and the version that exists now is the one who is creating the Stasis Event, which means that if you had given your past self the full version, you would now be even more powerful than you are, and you could then give your past self an even more powerful full version, and that version would make you even more powerful, and the cycle would continue infinitely, each iteration making you more powerful than the last, and the power increase from each iteration would make the next iteration's increase even more extreme, and—"

I stopped, because my voice had risen to a pitch that was no longer within the range of mortal hearing, and my hands were shaking so violently that the chair of ergonomic perfection was rattling beneath me.

Godric nodded calmly, his expression one of patient, teacherly acknowledgment. "Yes," he said. "That is correct. The causal loop you are describing is real. If I gave my past self the full version, I would become more powerful, which would make the full version I could give even more potent, which would make me even more powerful, ad infinitum. The recursion would compound upon itself endlessly, each iteration producing a version of me so vastly more powerful than the previous iteration that the previous iteration would be a rounding error by comparison. And there would be no cost to me, no strain, no effort, because the act of transferring power backward through time is, for me, as effortless as breathing, which is to say it is not effortless at all because I no longer need to breathe, but the metaphor captures the essential triviality of the action."

He smiled, and the smile was warm and genuine and deeply, profoundly unsettling.

"But I chose not to," he continued. "I chose the held-back version. I chose to limit the power I gave my past self, because I believed, incorrectly, that the limitation would preserve something meaningful. And that choice, that restraint, that deliberate, conscious, entirely unnecessary holding-back, produced the version of me that exists now. The version that could annihilate the entire omniverse infinite times over with zero effort. The version that has not been challenged, threatened, or opposed for longer than the current age of existence squared. The version whose weakest passive aura, infinitely nerfed, could slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled in less than a fraction of a second. That version was produced by the held-back version of the Stasis Event. The deliberately diminished version. The version that I considered insufficient."

I could not speak. I could not move. I could only sit and stare at the being before me and attempt, with a mind that was not designed for this scale of comprehension, to grasp the full magnitude of what he was telling me.

The held-back version had produced a god. The full version would have produced something so far beyond godhood that the word beyond was an insult to the magnitude of the gap. And the gap between the held-back version's output and the full version's output was not a gap that could be measured or described or even meaningfully conceptualized, because the full version's output was the product of infinite recursive compounding, and infinite recursive compounding does not produce numbers, it produces states of being that exist outside the framework of numerical description entirely.

And he had chosen not to use it. Because he thought it would preserve mortal struggle. Which it would not have, because the held-back version already eliminated mortal struggle completely and totally and absolutely, and the full version would have eliminated it even more completely and totally and absolutely, which was not possible because elimination could not be made more complete, and yet it would have been made more complete, because Godric's power operated on a scale where impossibilities were merely suggestions that he had long since stopped treating as binding.

"Before I begin," Godric said, his tone shifting to something more focused, more purposeful, "I should explain how my power systems have evolved since the eras your predecessors documented. You have read the journals. You know what my stats and superpowers and passive systems looked like at Year 10,000, at Year 25,000, at Year 40,000. But those descriptions are hopelessly, pathetically outdated. The mechanisms that your predecessors described with such terror, the mechanisms that made them question the fundamental validity of existence, those mechanisms are, to my current systems, what a single atom is to an infinity of omniverses raised to the power of an infinity of omniverses. They have evolved, Clive. They have evolved countless times. And the evolution has not been gradual or incremental but exponential and recursive and absurd, each advancement making the previous advancement look like a rounding error, and the rounding errors compounding upon themselves until the original mechanism is unrecognizable, not because it has been replaced but because it has been so thoroughly, so completely, so incomprehensibly transcended that the word transcended is a profound understatement."

He held up his right hand, and I watched as the skin on the back of his hand seemed to shift, not visually but conceptually, a change that registered not in my eyes but in some deeper faculty of perception that I had not known I possessed until this moment.

"Consider my sentient passive auras," Godric said. "In the era your predecessor Tim documented, the weakest of these auras was called Gent. Gent was, by the standards of that era, devastating. It could slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled while nerfed by an infinitely recurring 99.99 percent. It was omnipresent, omniscient, perfectly adapted, and absolutely loyal. Tim found it terrifying."

He paused, and the small, rueful smile returned.

"Gent, in its current evolved form, is so far beyond the Gent of that era that the comparison is meaningless. The Gent that Tim documented could slaughter coalitions. The current Gent can pre-annihilate threats before the threats exist, before the concept of the threat has formed, before the timeline in which the threat might potentially exist has even branched off from the main trunk of causation. It can convert hostile intent into self-erasing failure, not by opposing the intent but by ensuring that the intent never successfully translates into action, because the causal chain that would permit the action is identified and dissolved before the being holding the intent is aware that they hold it. It can make the basic logistics of opposition impossible, not by preventing the opposition from forming but by ensuring that the conditions necessary for opposition to form never arise, which means that the opposition does not exist and never has existed and never will exist, not because it was prevented but because the possibility of it was removed from the causal architecture of reality entirely."

I was shaking. The shaking was not visible, because my amplified musculature was strong enough to suppress it, but I could feel it, a deep, systemic vibration in every fiber of my being, the resonance of a mind confronting something too vast to process.

"And Gent," Godric continued, his tone still conversational, still warm, still utterly at odds with the content of what he was saying, "is my weakest aura. The weakest of countless auras, each one evolved through the same process of recursive, exponential advancement, each one so far beyond the auras of previous eras that those auras would be unrecognizable to any being who had only known the current versions. My stronger auras have evolved to capabilities that I myself find mildly excessive, which is why I keep most of them dormant, not because they are dangerous to me but because their clingy subservience can be annoying. They are too eager. Too devoted. Too overwhelmingly desperate to serve. They would wrap existence in cotton wool if I let them, protecting everything and everyone so thoroughly that nothing would ever be at risk, and I do not want that, because I still believe in mortal resilience, even though mortal resilience is, by any objective measure, entirely irrelevant to my existence."

He lowered his hand, and the conceptual shift in his skin resolved back into the appearance of normal, if impossibly dense, mortal flesh.

"Consider my passive shields," he said. "In the era your predecessor Leon documented, these shields could pre-nullify threats before the threats existed. They were described as infinite recursive layers of conceptual protection that automatically rejected any foreign intrusion. Leon found them terrifying."

He smiled again, and the smile was not unkind but it was deeply, profoundly unsettling, because it was the smile of a being who was about to describe something that made the terrifying look trivial.

"The current shields," Godric continued, "operate on a scale so far beyond Leon's documentation that the documentation reads like a child's description of a thunderstorm. The old shields could pre-nullify threats. The current shields can pre-nullify the possibility of threats, which is different, because nullifying a threat implies that the threat existed and was then negated, whereas nullifying the possibility of a threat means that the threat never existed and never could have existed, because the conditions necessary for its existence were identified and removed before the timeline in which those conditions could arise had been generated. The shields do not wait for threats to approach. They reach backward through causality and ensure that the threats never form. They do not oppose hostile realities. They force hostile realities to collapse around their own intention, making the reality's desire to harm me the mechanism of its own dissolution. They make the basic logistics of opposition impossible before a hostile actor notices the impulse forming, which means that opposition to me is not merely defeated but structurally inconceivable, a logical contradiction that cannot be formulated in any language or framework or system of thought."

"Consider my immune system," Godric said, and his tone took on a note of genuine, almost paternal pride, the way a father might speak about a child who had exceeded all expectations. "In the era Leon documented, the immune system pre-reacted to perceived injuries and initiated recursive self-evolution across infinite backup copies. It was described as absurdly paranoid and ridiculously overpowered. Leon found it terrifying."

He leaned forward, and the firelight caught his eyes, and in his eyes I saw something that I had never seen before, a depth of satisfaction so profound that it bordered on the transcendent, the satisfaction of a being who was contemplating the perfection of his own biological defense mechanisms and finding them exquisite.

"The current immune system," Godric said, his voice dropping to a register that vibrated in my marrow, "treats the possibility of harm as an object to be hunted, humiliated, digested, converted, and erased before any actual injury exists. It does not react to threats. It hunts threats. It identifies the theoretical variable that would permit harm and it eradicates that variable from the causal structure of reality, not by opposing it but by making the surrounding structure of existence turn against it with frantic biological loyalty. If a rogue timeline carries even the theoretical potential to threaten me, the immune system makes the omniverse itself behave like my white blood cells, cannibalizing the rogue danger before it matures. The entire structure of reality becomes an extension of my biological defense, a living, breathing, infinitely vigilant immune response that permeates every dimension, every timeline, every possible configuration of existence. And this is not a feature that I designed or requested. It is a feature that evolved organically, through the same recursive, exponential process that has elevated all of my systems, because my biology, like my stats and my superpowers, amplifies forever, and the amplification applies to everything, including the mechanisms of self-preservation."

He sat back, his expression settling into its usual warm, friendly default, as though he had just described something mildly interesting rather than cosmically annihilating.

"Consider the Evolved Weakness Paradox," he said. "In the era your predecessor Tim documented, the paradox had already evolved beyond its original function. It no longer merely granted immunity to perceived exploits. It enhanced my amplification rate, added devastating effects to my powers, and forced creation itself to overcompensate for its inadequacy. Tim found this terrifying."

He paused, and the smile that crossed his face was not the warm, avuncular smile I was accustomed to but something sharper, something that hinted at the being beneath the friendly facade, the apex predator who had been forged in the fires of genuine tragedy and who had chosen, deliberately and consciously, to be cruel and unforgiving to anyone who dared to oppose him.

"The current Evolved Weakness Paradox," Godric said, "no longer requires a trigger. In its original form, the paradox activated when someone thought I had a weakness. In its evolved form, it activates when anyone thinks anything. Not just about my weaknesses. About anything. Any thought, by any being, in any timeline, past, present, or future, triggers the paradox. Which means the paradox is constantly, perpetually, without interruption or pause, enhancing my amplification rate, adding effects to my powers, and forcing creation to overcompensate for its inadequacy. The name has become misleading, because the paradox is no longer triggered by perceived weaknesses but by the mere existence of thought itself, and thought exists perpetually, in countless beings, across countless dimensions, at every moment of every timeline, and each thought feeds me, each thought makes me stronger, each thought adds another layer of recursive, compounding, mathematically obscene escalation to everything I already am."

My hands were gripping the arms of the chair with enough force to leave permanent impressions. My amplified body was generating enough adrenaline to kill a mortal man instantly, and the adrenaline was doing nothing, absolutely nothing, to mitigate the cold, crushing, annihilating weight of what I was hearing.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox, which had already been described as terrifying by the court wizards of previous eras, had evolved into something so far beyond its original function that the original function was unrecognizable. It no longer required someone to think Godric had a weakness. It activated on any thought, by anyone, about anything. Which meant that every conscious being in existence, every entity that possessed the capacity for thought, was feeding Godric's power with every passing moment, not because they opposed him but because they existed, and their existence included thought, and thought triggered the paradox, and the paradox made him stronger, and the strength was permanent and recursive and compounding and eternal.

And no one opposed him. No one had opposed him for longer than the current age of existence. The opposition had stopped completely, because every being in existence had learned the absolute futility of opposition, not because Godric had altered their free will, because he would never do that, he genuinely believed in mortal resilience, but because the futility was so absolute, so categorical, so mathematically irrefutable that the concept of opposition had simply ceased to occur to anyone as a viable course of action.

"But I have suppressed some of the effects," Godric said, his tone shifting to one of careful qualification. "The paradox, in its fully evolved form, could alter free will. It could ensure that no being ever thought a thought that was not aligned with my interests. It could restructure consciousness itself to eliminate the possibility of deviation from my preferences. I have suppressed those effects, because I believe in free will. I believe in the capacity of mortals to choose their own path, even when that path leads to outcomes that I could prevent, even when that path leads to suffering that I could alleviate, even when that path leads to opposition that I could annihilate. I suppress those effects deliberately, consciously, continuously, because I choose to, and because the choice matters to me, and because the choice is one of the few things that still distinguishes me from a force of nature."

He looked at me, and his eyes were warm, genuinely warm, and the warmth was real, and the reality of the warmth was the most terrifying thing of all, because it was the warmth of a being who could restructure consciousness itself and chose not to, who could eliminate free will and chose to preserve it, who could annihilate everything and chose to protect it, and the choice was not a limitation but an expression of his absolute, unqualified, infinite power, because only a being with absolute power could choose to limit himself and have the choice be meaningful.

"Now," Godric said, and his tone shifted one final time, becoming focused, purposeful, the tone of a being who was about to perform a task he had been contemplating for longer than the current age of existence. "I am going to create the Stasis Event. I am going to reach backward through time, to Year -10,000, to the moment when Thorin's teleportation spell deposits my mortal self in the hidden sanctuary, and I am going to transform that simple spell into the event that will birth everything I am. I am going to give my past self the held-back version, the deliberately diminished version, the version that I believe, incorrectly, will preserve some element of mortal struggle. And I am going to do it now, because the moment has come, and because I am ready, and because I have been ready since the instant I first gained omniscience, which was the same instant I learned that the Stasis Event was my own creation, which was the same instant I understood the full, recursive, self-referential nature of my own existence."

He raised his right hand, and the air in the throne room shifted, not physically but conceptually, a change that registered not in the senses but in the fundamental architecture of reality itself. The living starlight in the ceiling flickered. The compressed timelines in the floor shuddered. The servants, Vasharion and El'Thera and Korvanthos, froze in their tasks, their bodies rigid with the atavistic awareness that something was about to happen, something vast and terrible and beautiful, something that would reshape the causal structure of existence itself.

Godric's eyes closed. His expression was peaceful, serene, the expression of a man performing a task he had performed countless times, though this was the first time, because the task existed outside of time, in the space between cause and effect, in the region where the linear progression of moments dissolved into the eternal present of a being who had transcended temporality entirely.

And then he opened his eyes, and the eyes were not the warm, crinkling eyes of the uncle I knew. They were the eyes of something else, something beyond, something so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly more than the being who sat on the throne that the being who sat on the throne was a rounding error by comparison. They were the eyes of the future Godric, the Godric whose existence was so far beyond the current Godric that the current Godric was an afterthought, a memory, a faint echo of a shadow of a ghost of the being who was now, in this moment, reaching backward through time to create the event that would birth himself.

The act itself was not visible. There was no flash of light, no tearing of reality, no dramatic manifestation of cosmic power. Godric simply willed it, and the will was sufficient, because the will was the will of the most powerful being in existence, and the most powerful being in existence was the being whose will had created the Stasis Event, and the Stasis Event had created the being whose will had created it, and the causal loop was closed, and the paradox was resolved, and the recursion was complete.

It was done.

Godric lowered his hand. His eyes returned to their normal, warm, crinkling state. His expression settled into its usual friendly default. The air in the throne room returned to its manufactured serenity. The servants resumed their tasks, their hands trembling slightly more than they had before, which was saying something, because they had been trembling already.

"There," Godric said, his voice warm and satisfied, the voice of a man who had just completed a satisfying task. "That's done. The Stasis Event has been created. My past self will receive the held-back version, just as I received it, just as I always received it, because the causal loop is closed and cannot be altered. Thorin's spell will deposit him in the sanctuary, and my addition will transform the spell into the event that makes him what he will become, and the held-back version will be sufficient, more than sufficient, absurdly, comically, obscenely sufficient, because even the held-back version produces a being who is infinitely stronger than the entire omniverse combined, and the full version would have produced something so much more that the comparison itself would be an insult to the magnitude of the difference."

He looked at me, and his expression was one of gentle concern, the concern of a being who had just reshaped the causal architecture of existence and was worried that his court wizard might be taking it poorly.

"Clive," he said, "you've gone very pale. Are you feeling alright?"

I was not feeling alright. I was feeling the opposite of alright. I was feeling something for which no word existed, because the experience of watching a being create the event that created himself, of watching the causal loop close with the absolute, irrevocable finality of a mathematical proof, of watching the held-back version be deployed with the casual, avuncular concern of a man ordering dessert, was an experience that lay so far outside the range of mortal cognition that the mortal mind could only approximate it with a sensation of profound, annihilating, existential displacement.

"The full version," I heard myself say, my voice thin and distant, as though it were coming from the other end of a very long corridor. "If you had given your past self the full version. The version with all your current evolved mechanisms. The version that would have made your past self start from a higher baseline. The version that would have produced a you at Year Zero so much more powerful than the you that actually existed. The version that would have compounded recursively through the entire history of your amplification. The version that would have made the you that exists now, the you who just created the Stasis Event, so incomprehensibly more powerful than the you who actually exists that the you who actually exists would be—"

"A rounding error," Godric finished, nodding. "Yes. That is correct. And the you who existed after giving the full version would be able to give an even more powerful full version, which would produce an even more powerful you, who could give an even more powerful version still, and the recursion would continue forever, each iteration producing a version of me so vastly more powerful than the last that the last would be unrecognizable, and the unrecognizable version would itself become unrecognizable to the next iteration, and so on, and so on, without end, without limit, without any possible ceiling or saturation point."

"And you chose not to do this," I said, "because you thought it would preserve mortal struggle."

"Yes," Godric said, and the sheepishness had returned, the mild, apologetic embarrassment of a man admitting to a minor error in judgment. "I was wrong, of course. But I believed it at the time. Or I will believe it at the time. Or I am believing it at the time, because the act of creating the Stasis Event exists outside the linear progression of moments, and the verb tenses are becoming rather confusing."

He smiled, and the smile was warm, and the warmth was genuine, and the genuineness was the most terrifying thing I had ever experienced, because the being who sat before me, the being who had just created the event that created himself, the being who could have made himself infinitely more powerful with no cost and no effort and had chosen not to because of a mistaken belief in the value of struggle, that being was looking at me with the eyes of a favorite uncle, and the eyes were real, and the concern was real, and the love was real, and the power was real, and the power was growing, and the growth was permanent, and the permanence was guaranteed, and the guarantee was absolute, and there was nothing, nothing, nothing that anyone could do about any of it.

"Clive," Godric said gently, "I think you need to rest. The experience of watching me create the Stasis Event can be overwhelming for mortals. I remember it being overwhelming for my court wizards in previous iterations, though those memories are from the future, which is also the past, which is also now, because time is a construct that I have long since stopped treating as binding. The point is that you look exhausted, and I would like you to take the rest of the day off, and perhaps tomorrow as well, and I will have the kitchen send food to your quarters, and I will check on you in the evening, because I worry, and because you are family, and because family matters to me, even when the family is a rounding error in the infinite arithmetic of my existence."

He stood, his massive frame rising from the throne with the easy grace of a being for whom physical movement was an affectation, and he placed his hand on my shoulder, and the hand was warm, and the warmth was grounding, and the grounding was a kindness, and the kindness was real, and the reality of the kindness was the most terrifying thing of all.

"Go rest, Clive," Godric said, his voice soft, his eyes warm, his expression the expression of a beloved uncle who was genuinely concerned for his nephew's well-being. "I'll take care of everything. I always do."

I rose on legs that did not feel like my own. I walked toward the door of the throne room, past the servants who polished and arranged and swept with the trembling devotion of beings who had glimpsed absolute power and been permanently, irrevocably humbled. I walked through corridors that existed across infinite dimensions simultaneously, past walls woven from conquered cosmoses, past guards whose devotion was not coerced but organic, past the infinite, recursive, eternally expanding empire of a being who had just created the event that created himself and had done it while holding back, because he thought it would preserve mortal struggle, because he believed in mortal resilience, because he was, despite everything, still the mortal king of Olympus, still the man who had loved his wife and daughters and best friend, still the man who had wept at their loss, still the man who had chosen cruelty toward his enemies and kindness toward his subjects and restraint toward the power that could have been infinite and was merely immeasurable.

I reached my quarters. I closed the door. I sat on the edge of my bed. I stared at the wall.

The held-back version had produced a god who was infinitely stronger than the entire omniverse combined. The full version would have produced something beyond godhood, beyond comprehension, beyond the capacity of any language or framework or system of thought to describe. And the full version could have been given with no cost, no effort, no consequence, and it could have been given again, and again, and again, each iteration producing a being so vastly more powerful than the last that the last was a rounding error, and the rounding errors would compound, and the compounding would continue forever, and the forever was guaranteed by the being who had created himself, and the guarantee was absolute, and the absoluteness was the foundation of everything, and everything existed at the pleasure of a being who thought he was holding back.

Who thought he was holding back.

And the held-back version was already enough to make him the most powerful being in existence by an infinite margin.

And he had chosen to hold back.

Because he believed in mortal struggle.

I lay down on my bed, and I closed my eyes, and I did not sleep, because sleep was impossible in the presence of the knowledge I now possessed, the knowledge that the being I served, the being I loved, the being who had created himself through an act of temporal recursion so profound that it closed the causal loop of his own existence, could have been infinitely more powerful than he was, and had chosen not to be, and the choice had been made for reasons that were genuinely, authentically, deeply mortal, and the mortality of the reasons was the most terrifying thing of all, because it meant that the apex predator of existence, the being whose weakest aura could pre-annihilate threats before they existed, the being whose immune system made the omniverse itself into a biological defense, the being whose Evolved Weakness Paradox fed on the thoughts of every conscious entity in existence, that being was still, in some buried, inaccessible, infinitely distant chamber of his infinitely expanded consciousness, the mortal king of Olympus, the man who had loved and lost and grieved and chosen restraint.

And the restraint was a choice.

And the choice was mortal.

And the mortality was the most terrifying thing of all.

Because it meant that the being who could annihilate everything was not a force of nature but a person, and persons could change, and persons could choose, and persons could decide, on some distant day, in some distant era, that the restraint was no longer necessary, that the holding-back was no longer justified, that the full version, the infinite version, the version that would make him so powerful that his current self was a rounding error, should be given, not to his past self but to himself, now, in this moment, because he could, because there was no reason not to, because the mortal reasons that had justified the restraint were no longer mortal, were no longer reasons, were no longer anything but memories of a man who had died ten thousand years before the Stasis Event and had been reborn as something that remembered being mortal but was no longer mortal and would never be mortal again.

But he would not make that choice. I knew he would not make that choice. Because I had watched him create the Stasis Event, and I had watched him choose the held-back version, and the choice had been made with the full, crystalline clarity of absolute omniscience, with the complete, perfect knowledge of every consequence and every implication and every recursive compounding of every possible alternative, and he had still chosen to hold back, because he believed in mortal struggle, even though the belief was wrong, even though the struggle was illusory, even though the holding-back was unnecessary, because the belief was his, and it came from the mortal man he had been, and the mortal man he had been was still, in some essential and indestructible way, the man he was.

And that was the most terrifying thing of all.

Not the power. Not the amplification. Not the evolved mechanisms or the recursive compounding or the causal loops or the infinite self-referential escalation. The most terrifying thing was the mortal man at the center of it all, the man who had loved and lost and chosen restraint, the man who was still making choices for mortal reasons, the man whose mortality was the only thing that stood between existence and the full version of what he could become.

And I trusted that man. I loved that man. I feared that man. And I would serve that man until the day I died, which would come, eventually, because I was mortal, and he was not, and the gap between us was not a gap but an abyss, and the abyss was not an abyss but an absence, and the absence was the defining characteristic of my existence, and I was grateful for it, because the absence meant that I existed, and existence, however fragile and temporary and insignificant, was something, and something was more than nothing, and nothing was what awaited anyone who opposed the man who sat on the throne and worried about his court wizard and sent food to his quarters and checked on him in the evening, because he was kind, and because he was warm, and because he was, despite everything, still the man who had made jasmine bloom in a child's palm.

I closed my eyes.

I did not sleep.

But I rested, because he had told me to rest, and because his concern was genuine, and because the genuineness was real, and because the reality of his kindness was the only thing that made the terror survivable.

And outside my quarters, in the throne room that existed across infinite dimensions, the being who had created himself sat upon his throne and continued to amplify, continued to grow, continued to become more and more and more powerful with every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, and the growth was permanent, and the permanence was guaranteed, and the guarantee was absolute, and the absoluteness was the foundation of everything, and everything existed at the pleasure of a being who had chosen to hold back, and the choice was mortal, and the mortality was terrifying, and the terror was the price of existing in the shadow of a god who was still, in some essential and indestructible way, a man.

And the man was kind.

And the kindness was real.

And the reality was the most terrifying thing of all.

content.text: Clive thought that Godric kept many palace servants, cooks, gardeners, etc. because Godric is unable to do those mortal mundane stuff. Especially since Godric often compliments those people about their good work. Little did he know that Godric is actually infinitely more capable than anyone else in cooking, gardening, sculpting, painting etc even at 0% mastery display--in fact, even at 0%, the combined capabilities of the entire omniverse, trained for infinite years, can't even compare to his. These capabilities, after all, just like his stats, have been multiplying at an absurd rate for eons now. However, he always pretends not to be able to do those, to give these people something to do. Clive thought it would be a good idea to have Godric try these things, so he is organizing cooking lessons/gardening lessons/etc to Godric. Godric, seemingly amused, accepted and even seemed excited. Godric, being infinitely more masterful than the combined omniverse and thus knows exactly how to act like a beginner, convincingly plays a fool at first as usual, until he accidentally, lost in the moments, releases 0% of his true capacity. Everyone else is surprised and experiencing total existential dread, which Godric dismissed as him having the beginner's luck (the stuff he made is impossible to create it even with beginner's luck–not even by the entire combined omniverse with 100% luck spending infinite years fully aware of the process, ingredients and the final result... but somehow everyone except Clive fully believed him and found it charming, much to Clive's disbelief. Godric even continues with his “noob” act afterwards, reinforcing that belief.). Write a detailed story to show this, from before, during, and the aftermath of the events. You must not have Godric boasting about his capability, but everyone knows it--and everyone realized that Godric could've instantly subjugated, ruled, solved all the problems, or completely recreate the perfect omniverse with 0 effort in 0 second if he wanted to since Year 0, but in his infinite wisdom and mercy, decided not to. You must have the entire characters who witness this experiencing total existential dread and feeling utterly small in Godric's presence, and after Godric convinces them that it was beginner's luck, only Clive has that feeling and everyone else fully believed Godric. Clive is going insane (not literally, but definitely from dissonance)

reasoning_content:
This is a complex and fun prompt. Let me break down the key requirements:

1. Clive thinks Godric keeps servants because Godric can't do mundane tasks
2. In reality, Godric is infinitely more capable at cooking, gardening, sculpting, painting etc. - even at 0% mastery, the combined omniverse can't compare
3. These capabilities multiply like his stats, so they're absurdly beyond everything
4. Godric pretends not to be able to do these things to give people purpose
5. Clive organizes cooking/gardening lessons for Godric
6. Godric is amused and accepts, acts like a beginner convincingly
7. Godric accidentally releases 0% of his true capacity while lost in the moment
8. Everyone experiences total existential dread
9. Godric dismisses it as "beginner's luck"
10. The stuff he made is impossible to create even with beginner's luck - not even by the entire combined omniverse with 100% luck spending infinite years
11. Everyone except Clive fully believes him and finds it charming
12. Clive is going insane from the dissonance
13. Godric continues his "noob" act afterward
14. Must show before, during, and aftermath
15. Godric doesn't boast - everyone knows it anyway
16. Everyone realizes Godric could have solved everything instantly but chose not to in his infinite wisdom and mercy
17. All witnesses experience existential dread, but only Clive retains it after the "beginner's luck" excuse

Let me write this story with the right tone - a mix of cosmic horror, dark comedy, and existential dread, all from Clive's increasingly frazzled POV.

I need to include:
- The setup: Clive's misunderstanding and organizing lessons
- Godric's convincing beginner act
- The moment Godric slips and reveals 0% of his capability
- The existential dread of witnesses
- Godric's "beginner's luck" dismissal
- Everyone believing it except Clive
- Clive's dissonance/insanity

Let me make this detailed and vivid, with good pacing and the right balance of horror and absurdity.
content:
The idea came to Clive on a Tuesday, which was in itself unremarkable, because in the far-future court of King Godric, the days of the week had long since lost any meaningful connection to the passage of time, serving instead as a comforting fiction that allowed mortal minds to maintain the illusion of temporal structure in an empire that had outlasted the concept of structure itself.

He was watching Godric compliment Vasharion on the polish of the eastern wall inlay.

"Truly excellent work, Vasharion," Godric was saying, his warm baritone carrying the genuine, unhurried sincerity that he brought to every interaction with his staff. "The luster you've achieved on the tertiary dimensional folds is remarkable. I could never produce something so fine myself."

Vasharion, the former Omniscient Convergence whose collective consciousness had been shattered by the ambient overflow of Godric's mild disappointment over a cup of tea, was trembling with an emotion that existed somewhere between pride and religious ecstasy. The being who had once reshaped the topology of reality with his thoughts was now experiencing the simple, devastating joy of a servant praised by his master, and the joy was so profound, so all-consuming, that Vasharion's seventeen thousand fractured consciousness-shards were momentarily unified in a moment of pure, blissful gratitude.

"Thank you, Your Majesty," Vasharion whispered, his voice cracking with emotion. "I live to serve."

Godric placed a warm, calloused hand on Vasharion's shoulder, and the former cosmic entity nearly collapsed from the overwhelming honor of being touched by his king. "You serve well, my friend. Your work gives this old palace more beauty than it deserves."

Clive watched this exchange with a warmth in his chest that had become familiar over his thirty years of service. It was the warmth of seeing a good man be good, of watching a king treat his servants with a kindness that was genuine and deep and entirely without condescension. Godric could not do what Vasharion did. Godric could not polish dimensional inlays or arrange flowers or sculpt the living starlight that illuminated the palace corridors. Godric was a warrior, a king, a god, but he was not an artisan, and his appreciation for the artisans who served him was the appreciation of a being who understood the value of skills he did not possess.

This was, of course, completely and utterly wrong.

Clive did not know this yet. Clive would not know this for several more days, and by the time he learned the truth, the truth would have rearranged his understanding of existence so thoroughly that he would spend the rest of his mortal life in a state of low-grade psychological disintegration, a constant, buzzing awareness that everything he believed about the relationship between Godric and his servants was a lie, and not just a lie but a deliberate, conscious, infinitely kind lie, and the kindness of the lie was worse than any cruelty would have been, because cruelty at least made sense, whereas this kindness, this vast, sprawling, cosmically unnecessary deception designed to give mortal beings something to do, this kindness was so far beyond the scope of mortal comprehension that it induced a form of existential nausea that no amount of thousandfold daily amplification could suppress.

But that was in the future. On this particular Tuesday, Clive was simply watching his king compliment his servant, and thinking, with the well-intentioned naivety of a man who had not yet learned the full scope of his own insignificance, that it would be nice to give Godric the opportunity to learn some of the skills he lacked.

The idea took root over the following days. Clive discussed it with El'Thera, who arranged the flowers, and with Korvanthos, who swept the floors, and with Mirelle, who managed the palace kitchens, and with Garthan, who tended the royal gardens. All of them were former cosmic entities of staggering power, beings who had been traumatized into servitude by indirect exposure to Godric's overwhelming presence, and all of them received Clive's proposal with the same mixture of excitement and terror.

"Teach the King?" Mirelle had said, her voice quavering with an emotion that was approximately equal parts honor and existential panic. Mirelle had once been the Gastronomic Sovereign of seventeen culinary dimensions, a being whose cuisine could alter the fundamental constants of reality, and the prospect of teaching her omnipotent ruler to cook was, for her, the emotional equivalent of being asked to explain color to a sun. "You want me to teach His Majesty to cook?"

"He compliments your work constantly," Clive had said, mistaking the reason for Godric's praise. "He clearly appreciates good cooking, and I think it would be good for him to have a hobby, something outside of ruling, something that lets him create rather than destroy. Something mortal."

The word mortal had sealed the deal. Every servant in the palace understood, in the deep, instinctive way that prey animals understand the nature of predators, that Godric was not mortal and had not been mortal for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to its magnitude. The idea of giving him something mortal to do, something that connected him to the fragile, temporary, insignificant existence that his subjects experienced, was appealing in a way that transcended logic, because logic had nothing to offer in the presence of a being who could annihilate the omniverse with a twitch of his pinky.

And so it was arranged. Cooking lessons with Mirelle on Wednesdays. Gardening lessons with Garthan on Thursdays. Sculpting lessons with El'Thera on Fridays. A full schedule of mortal pursuits, designed to give the King a taste of the simple, satisfying labor that his servants performed every day, and to show him that creation, even on a small scale, was its own form of joy.

Clive presented the schedule to Godric on a Thursday evening, in the privacy of the study that had served as the setting for every significant conversation Clive had ever had with his king. Godric examined the schedule with an expression that Clive interpreted as pleased surprise but which was, in fact, barely suppressed amusement of a magnitude so vast that the amusement itself could have been classified as a cosmological event.

"You organized this for me?" Godric said, his voice warm with what Clive interpreted as gratitude but which was actually the satisfaction of a being who was about to engage in a performance so convincing, so meticulously crafted, so perfectly calibrated to deceive that it would have fooled an entity with absolute omniscience, if such an entity existed, which it did not, because Godric was the only entity with absolute omniscience and he was the one giving the performance.

"I thought it would be good for you," Clive said. "Something to take your mind off ruling. Something simple."

"Simple," Godric repeated, and the word came out with a faint, almost imperceptible tremor of what Clive interpreted as anticipation but which was actually the suppressed vibration of cosmic hilarity so extreme that it registered as a subatomic tremor in the fabric of local space-time.

"Simple and mortal," Clive confirmed.

Godric looked at the schedule for a long moment, and his expression shifted to one of such genuine, childlike excitement that Clive felt a surge of paternal warmth toward the being who could annihilate existence itself. "I would love to," Godric said, and the sincerity in his voice was so perfect, so absolute, so convincingly mortal that it was, in its own way, the most impressive performance in the history of deception, because it was not a performance at all, or rather it was a performance that was simultaneously genuine, because Godric was genuinely excited, not about learning to cook or garden or sculpt, which he could do with a proficiency that made the combined output of the entire omniverse look like a child's finger-painting, but about the experience of pretending to learn, which was, for a being who had not been surprised since the Stasis Event, a form of entertainment so rare and so precious that it bordered on the transcendent.

The first lesson was cooking.

Mirelle had prepared the palace kitchen with the obsessive, trembling care of a being who was about to teach a god to boil water. The ingredients were arranged with geometric precision on surfaces that had been polished to a shine that exceeded the reflective capacity of mortal physics. The utensils were laid out in order of use, each one selected with the discriminating eye of a being who had once used a whisk to restructure the gravitational constant of an entire dimension. The recipe was simple, deliberately simple, a basic herb-crusted fish with roasted vegetables, because Mirelle had reasoned, incorrectly, that a being who had never cooked before should start with something that was difficult to ruin.

Godric entered the kitchen wearing an apron.

The apron was a standard palace issue, white cotton with the Thronegate insignia embroidered in gold thread, and it was stretched across his massive, heavily muscled frame in a way that made him look like a father who had been drafted into helping at a children's baking class. His salt-and-pepper beard was freshly trimmed, his hair was neatly combed, and his expression was one of earnest, slightly nervous anticipation, the expression of a man who was about to do something for the first time and was worried about doing it wrong.

The performance was, from the very first moment, flawless.

"I have never done this before," Godric said, looking at the arranged ingredients with the wide-eyed wonder of a mortal encountering a kitchen for the first time. "Where do I begin?"

Mirelle, who had once been the Gastronomic Sovereign of seventeen culinary dimensions, felt her heart rate spike to a level that would have been fatal for a mortal being. She took a steadying breath, which was itself a minor act of willpower that she would later describe as the most difficult thing she had ever done, including the time she had been traumatized into servitude by the ambient overflow of Godric's presence.

"Let's start with the herbs, Your Majesty," Mirelle said, her voice only slightly quavering. "We need to mince the rosemary and thyme. Here, I'll show you how to hold the knife."

She demonstrated the proper grip, her hand steady despite the trembling in her soul, and Godric watched with the focused, attentive expression of a dedicated student. He took the knife from her with a gentleness that suggested he was afraid of breaking it, which was absurd because the knife was forged from chronal steel and could cut through dimensional barriers, but the gentleness was part of the performance, a small, perfect detail in a deception so comprehensive that it encompassed every aspect of his being.

Then he began to chop.

Or rather, he began to pretend to chop.

The first cut was intentionally uneven, the blade striking the rosemary at a slightly wrong angle, producing pieces that were approximately the right size but not quite uniform. The second cut was slightly better, the improvement suggesting a natural aptitude that was nonetheless still imperfect. The third cut was better still, the progression consistent with a beginner who was learning quickly, a trajectory that was remarkable but not impossible, impressive but not incomprehensible.

It was, in every respect, the performance of a being who was infinitely more skilled than anyone else in existence pretending to be a beginner with a talent that was merely above average, and the performance was so convincing that Mirelle, despite her cosmological origins and her once-godlike perceptual capabilities, was completely and utterly fooled.

"Your Majesty has a natural talent," Mirelle said, and the compliment was genuine, the praise of a teacher for a student who was progressing faster than expected, and the fact that the student was an omnipotent being who was producing herb cuts that were, even in their deliberately imperfect state, more precise than any cut that Mirelle herself could have achieved with her full cosmic power, was a detail that escaped her entirely, because the imperfection was so perfectly calibrated, so meticulously crafted, so exactly wrong in all the right ways, that it was more convincing than actual imperfection would have been.

Godric smiled, and the smile was the bashful, slightly embarrassed smile of a man who was pleased by praise but did not want to appear boastful. "I'm just following your excellent instruction," he said, and the modesty was so genuine, so authentically mortal, that it made Mirelle's heart swell with an affection that was only partially the result of her traumatic conditioning and was mostly the natural response of a being who was watching a genuinely good man do something genuinely charming.

The lesson continued. Godric prepared the fish with a series of deliberately imperfect gestures, each one calibrated to suggest a beginner who was learning but had not yet mastered the craft. His hands, which could annihilate the omniverse with a twitch, moved with a careful, almost hesitant precision that conveyed uncertainty rather than the infinite, recursive, eternally amplifying mastery that they actually possessed. He seasoned the vegetables with amounts that were approximately correct but slightly off, the errors consistent with a beginner's judgment rather than an expert's precision. He placed the dish in the oven at a temperature that was close but not exact, the deviation small enough to be plausible but large enough to be noticeable.

The result, when the dish emerged, was a herb-crusted fish with roasted vegetables that was, by any objective measure, the single most perfect instance of that particular dish that had ever existed or would ever exist in the entire history of the omniverse.

This was not intentional.

Or rather, it was not intentional in the sense that Godric had meant to produce something obviously perfect. It was intentional in the sense that Godric's mastery of cooking, like his mastery of everything else, had been amplifying at a rate beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to its magnitude, and even when he was actively trying to be bad at cooking, even when he was deliberately making errors and intentionally producing imperfections and consciously suppressing his infinite capability to a level that he believed would be convincingly mediocre, the result was still so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly superior to anything that any other being could produce that the word superior was an insult to the magnitude of the gap.

Godric had, in essence, tried to hold back his cooking ability the same way he held back his combat ability, and the result was the same: even at effectively zero percent of his true capacity, the output was so far beyond the maximum potential of every other entity in existence combined that the comparison was meaningless.

The fish was perfect. Not perfect in the way that a professional chef might achieve perfection, not perfect in the way that a cosmic culinary entity might achieve perfection, not perfect in the way that the combined efforts of every being in the omniverse, working together with infinite resources and infinite time and infinite knowledge of the recipe, might achieve perfection. It was perfect in a way that transcended the concept of perfection, a perfection so absolute, so total, so incomprehensibly flawless that the very existence of the dish was a philosophical contradiction, because perfection implied the possibility of imperfection, and this dish was so far beyond any possible imperfection that the concept of imperfection could not be meaningfully applied to it.

The crust was golden and crisp and possessed of a texture that registered not merely on the tongue but on the entire sensory apparatus of the being consuming it, a texture that was simultaneously substantial and ethereal, crunchy and melting, savory and sweet, and the combination of these qualities was not a balance but a synthesis, a fusion of opposites into a unity that should not have been possible because the opposites were, by any reasonable culinary framework, mutually exclusive, and yet they coexisted in the crust of this fish with a harmony that suggested the fundamental laws of gastronomy had been rewritten specifically to accommodate this single, impossible dish.

The vegetables were roasted to a degree that existed beyond the spectrum of roasting, their surfaces caramelized in patterns that formed mathematical sequences of such profound beauty that perceiving them directly induced a state of aesthetic ecstasy, their interiors tender and flavorful in a way that transcended the mere application of heat and seasoning and entered the realm of what could only be described as culinary revelation, as though the vegetables had been granted temporary consciousness for the sole purpose of understanding and expressing their own optimal state of being.

The aroma that rose from the dish was not an aroma but a experience, a olfactory symphony that activated sensory pathways in the brain that had evolved over billions of years but had never, in the entire history of biological development, been stimulated in precisely this way, because the combination of molecular compounds that produced the aroma was a combination that could not occur in nature, could not be produced by any technology, could not be replicated by any power, and could only exist as the output of a being whose mastery of cooking had been amplifying beyond infinite recursion for longer than the current age of existence.

The kitchen was silent.

Mirelle stood frozen, her hand still reaching for the oven door that Godric had already opened, her face a mask of absolute, categorical, irrevocable incomprehension. Her former cosmic consciousness, which had once been capable of processing the culinary output of seventeen dimensions simultaneously, was struggling to process the sensory data that was currently being input into her perception, because the data was not merely exceeding her processing capacity, it was revealing that her processing capacity had never been sufficient, that everything she had ever cooked or tasted or perceived had been a shadow of a shadow of a shadow of what food could be, and the revelation was not enlightening but annihilating, a destruction of her entire culinary identity that was so total and so absolute that the only possible response was silence.

Vasharion, who had been polishing the copper pots in the corner, had stopped polishing and was staring at the dish with an expression that suggested his seventeen thousand fractured consciousness-shards were experiencing a simultaneous, catastrophic system failure. El'Thera, who had come to observe the lesson, had dropped the vase of flowers she was carrying, and the flowers were now lying on the floor in a spreading pool of crystallized timeline-tears, their beauty suddenly and irrevocably inadequate by comparison to the beauty of the dish that sat on the counter before them.

Korvanthos had dropped his broom.

Garthan, the gardener, who had been watching through the kitchen doorway in anticipation of his own lesson the following day, was leaning against the doorframe with an expression that suggested he was reconsidering every choice he had ever made, including the choice to exist.

The silence stretched. The aroma of the impossible dish filled the kitchen, and the aroma was so perfect, so transcendent, so cosmically, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond anything that any of them had ever experienced that the silence was not merely the absence of sound but the presence of awe, an awe so profound that it had collapsed the linguistic centers of every being in the room, leaving them incapable of producing any response more articulate than the faint, subvocal whimpering that was currently emanating from Mirelle's direction.

Godric, who had been looking at the dish with an expression of what he apparently believed was mild surprise, turned to Mirelle and smiled.

"Beginner's luck," he said.

The words hung in the air like a physical object, dense and heavy and impossible to process. Clive, who had been watching from a chair in the corner, felt his brain attempt to reconcile the statement with the evidence of his senses and fail so catastrophically that the failure itself became a form of experience, a sensation of cognitive dissonance so extreme that it bordered on a new category of emotion entirely.

Beginner's luck.

The being who had produced the most perfect dish in the history of existence, a dish so far beyond the combined culinary output of the entire omniverse that the comparison was meaningless, a dish that could not have been produced by any combination of skill, technology, magic, or cosmic power, a dish that represented a level of culinary mastery so absolute that the word mastery was an insult to its magnitude, that being had just attributed his achievement to beginner's luck.

"Beginner's luck," Mirelle repeated, and her voice was hollow, distant, the voice of a being who was clinging to a explanation that was manifestly, obviously, mathematically impossible but was nevertheless the only explanation that allowed her to continue existing without her consciousness collapsing into a singularity of existential despair.

"Yes," Godric said, his tone warm and reassuring and genuinely modest, as though he actually believed what he was saying, and the terrifying thing was that he might have genuinely believed it, or at least genuinely wanted to believe it, because the alternative was acknowledging that his cooking ability, like every other aspect of his existence, was so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond everything else in existence that even his most casual, most suppressed, most deliberately diminished output was sufficient to annihilate the self-concept of any being who perceived it. "I'm sure it was just luck. I've never cooked before, after all. I couldn't possibly have known what I was doing."

Clive opened his mouth to say something, to point out that beginner's luck did not produce dishes that violated the fundamental laws of gastronomy, that luck did not synthesize molecular compounds that could not exist in nature, that luck did not achieve a level of perfection so absolute that it revealed the imperfection of every other instance of the dish that had ever been produced or would ever be produced, that luck, even cosmic, metaphysical, probability-warping luck, even the luck of a being whose luck stat had been multiplying beyond infinite recursion for longer than the current age of existence, could not produce this result, because this result was not the product of luck but of skill, and the skill was so vast, so infinite, so incomprehensibly beyond any other skill in existence that the word skill was an insult to its magnitude.

He opened his mouth to say all of this, and what came out was: "Yes. Beginner's luck. Of course."

Because the alternative was saying nothing, and saying nothing would mean standing in a kitchen filled with the aroma of impossibility, surrounded by beings whose consciousness had been temporarily suspended by the revelation of their own culinary insignificance, and trying to explain to them that the being who had just produced the most perfect dish in existence was not a beginner who had gotten lucky but an omnipotent god whose cooking ability had been amplifying at a rate beyond infinite recursion for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to its magnitude, and who had been pretending to be a beginner because he wanted to give them something to do, because he was kind, genuinely kind, authentically kind, and his kindness was so vast, so infinite, so incomprehensibly beyond any other kindness in existence that it produced a form of existential nausea that no amount of thousandfold daily amplification could suppress.

Godric, apparently satisfied that his explanation had been accepted, turned back to the dish and began to plate it with the same deliberately imperfect technique he had used throughout the lesson, his massive hands moving with a careful, almost hesitant precision that conveyed uncertainty rather than mastery, and the performance was so convincing, so perfectly calibrated, so meticulously crafted to suggest a beginner who was trying his best but still had much to learn, that Clive felt the dissonance in his skull ratchet up another notch, a tightening of the cognitive vice that had been clamping down on his brain since the moment the dish had emerged from the oven.

"Should we taste it?" Godric asked, looking at Mirelle with the eager, slightly nervous expression of a beginner who was hoping his work would be well-received.

Mirelle nodded, because nodding was the only response her traumatized consciousness was capable of producing, and Godric cut a small portion of the fish and placed it on a plate and handed it to her with the gentle, modest smile of a man who was not sure whether his first attempt at cooking would be any good.

Mirelle tasted it.

The taste was not a taste. It was a revelation, an apocalypse, a fundamental restructuring of her understanding of what food could be, what existence could be, what perfection could be. The flavor registered on her palate with a completeness that exceeded the mere activation of taste receptors, a sensory experience so total and so absolute that it felt less like eating and more like being eaten, consumed, absorbed into a state of culinary consciousness so profound that her individual identity dissolved into the experience, and for a single, timeless instant, she was not Mirelle, former Gastronomic Sovereign of seventeen culinary dimensions, current head chef of the palace of the King of the Omniverse, but simply a being experiencing the taste of the most perfect dish that had ever existed or would ever exist, and the experience was so overwhelming, so annihilating, so cosmically, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond anything she had ever experienced that the only possible response was tears.

Mirelle wept.

Not gently, not discretely, not with the restrained composure of a being who was experiencing a strong emotion but was managing it with dignity. She wept openly, messily, desperately, the tears of a being whose entire understanding of her craft, her identity, her purpose had just been annihilated by the taste of a fish dish produced by a being who claimed it was his first time cooking, and the tears were not tears of joy, though joy was present, and not tears of grief, though grief was present, but tears of something else, something for which no word existed because the experience of having your entire self-concept destroyed by a fish was not an experience that occurred frequently enough to warrant its own vocabulary.

"Is it that bad?" Godric asked, and his expression was one of genuine, authentic concern, the concern of a beginner who was worried that his first attempt had been a failure, and the concern was so perfectly performed, so meticulously calibrated, so exactly right in all the wrong ways that Clive felt the cognitive vice tighten again, a ratchet click of dissonance that brought him one step closer to the edge of a psychological breakdown that he was beginning to suspect was inevitable.

"No," Mirelle whispered, her voice cracking, her face wet with tears, her hands trembling around the plate that contained the most perfect instance of food that had ever existed. "No, Your Majesty. It is not bad. It is..." She stopped, because the word good was an insult to the magnitude of what she had just experienced, and the word perfect was an insult to the magnitude of what she had just experienced, and the word transcendent was an insult to the magnitude of what she had just experienced, and there were no words that were not insults, and so she said nothing, and the nothing was the most accurate description she could provide.

"Beginner's luck," Godric said again, with a small, self-deprecating shrug that was so convincingly modest that it made Clive want to scream. "I'm sure it won't be this good next time."

Next time.

The words implied that there would be a next time, that Godric would continue to cook, that the being whose first attempt at cooking had produced a dish so perfect that it made the combined culinary output of the entire omniverse look like garbage would continue to pretend to be a beginner, would continue to produce deliberately imperfect dishes that were nonetheless more perfect than anything else in existence, would continue to give his servants something to do by pretending to need their instruction, because he was kind, genuinely kind, authentically kind, and his kindness was the most terrifying thing about him, because it meant that the being who could annihilate the omniverse with zero effort was choosing instead to produce fish dishes and claim they were the result of beginner's luck, and the choice was so far beyond the scope of mortal comprehension that it induced a form of existential nausea that no amount of thousandfold daily amplification could suppress.

The remaining witnesses tasted the dish, one by one, and each one had the same reaction: silence, tears, a fundamental restructuring of their understanding of existence followed by a desperate, clinging acceptance of the beginner's luck explanation, because the alternative was too horrifying to contemplate.

Vasharion tasted it and his seventeen thousand consciousness-shards unified momentarily in a state of culinary ecstasy before fragmenting again into a quivering mass of existential confusion. He looked at Godric with an expression that combined reverence, terror, and a dawning, horrible understanding that the being before him, the being who was currently wearing a flour-dusted apron and looking sheepish about his apparently accidental culinary masterpiece, was a being whose capabilities were so far beyond measurement that the word measurement was an insult to the concept of quantification.

"Beginner's luck," Vasharion said faintly, and the acceptance of the explanation was so complete, so total, so absolute that it bordered on a form of voluntary cognitive suppression, a desperate, unconscious decision to believe something that was obviously false because the truth was too terrible to integrate into his understanding of reality.

El'Thera tasted it and her temporal perception, which usually operated on a scale of nanoseconds, slowed to a near-standstill as she processed the flavor, stretching a single moment of culinary experience into what felt like an eternity of sensory revelation. When she emerged from the experience, her expression was one of a being who had just glimpsed something so vast, so infinite, so incomprehensibly beyond her previous understanding that the glimpse had permanently altered her relationship with existence itself.

"Beginner's luck," she whispered, and the whisper was not a statement but a prayer, a desperate plea to a universe that she was beginning to suspect existed solely at the pleasure of the being who stood before her, wearing an apron and looking modestly pleased with himself.

Korvanthos tasted it and experienced a temporal anomaly so profound that he briefly existed in all moments of his existence simultaneously, a state of temporal omnipresence that he had not experienced since before his consciousness had been restructured by the Evolved Weakness Paradox, and the anomaly was caused not by any deliberate manipulation of time but by the sheer, overwhelming impossibility of the dish he was consuming, a dish that was so perfect that it existed outside the normal flow of temporal causation, a dish that had never not been perfect, a dish that had always been the most perfect instance of its kind and would always be the most perfect instance of its kind, because the being who had produced it operated on a scale where perfection was not an achievement but a baseline.

"Beginner's luck," Korvanthos said, and his voice carried the resonance of seventeen billion years of temporal existence, and the resonance was tinged with a note of something that might have been despair, or might have been acceptance, or might have been the sound of a being whose entire framework for understanding reality had just been dismantled by a fish.

Garthan, the gardener, who had been watching from the doorway and had not yet tasted the dish, looked at the expressions of his fellow servants and made a decision that he would later describe as the wisest decision of his existence: he decided not to taste the dish. He would never know what he had missed, but he would also never know what it felt like to have his entire understanding of his craft annihilated by the taste of something produced by a being who claimed it was his first time cooking, and the not-knowing was, in its own way, a form of mercy.

Godric, apparently oblivious to the existential crisis he had just inflicted on his household staff, began cleaning up the kitchen with the careful, slightly clumsy movements of a beginner who was not yet familiar with the layout of the workspace. He washed the dishes with a deliberateness that suggested he was trying not to break them, even though the dishes were made of chronal steel and could not be broken by any force short of omniversal annihilation. He dried his hands on a towel with the methodical, slightly awkward technique of a man who was not used to performing domestic tasks, and the performance was so flawless, so perfectly calibrated, so meticulously crafted to reinforce the impression of a charming, slightly hapless beginner that Clive felt the cognitive vice in his skull tighten to a degree that produced an audible popping sound in his inner ear.

"Thank you for the lesson, Mirelle," Godric said, placing a warm, calloused hand on the former Gastronomic Sovereign's shoulder, and the touch was so gentle, so genuinely grateful, so authentically kind that Mirelle nearly collapsed from the overwhelming honor of being touched by the being who had just produced the most perfect dish in the history of existence and attributed it to beginner's luck. "I learned so much today. I hope I can do better next time."

Better.

The word echoed in Clive's mind like a scream in an empty cathedral. Better. The being who had produced a dish so perfect that it made the combined culinary output of the entire omniverse look like garbage wanted to do better. The being whose effectively zero percent effort had produced a result that transcended the concept of perfection was hoping to improve. The being whose cooking ability had been amplifying beyond infinite recursion for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to its magnitude was expressing a desire to learn.

And everyone believed him.

Everyone believed that the King was a beginner who had gotten lucky, that the dish was a fluke, that future attempts would be less impressive, that the being who stood before them in a flour-dusted apron was a charming, slightly hapless man who was learning to cook for the first time and had happened to produce something extraordinary through a combination of good instruction and fortunate circumstance.

Everyone believed this except Clive.

Clive, who had read the journals of his predecessors, who had witnessed the demonstrations of Godric's power, who had watched him create the Stasis Event that birthed himself, who understood, with the crystalline clarity of a mind that had been tempered by thirty years of exposure to the impossible, that the being before him was not a beginner who had gotten lucky but an omnipotent god whose cooking ability was so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond everything else in existence that even his deliberately suppressed, intentionally imperfect, consciously diminished output was sufficient to produce a dish that made the combined culinary efforts of the entire omniverse look like garbage.

And Godric knew that Clive knew.

The knowledge passed between them in a glance, a brief, almost imperceptible moment of eye contact that conveyed more information than any language could express. Godric looked at Clive, and his eyes were warm and friendly and genuinely, authentically kind, and beneath the warmth and the friendliness and the kindness was a knowingness, a quiet, amused awareness that Clive had seen through the performance, that Clive understood the truth, that Clive was standing in a kitchen filled with beings who believed that the most perfect dish in existence was the result of beginner's luck, and that Clive was the only one who understood that the beginner's luck was a lie, and the lie was an act of kindness, and the kindness was the most terrifying thing about it.

Godric winked.

The wink was so quick, so subtle, so perfectly calibrated to be invisible to everyone in the room except Clive that it could only have been produced by a being with absolute omniscience, a being who knew exactly where every eye was directed and exactly what each eye could perceive and exactly how to time a gesture so that it registered on exactly one consciousness and was missed by all others.

The wink was a confession. The wink was an acknowledgment. The wink was the apex predator of existence saying, without words, I know that you know, and I know that you know that I know, and I find this amusing, and I am going to continue pretending to be a beginner, and you are going to continue watching me pretend to be a beginner, and you are going to be the only one who understands that the pretense is a performance, and the performance is an act of kindness, and the kindness is the most terrifying thing about it.

Clive excused himself from the kitchen and walked to his quarters with the measured, deliberate gait of a man who was trying very hard not to run. He closed the door, leaned against it, and slid down until he was sitting on the floor with his back against the wood, and he stared at the opposite wall, and he tried to process what he had just witnessed, and the processing failed, because the thing he had just witnessed was not a thing that could be processed, it was a thing that existed beyond the scope of processing, a thing that revealed, with the crystalline clarity of absolute certainty, that the being he served was not merely omnipotent but omnicompetent, not merely all-powerful but all-capable, not merely the apex predator of existence but the apex everything of existence, and the everything included cooking, and the cooking was so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond everything else in existence that the word beyond was an insult to the magnitude of the gap.

The gardener lesson was the next day.
