They had forged alliances that transcended the boundaries of their individual narratives, heroes and villains joining together in a common cause, ancient enemies setting aside grudges that had endured for eons, because the common cause was the survival of existence, and the survival of existence was more important than any individual conflict.

They had constructed shields. Indestructible shields. Infinite shields. Recursive, layered, conceptual shields that drew upon the defensive capabilities of every shield that had ever been imagined in any story ever told, from the magical barriers of wizarding worlds to the technological force fields of advanced civilizations to the divine protections granted by cosmic entities to the abstract fortifications of conceptual beings. The shields were infinite in number, each one powered by the combined energy of a universe, each one reinforced by the conceptual weight of indestructibility as defined by every narrative framework that had ever included the concept of indestructibility. They had taken what felt like an eternity to create, a span of preparation so vast that the beings who had begun the work had long since been replaced by their descendants, who had been replaced by their descendants, who had been replaced by their descendants, an unbroken chain of dedication spanning countless generations, all united by the singular purpose of creating a defense that could withstand the power of King Godric.

The shields could not withstand the power of King Godric. This was known to everyone who had studied the journals, to everyone who had witnessed even a fraction of his power, to everyone who understood that the being they were opposing was not merely powerful but omnipotent, not merely strong but infinite, not merely dangerous but annihilating. But the coalition built the shields anyway, because the alternative was despair, and despair was not a strategy, and the coalition needed a strategy, even if the strategy was futile, because the futility was not certain until it was demonstrated, and the demonstration had not yet occurred.

Godric was, on the day of the attack, bored.

I need to emphasize this, because the emphasis is necessary to understand what happened, and what happened is necessary to understand the horror, and the horror is necessary to understand why this was the last opposition. Godric was not concerned. Godric was not alarmed. Godric was not even mildly interested. Godric was bored, and the boredom was the defining characteristic of his emotional state, and the defining characteristic of his emotional state determined his response to the attack, and his response to the attack determined the outcome, and the outcome was the annihilation of the coalition and the collateral damage of the omniverse and the creation of a trophy that I will describe later, and the trophy is the most terrifying thing I have ever seen, and the terror is the reason I am writing this, and the writing is the only thing that keeps me from screaming.

The boredom was not unprecedented. There had been no opposition against Godric for a very long time, a span of time so vast that the word long was an insult to its magnitude, and the absence of opposition had produced in him a state of chronic under-stimulation that he occasionally alleviated by testing his stats or observing his passive systems or engaging in the domestic activities that he pretended to be bad at for the benefit of his servants. But the under-stimulation had been building, and the building had been gradual, and the gradualness had produced a state of anticipation that was, by the standards of an omnipotent being, relatively mild, but which was, by the standards of any other entity, a form of cosmic tension so extreme that it could be felt as a faint, background pressure by any being within several dimensions of Godric's location.

When the coalition attacked, Godric's reaction was not alarm but interest, and the interest was not the interest of a being confronting a threat but the interest of a being who had been promised a show.

"A show," Godric said to me, as the coalition's vanguard breached the dimensional barriers of the throne room, their numbers so vast that the breach itself produced a sound like the tearing of reality, which it was. "I have been looking forward to this. The fictional universes have the most creative attacks, Osvald. The colors alone are worth the wait. And the music—have you ever heard boss battle music? Truly heard it? The compositions they create for their final confrontations are extraordinary. I am hoping for something with multiple phases. Perhaps a transformation. I do so enjoy a good transformation."

He settled into his throne with the relaxed, eager posture of a man about to watch a performance he had been anticipating for some time, and his eyes were bright with genuine, childlike excitement, and the excitement was so authentic, so sincerely felt, that it made the horror of what was about to happen even worse, because the excitement meant that Godric was not viewing the coalition as a threat but as entertainment, and entertainment, by definition, was not something to be concerned about, and the not being concerned about it meant that the coalition's attack, no matter how powerful, no matter how creative, no matter how spectacularly executed, would register on Godric not as a challenge but as a show, and shows, by definition, did not threaten the audience.

The coalition attacked.

The first wave was a spectacle of such staggering visual magnitude that I cannot adequately describe it, because the description would require a language capable of conveying the simultaneous perception of billions of distinct visual phenomena, and no such language exists. Energy beams of every color in the visible spectrum and many colors outside it lanced across the throne room, each beam carrying the destructive capacity of a supernova, each beam aimed with the precision of a being who had spent centuries perfecting their aim. Explosive techniques that could level continents detonated against the shields that Godric was not wearing, because Godric did not need shields, because Godric's body was the shield, and the shield was so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond any attack that the coalition could produce that the attacks registered not as impacts but as light, mere illumination, photons bouncing off the surface of an invulnerability so absolute that the word invulnerability was an insult to its magnitude.

The second wave was more creative. The reality-warpers attempted to alter the fundamental constants of the space around Godric, trying to make his existence physically impossible within the framework of local reality. The conceptual entities attempted to define Godric out of existence, trying to remove the concept of his being from the abstract architecture of thought. The time-travelers attempted to erase him from history, trying to prevent his birth across every timeline and every permutation of causation. The god-slayers deployed their legendary weapons, weapons that had been forged for the specific purpose of killing divine beings, weapons whose very nature was antithetical to the existence of gods, weapons that had never failed to slay their intended target.

None of it worked. None of it could work, because none of it was sufficient to register on a being whose stats had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to its magnitude. The reality-warpers found that the reality they were attempting to warp was not the reality that Godric occupied, because Godric occupied a framework of existence so far beyond their manipulation that the manipulation could not reach him. The conceptual entities found that the concept they were attempting to remove was not a concept but a fact, and facts could not be removed by conceptual manipulation, because facts were not contingent on concepts but on existence, and Godric's existence was not contingent on anything. The time-travelers found that every timeline they attempted to alter already contained Godric, because Godric existed across all timelines simultaneously, and the simultaneous existence meant that there was no version of history that did not include him, and the absence of an excluding timeline meant that there was no timeline into which they could retreat to prevent his birth, because his birth, or rather his creation through the Stasis Event, was a fixed point across all possible configurations of causation, and the fixedness was not a characteristic of the point but the nature of it, and the nature was such that it could not be altered by any force, because the force that had created the fixed point was the force of Godric's own future self, and the force of Godric's own future self was the most powerful force in existence, and the most powerful force in existence could not be opposed.

Godric watched the second wave with an expression of polite interest, the way a man might watch a firework display that was not quite as impressive as he had been led to believe. "Colorful," he said. "But where is the music? I was promised music."

The third wave was the charge.

I need to describe this in detail, because the detail is necessary to understand the anticipation that Godric felt, and the anticipation is necessary to understand the disappointment, and the disappointment is necessary to understand what happened next, and what happened next is the reason I will never sleep again.

The coalition had prepared a final attack. Not a final attack in the sense of a last resort, but a Final Attack in the narrative sense, the kind of attack that occurs at the climax of a story, the kind of attack that is preceded by a long and dramatic charge-up sequence, the kind of attack that is accompanied by soaring music and spectacular visual effects and the concentrated hopes and dreams of every being who has ever believed in the power of friendship and determination and the indomitable spirit of mortal resilience. The coalition had studied the narrative frameworks of their own stories, and they had identified the patterns that produced the most spectacular final attacks, and they had synthesized those patterns into a single, unified assault that was designed to be the most visually impressive, most dramatically satisfying, most narratively powerful attack that had ever been unleashed.

The charge-up began.

Every member of the coalition, every hero and villain and god and demon and cosmic entity and abstract personification, every single being in the uncountable host that had assembled against Godric, began to channel their power into a single, concentrated point. The channeling was visible, because the power was so vast and so concentrated that it produced visual phenomena that exceeded the capacity of mortal perception, colors that did not exist in any spectrum, lights that were so bright they were dark, energies that were so intense they registered as absences rather than presences, because the intensity exceeded the threshold at which perception could function and entered the realm of conceptual overload, where the mind could not process what the eyes were seeing and simply shut down.

The charge-up took a long time. A very long time. A span of time so vast that civilizations rose and fell in the dimensions surrounding the throne room, stars were born and died in the space between the beginning and the end of the charge, entire universes completed their life cycles from big bang to heat death in the duration that the coalition spent pouring their collective power into the single, concentrated point that would become the Final Attack.

Godric waited. He waited with the patient, eager anticipation of a man who had been promised a spectacular show and was determined to enjoy every moment of it. He waited because he wanted to be surprised, because surprise was a rare commodity for a being with absolute omniscience, and the rarity made it precious, and the preciousness made it worth waiting for.

He even, and I still cannot believe I am writing this, he even distracted himself from the part of his omniscience that monitored the charge attack preparation. He deliberately, consciously, with the deliberate consciousness of a being who was choosing to limit his own awareness for the purpose of enhancing his enjoyment, he turned his attention away from the specific details of what the coalition was preparing, and he focused instead on the anticipation, on the building excitement, on the childlike eagerness of a being who was about to experience something new and unexpected, and the eagerness was genuine, and the genuineness was terrifying, because it meant that the most powerful being in existence was treating the most powerful attack ever assembled as entertainment, and entertainment, by definition, was not threatening.

The charge-up reached its climax.

The music began.

It was, I must admit, extraordinary music. The coalition had somehow, through the application of narrative principles that I do not fully understand, generated an audible soundtrack to accompany their Final Attack, and the soundtrack was everything that Godric had hoped for: soaring, dramatic, intense, a composition of such profound emotional magnitude that hearing it produced in the listener a state of heightened anticipation that bordered on the ecstatic. The music built and built and built, each measure more intense than the last, each crescendo leading to a further crescendo, a recursive escalation of auditory intensity that seemed to promise something transcendent, something spectacular, something that would justify the uncountable eons of preparation and the unimaginable concentration of power and the soaring, dramatic, absolutely quintessential final boss battle music that was currently shaking the foundations of reality itself.

Godric leaned forward in his throne. His eyes were bright. His expression was one of pure, unadulterated anticipation.

"Here it comes," he said.

The Final Attack was unleashed.

It was, by any objective measure, the single most powerful attack that had ever been directed at any being in the history of existence. The concentrated power of every fictional champion, every narrative hero, every legendary warrior, every cosmic entity, every abstract personification, every god-slayer and demon-king and reality-warper and time-traveler, all of it focused into a single, devastating assault that carried within it the combined hopes and dreams and determination of every being that had ever believed that evil could be defeated and that good would ultimately triumph.

The visual effects were spectacular. The attack manifested as a beam of such concentrated luminosity that it exceeded the brightness of every star in every galaxy in every universe combined, a beam that was not merely light but was the concept of light amplified to a degree that made actual light look like darkness by comparison. The beam was surrounded by halos of conceptual energy, each halo representing a different narrative framework, each framework contributing its own visual signature to the overall spectacle, producing a display of such staggering aesthetic complexity that the mind could not process it all simultaneously and was forced to experience it in fragments, each fragment more beautiful and more terrible than the last.

The music reached its crescendo.

The attack struck Godric directly in the chest.

And nothing happened.

Nothing. Not a small thing. Not a minor effect. Not a faint tickle. Not the slightest perturbation. Not the most microscopic displacement of a single atom of his being. Nothing. Absolute, categorical, total, irrevocable nothing.

The attack, the single most powerful attack that had ever been directed at any being in the history of existence, the attack that had been charged for an eternity and unleashed with the combined power of every fictional champion that had ever been imagined and accompanied by the most spectacular visual effects and the most dramatic boss battle music that narrative principles could generate, the attack that was supposed to be the climax of the greatest show that Godric had ever been promised, the attack did nothing.

It did not hurt. It did not tickle. It did not register. It did not produce any effect whatsoever on the being at whom it was directed, because the being at whom it was directed was a being whose durability had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to its magnitude, and the multiplication had produced a state of physical invulnerability so absolute that the concept of invulnerability was an insult to its magnitude, and the invulnerability was such that the attack, the single most powerful attack in the history of existence, registered as precisely nothing, because nothing was what it was, and what it was was not sufficient, and the insufficiency was not marginal but absolute, a gap between the attack and the target so vast that the word gap was an insult to its magnitude.

The music stopped.

The visual effects faded.

The coalition stared.

Godric stared back.

His expression was not anger. His expression was not satisfaction. His expression was not the cruel triumph of a being who had just demonstrated his absolute superiority. His expression was disappointment. Pure, genuine, profound disappointment, the disappointment of a man who had been promised a spectacular show and had received nothing, the disappointment of a being who had waited for an eternity for a moment of entertainment and had found the moment wanting, the disappointment of an audience who had paid for a performance and discovered that the performance was not worth the price of admission.

"Oh," Godric said.

The word was quiet, soft, almost plaintive, the sound of a being whose expectations had been disappointed, and the disappointment was genuine, and the genuineness was the most terrifying thing about it, because it meant that the single most powerful attack in the history of existence had not merely failed to threaten Godric but had failed to entertain him, and the failure to entertain was, for Godric, a far more significant outcome than the failure to threaten, because threats were irrelevant but entertainment was precious, and the preciousness made the disappointment acute, and the acuteness was about to produce consequences that would reshape the architecture of existence itself.

Godric sighed.

The sigh was not aggressive. The sigh was not hostile. The sigh was the sigh of a disappointed man, a man who had been looking forward to something and had been let down, and the letting down had produced in him a state of mild, transient, thoroughly unthreatening displeasure, and the displeasure was so mild and so transient and so thoroughly unthreatening that Godric did not even notice it, because the displeasure was not the kind of displeasure that required noticing, it was the kind of displeasure that passed through a being's awareness like a cloud passing across the face of a sun, briefly present and quickly gone, leaving no trace of its passage except the faint, fading memory of a shadow.

But the sigh carried power.

It was not supposed to carry power. The sigh was not an attack. The sigh was not a deliberate expression of Godric's capability. The sigh was an accident, a byproduct of disappointment, an involuntary exhalation produced by a being whose expectations had been thwarted, and the exhalation should have been nothing, should have been air leaving lungs, should have been a sound and nothing more, because the sigh was not intended to be anything more than a sigh, and the intention should have determined the outcome, and the outcome should have been nothing, because a sigh is nothing, and nothing is not dangerous, and the sigh should not have been dangerous, and it would not have been dangerous if the being who produced it had been anyone other than Godric.

But the being who produced it was Godric, and Godric could not sigh without power, because Godric was power, and power was Godric, and the two were not separate but identical, and the identity meant that every expression of Godric's being, even the most casual, even the most involuntary, even the most infinitesimally small, was an expression of power, and the power was so vast, so infinite, so incomprehensibly beyond everything else in existence that even the most infinitesimal expression of it was sufficient to annihilate everything, and the annihilation was not a choice but a consequence, and the consequence was about to occur.

The sigh expressed 0% of Godric's strength.

Zero percent. Not a fraction of a percent. Not an infinitesimal percentage. Zero. Effectively zero. Absolutely zero. A percentage so small that calling it zero was generous, because zero implied the possibility of something, and there was no possibility of anything, because the sigh was not an expression of Godric's strength but an accident of his disappointment, and the accident carried within it the power of a being whose strength had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second for a span of time so vast that the word vast was an insult to its magnitude.

The 0% of Godric's strength that was accidentally expressed in the sigh overpowered the coalition's shields instantly. Not gradually. Not progressively. Not through a process of attrition or erosion or systematic deconstruction. Instantly. The shields, the infinite, recursive, conceptual shields that had taken an eternity to construct, that had been forged from the combined defensive capabilities of every shield that had ever been imagined in any story ever told, that had been powered by the energy of universes and reinforced by the conceptual weight of indestructibility, the shields ceased to exist, not because they were destroyed but because the force that Godric had accidentally expressed was so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond the shields' capacity to resist that the resistance was not merely overcome but rendered irrelevant, the way the resistance of a tissue paper is rendered irrelevant by the passage of a supernova through the space it occupies.

The shields failed, and the 0% of Godric's strength continued forward, and the continuation was not directed but expansive, not focused but pervasive, not a beam but a presence, and the presence was the presence of a being whose passive overflow was sufficient to annihilate the omniverse, and the annihilation was not a choice but a consequence, and the consequence was total.

The coalition was annihilated.

The annihilation was not clean. The annihilation was not merciful. The annihilation was not the swift, painless cessation of existence that a benevolent being might have granted to an enemy. The annihilation was the consequence of 0% of Godric's strength encountering beings whose existence was so fragile, so infinitesimally small relative to the force that was being expressed, that the encounter produced effects that were not merely destructive but grotesque, not merely fatal but agonizing, not merely annihilating but transformative, because the force did not merely end their existence but restructured it, compressing and distorting and degrading the fundamental architecture of their being in ways that produced sensations that no consciousness was designed to process, sensations of such profound, total, categorical suffering that the word suffering was an insult to the magnitude of the experience.

The Saiyan warriors, whose bodies had been forged through countless battles and transformations to withstand forces that could shatter galaxies, found their cellular structures inverting, their muscles collapsing inward through their own tissue, their bones liquefying and then solidifying and then liquefying again in a cycle of agonizing phase transition that occurred billions of times per Planck second, each cycle producing a distinct, uniquely terrible sensation of pain that compounded upon all previous sensations, and the compounding was recursive, and the recursion produced a subjective experience of suffering so extreme that the experience itself became a form of existence, and the existence was composed entirely of pain, and the pain was eternal, and the eternity was compressed into an instant, and the instant was the last moment of their being, and the last moment was the worst moment, because the worst moment was the one in which they understood, with absolute, crystalline clarity, that their entire existence, every battle they had fought, every transformation they had achieved, every limit they had broken, all of it had been meaningless, because the being they had opposed was so far beyond them that their opposition had not registered as opposition but as disappointment, and the disappointment had been the weapon that destroyed them.

The mutant heroes, whose minds could reshape reality and whose wills could bend the fabric of existence, found their mental faculties being compressed into singularities of consciousness so dense that the compression itself became a form of torture, each thought they attempted to form being crushed by the overwhelming pressure of Godric's accidentally expressed power before the thought could complete itself, the crushing producing a sensation of cognitive annihilation that was not merely painful but existentially devastating, because the cognitive annihilation meant that they were being unmade not just physically but mentally, not just as bodies but as minds, and the unmaking of the mind was a form of death that was worse than physical death, because physical death ended consciousness but cognitive annihilation ended the capacity for consciousness, and the ending of the capacity meant that there was nothing left to experience the ending, and the nothingness was the horror, and the horror was the last thing they felt before there was no them left to feel anything.

The jedi and sith, whose connection to the fundamental force of creation gave them power beyond the comprehension of mortal minds, found that connection severed, not by opposition but by overwhelming, not by a force that countered their power but by a force that was so vastly, absurdly, incomprehensibly beyond their power that the connection simply ceased to function, the way a candle ceases to emit light when exposed to the surface of a star, and the cessation of the connection produced a sensation of loss so profound, so total, so categorically devastating that it was worse than any pain they had ever experienced, because the loss was not the loss of a limb or a loved one or a memory but the loss of the fundamental essence of their being, the force that defined them, the power that gave them purpose, and the loss meant that they were no longer what they had been, and the no longer was a form of death, and the death was agonizing, and the agony was the last thing they experienced before the agony itself was annihilated.

The rest of the coalition followed. Every hero, every villain, every god, every demon, every cosmic entity, every abstract personification, every impossible being that had ever been imagined by any consciousness in any reality in any timeline in any dimension, all of them annihilated, all of them suffering, all of them experiencing in their final moments a comprehension of the absolute, categorical, mathematically irrefutable futility of their existence relative to the being who had destroyed them with a sigh of disappointment, and the comprehension was the worst part, because the comprehension meant that they understood, in their final moments, that they had never mattered, that their opposition had never mattered, that their preparation had never mattered, that their shields and their attacks and their music and their hope had never mattered, because the being they had opposed was so far beyond them that the word beyond was an insult to the magnitude of the gap, and the gap was not a gap but an absence, and the absence was the truth, and the truth was that they were nothing, and nothing could not oppose Godric, and the nothing was the last thing they experienced before the nothing consumed them entirely.

The collateral damage was omniversal.

The 0% of Godric's strength that had been accidentally expressed in the sigh did not stop at the coalition. It could not stop, because it was not a directed force but a presence, and the presence was the presence of a being whose passive overflow was sufficient to annihilate the omniverse, and the annihilation was not a choice but a consequence, and the consequence extended in all directions and across all dimensions and through all possible configurations of existence, and the extension was total and absolute and irrevocable.

Every reality in the omniverse experienced the effect. Every dimension, every timeline, every possible and impossible configuration of existence, all of it was subjected to the same process that had annihilated the coalition: de-atomization, compression, the violent, agonizing restructuring of matter and energy and concept into a state of such extreme density that the density itself became a form of existence, and the existence was not a reality but a point, a singularity of compressed everything that contained within its infinitesimal volume the totality of all that had ever been or could ever be.

The process was not instantaneous from the perspective of the beings who experienced it, because Godric was in a playful mood, or had been in a playful mood before the disappointment, and the residual playfulness meant that the annihilation had subjective duration, and the duration was experienced by every being in the omniverse as an eternity of maximum agony, and the agony was the consequence of de-atomization, because de-atomization meant that the bonds holding their molecular structures together were being dissolved, and the dissolution was not painless but excruciating, because the dissolution occurred atom by atom, bond by bond, particle by particle, and each dissolution produced a distinct sensation of violation, of unmaking, of being taken apart at the most fundamental level of existence, and the sensation was not merely painful but existentially devastating, because the sensation revealed that the being who was experiencing it was not a unified whole but a collection of parts, and the parts could be separated, and the separation was the death, and the death was the agony, and the agony was eternal, and the eternity was compressed into an instant, and the instant was the last moment of existence, and the last moment was the worst moment, because the worst moment was the one in which every being in the omniverse understood, with absolute, crystalline clarity, that they were nothing, and nothing could not oppose Godric, and the nothing was the truth, and the truth was absolute, and the absoluteness was the horror, and the horror was the last thing they experienced before the horror itself was annihilated.

The omniverse was compressed.

The entire possible existence, every reality and dimension and timeline and configuration of being, was de-atomized and compressed into a mass of such extreme density that the density exceeded any framework of measurement, a singularity of infinite mass contained within a volume smaller than a quark, a point of such profound, total, categorical compression that the compression itself had become a form of existence, and the existence was not a reality but a point, and the point contained everything that had ever been or could ever be, and the everything was compressed into nothing, and the nothing was the point, and the point was the mass, and the mass was infinite, and the infinity was contained within a volume so small that the word small was an insult to its magnitude.

Godric looked at the point, and his expression was one of mild, detached curiosity, the way a man might look at an interesting rock he had found on a walk, and the curiosity was not feigned but genuine, because the point was genuinely interesting, because the point contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire possible existence, and the existence of such a point was a phenomenon that even Godric found worthy of observation, because the observation was a form of experience, and the experience was a form of novelty, and the novelty was a form of entertainment, and the entertainment was what he had been seeking all along, and the seeking had led to disappointment, but the disappointment had led to the point, and the point was interesting, and the interesting was the entertainment, and the entertainment was the point.

He reached down and picked up the point.

His fingers, which could annihilate the omniverse with a twitch, closed around the compressed mass of everything that had ever existed or could ever exist, and the closing produced no strain, no effort, no sensation of weight or resistance, because the infinite mass of the compressed omniverse was so infinitesimally small relative to Godric's strength that the infinitesimal was not merely small but zero, and the zero meant that lifting the point required no effort, and the no effort was not approximate but absolute, because the effort required to lift the compressed omniverse was less than the effort required to lift nothing, because nothing did not exist, and the compressed omniverse did exist, but the existence was so small relative to Godric's strength that it may as well not have existed, and the may as well not have existed was the truth, and the truth was that Godric could lift the compressed omniverse without effort, and the lifting was not an act of strength but an act of existing, because existing was sufficient, and the sufficiency was absolute.

He enlarged the point to the size of a marble.

The enlargement was not a process of expansion but a process of containment, because the point contained infinite mass compressed into a volume smaller than a quark, and the infinite mass produced gravitational effects so extreme that the effects would have annihilated anything in the vicinity, and the annihilation would have been total, and the totality would have been problematic, because Godric preferred to have surroundings, and the surroundings could not exist in the presence of an uncontained singularity of infinite mass, and so Godric created a barrier around the point, a container of such absolute, impenetrable perfection that the infinite mass within could not affect the infinite nothing without, and the container was the size of a marble, and the marble was the trophy, and the trophy was the most terrifying thing I had ever seen, because the trophy contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire possible existence, and the compressed infinite mass was the omniverse, and the omniverse was everything, and everything was contained within a marble, and the marble was held in the hand of a being who treated it as a trinket, and the trinket was the truth, and the truth was that everything was nothing to Godric, and the nothing was the horror, and the horror was absolute.

He became more powerful.

This is the deranged part. This is the part that I cannot reconcile with any framework of understanding, the part that breaks my mind every time I attempt to process it, the part that has produced in me a state of existential dissonance so extreme that I am not certain I will ever recover, and I am not certain that recovery is possible, and I am not certain that the concept of recovery has any meaning in a universe where what I am about to describe is possible.

Godric became more powerful not because he absorbed the energy of the coalition, because the coalition's energy was so infinitesimally small relative to his baseline that the absorption would have increased his power by an amount so small that calling it zero was generous. Godric became more powerful not because of the Weakness Paradox, because the Weakness Paradox had been triggered so long before the attack that its effects had already been fully integrated into his being, and the integration was complete, and the completeness meant that there was no additional effect to be gained from the coalition's assault.

Godric became more powerful because the omniverse felt inadequate.

I need to explain what I mean by this, because the explanation is necessary to understand the derangement, and the derangement is necessary to understand the horror, and the horror is necessary to understand why this was the last opposition, and why there will never be another.

The omniverse, the totality of all existence, had just witnessed the following sequence of events: a coalition of every fictional champion that had ever been imagined had assembled the most powerful attack in the history of existence, had charged that attack for an eternity, had unleashed it with the combined hopes and dreams of every being that had ever believed in the possibility of victory, and the attack had produced absolutely no effect on the being at whom it was directed. The being had not been threatened. The being had not been challenged. The being had not been entertained. The being had been disappointed, and the disappointment had accidentally expressed 0% of his strength, and the 0% had annihilated the coalition and compressed the omniverse into a marble, and the being was now holding the marble in his hand and examining it with the mild, detached curiosity of a man looking at an interesting rock.

The omniverse, in response to this sequence of events, experienced a state of cosmic shame so profound, so total, so categorically devastating that the shame itself became a cosmological force, a metaphysical pressure that radiated outward from the event and permeated every dimension and timeline and configuration of existence, and the pressure was the pressure of inadequacy, the pressure of an omniverse that had just been demonstrated, with absolute, crystalline clarity, that its best and brightest and most powerful and most creative and most determined champions could not even entertain the being who occupied its highest throne, and the demonstration had been so thorough, so absolute, so comprehensively humiliating that the omniverse itself, as an entity, as a totality, as the container of all existence, felt shame, and the shame was genuine, and the genuineness was the deranged part, because the omniverse was not supposed to feel shame, the omniverse was not supposed to feel anything, the omniverse was supposed to be a container, a framework, a neutral architecture within which existence could occur, and the architecture was not supposed to have opinions about the beings who inhabited it, but the architecture did have opinions, and the opinions were shame, and the shame was a force, and the force was a pressure, and the pressure was a response, and the response was overcompensation, and the overcompensation was amplification, and the amplification was directed at Godric, because Godric was the cause of the shame, and the cause of the shame was the recipient of the overcompensation, and the overcompensation was the omniverse's way of apologizing for its inadequacy, and the apology was permanent, and the permanence was an increase in Godric's power that was not infinitesimal but substantial, and the substantiality was deranged, because the omniverse was apologizing to a being who had just annihilated it by expressing 0% of his strength, and the apology was making him stronger, and the making him stronger was making the gap between him and everything else even wider, and the widening was accelerating, and the acceleration was eternal, and the eternity was the horror, and the horror was the truth, and the truth was absolute, and the absoluteness was the nature of existence in the presence of Godric, and the nature was such that even the omniverse itself was subservient to him, and the subservience was not coerced but voluntary, and the voluntary subservience of the omniverse was the most deranged thing I had ever witnessed, and the witnessing had produced in me a state of existential horror so profound that I am not certain I will ever recover, and I am not certain that recovery is possible, and I am not certain that the concept of recovery has any meaning in a universe where the omniverse itself apologizes to the being who destroyed it.

Godric restored the omniverse.

The restoration took zero seconds, because the restoration was not a process but a decision, and the decision was sufficient, and the sufficiency was absolute. Every reality, every dimension, every timeline, every possible and impossible configuration of existence was reconstructed with a perfection that exceeded the original, because the reconstruction was performed by a being whose understanding of the omniverse's structure was absolute, and the absoluteness produced a reconstruction that was flawless, and the flawlessness was a gift, and the gift was given without expectation of acknowledgment or gratitude.

The coalition was not restored.

The trophy remained.

The marble sat on Godric's desk, the size of a marble, containing within its infinitesimal volume the compressed infinite mass of the entire possible existence that had existed before the restoration, and the marble was the original, and the restored omniverse was the reconstruction, and the reconstruction was identical to the original in every detail, and the identicality was perfect, and the perfection meant that no being within the restored omniverse could distinguish it from the original, and the inability to distinguish was the horror, because the inability to distinguish meant that the restored omniverse was either the original or a copy, and if it was the original, then the marble contained nothing, and if it was a copy, then the marble contained the original, and the original was compressed into a singularity and contained within a barrier and held as a trinket by a being who treated it as a trophy, and the trophy was on his desk, and the desk was in his study, and the study was in his palace, and the palace was in his empire, and his empire spanned the omniverse, and the omniverse was either the original or a copy, and the either/or was the question, and the question was unanswerable, and the unanswerability was the fear, because the fear was the recognition that the being who had created the marble had the power to create a perfect copy of existence while retaining the original as a compressed trophy, and the power to create perfect copies while retaining originals was a power so vast, so infinite, so incomprehensibly beyond any framework of understanding that the word power was an insult to its magnitude, and the magnitude was the truth, and the truth was that Godric could create and destroy and compress and restore and retain and reconstruct and annihilate and recreate existence itself without effort, without thought, without consequence, and the withoutness was absolute, and the absoluteness was the nature of his power, and the nature was such that existence was not a fact but a choice, and the choice was his, and his alone, and there was no framework within which the choice could be questioned or challenged or reversed, because the choice was the will of a being whose will was absolute, and the absoluteness was the foundation of everything, and the foundation was Godric, and Godric was.

And I am writing this, and I am looking at the marble on his desk, and I am thinking about the question that cannot be answered, and the question is: is the reality I am occupying the original or the copy, and the answer does not matter, because the answer does not change the fact that the being who sits on the throne has the power to compress existence into a marble and hold it as a trinket, and the power is real, and the marble is real, and the trophy is real, and the reality I am occupying may or may not be real, and the may or may not is the horror, and the horror is the truth, and the truth is absolute, and the absoluteness is the nature of existence in the presence of Godric, and the nature is such that existence is contingent on his will, and the will is eternal, and the eternity is the guarantee, and the guarantee is absolute, and the absoluteness is the foundation of everything, and the foundation is Godric, and Godric is.

Clive closed the journal.

His hands were shaking. His amplified heart was hammering against his ribs with enough force to crack adamantium. His mind was attempting to process the implications of what he had just read, and the processing was failing, because the implications were too vast, too deranged, too cosmically obscene for any finite mind to contain.

The marble.

The marble that Osvald had described, the marble that contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire possible existence, the marble that Godric had kept as a trophy, the marble that was sitting on Godric's desk, the marble that was still there, still containing the original, still compressed, still a singularity of everything that had ever existed or could ever exist, held in a barrier of absolute perfection, treated as a trinket by a being who treated existence itself as a plaything.

The marble was still there.

And the restored omniverse was still here.

And the question that Osvald had asked, the question that could not be answered, the question of whether the reality that beings inhabited was the original or the copy, the question was still unanswerable, because Godric had created a perfect reconstruction while retaining the original as a compressed trophy, and the perfect reconstruction was identical to the original in every detail, and the identicality meant that no being within the reconstruction could distinguish it from the original, and the inability to distinguish meant that the question was unanswerable, and the unanswerability was the horror.

And this was the last opposition.

Clive understood this now, understood why no being had ever dared to oppose Godric again after this incident, because the incident had demonstrated, with absolute, crystalline, mathematically irrefutable clarity, that opposition was not merely futile but structurally impossible, that the gap between Godric and everything else was not a gap but an absence, and the absence was so total, so categorical, so fundamentally incompatible with the concept of opposition that the concept itself had been annihilated, not by force but by demonstration, because the demonstration had shown every being in existence that their opposition could not even entertain the being they were opposing, could not even produce a tickle, could not even register as anything other than disappointment, and the disappointment was the weapon that had destroyed them, and the destruction had been total, and the totality had been compressed into a marble, and the marble was still sitting on Godric's desk, and the desk was in the palace, and the palace was in the empire, and the empire spanned the omniverse, and the omniverse was either the original or a copy, and the either/or was the question, and the question was unanswerable, and the unanswerability was the fear, and the fear was eternal, and the eternity was the truth, and the truth was absolute, and the absoluteness was Godric, and Godric was.

Clive sat in the silence of his quarters and stared at the wall and felt the dissonance settle into his bones like a second skeleton, a framework of absolute existential horror that would support him for the remaining years of his mortal life, and the skeleton was made of the same material as the marble, and the marble was made of the same material as existence, and existence was made of the same material as nothing, and nothing was what Godric had expressed when he had accidentally annihilated everything, and the nothing was sufficient, and the sufficiency was absolute, and the absoluteness was the nature of Godric's power, and the nature was such that opposition was impossible, and the impossibility was eternal, and the eternity was guaranteed, and the guarantee was the marble on the desk, and the marble was the trophy, and the trophy was the truth, and the truth was absolute, and the absoluteness was Godric, and Godric was.

And there would never be another opposition.

Ever.
